28.3      LIMS Basic Functions

28.3.1    Arithmetic Functions
 

Function

Abs    

Purpose:

The Abs function returns the absolute value of a number.

Syntax:

x = Abs(number)

Comments:

number must be a numeric expression.

Example:

x = Abs(-1)

y = Abs(1)

Returns:

x = 1

y = 1

 

ArcCos     

Purpose:

Given the cosine of an angle, the ArcCos function returns the angle in radians.

Syntax:

radians = ArcCos (number)

Comments:

The absolute value of number must be <= 1. 

Example:

radians = ArcCos(0.5)    

Returns:

radians = 1.04719755

 

ArcSin     

Purpose:

Given the sine of an angle, the ArcSin function returns the angle in radians.

Syntax:

radians = ArcSin (number)

Comments:

The absolute value of number must be <= 1. 

Example:

radians = ArcSin(0.5)    

Returns:

radians = 0.52359878

 

ArcTan    

Purpose:

Given the tangent of an angle, the ArcTan function returns the angle in radians.

Syntax:

radians = ArcTan (number)

Comments:

number is the tangent of the angle whose value is to be returned.

Example:

radians = ArcTan(0.5)    

Returns:

radians = 0.46364761

 

Ave    

Purpose:

The Ave function returns the average value of an array of values.

Syntax:

x = Ave(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

array[1]=1

array[2]=9

array[3]=14

x = Ave(array)

Returns:

x = 8

 

BitAnd    

Purpose:

The BitAnd function performs a bitwise AND on 2 integers.

Syntax:

integer = BitAnd(int1, int2)

Comments:

 

Example:

integer = BitAnd(3, 2)   

Returns:

integer = 2

 

BitAt    

Purpose:

The BitAt function returns the bit value at a bit position in an integer. Bit position is indexed from right to left.

Syntax:

bitValue = BitAt(integer, bitPosition)

Comments:

 

Example:

bitValue = BitAt(8, 4)   ‘ 8 = 1000

Returns:

bitValue = 1

 

BitOr    

Purpose:

The BitOr function performs a bitwise OR on 2 integers.

Syntax:

integer = BitOr(int1, int2)

Comments:

 

Example:

integer = BitOr(3, 2)   

Returns:

integer = 3

 

BitShift    

Purpose:

The BitShift functions shifts an integer by a bit number of positions.  shiftNumber can be positive or negative.  A positive value shifts the bits to the left.  A negative value shifts the bits to the right.

Syntax:

newInteger = BitShift(integer, shiftNumber)

Comments:

 

Example:

newInteger = BitShift(2, 2)  

Returns:

newInteger = 8

 

BitXor    

Purpose:

The BitXor function performs a bitwise exclusive OR on 2 integers.

Syntax:

integer = BitXor(int1, int2)

Comments:

 

Example:

integer = BitXor(3, 2)  

Returns:

integer = 1

 

CompareNumbers  

Purpose:

This function evaluates two given numbers with respect to a given comparison operator and returns whether the evaluation is True or False.

Syntax:

evaluation = CompareNumbers(number1, comparisonOperator, number2)

number1 – the first given number to be evaluated.

comparisonOperator – the given comparison operator with which to evaluate the numbers. Valid comparison operators are:

< 

<=

=

> 

>=

<> 

LT

LTE

EQUAL

GT

GTE

NOTEQUAL

number2 – the second given number with respect to which the first given number is to be evaluated using the comparison operator.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies if both input numbers are actually numeric; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with:  "number1 and number2 must be valid numbers".

•LIMS verifies if both the input numbers are open-ended; if yes, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "number1 and number2 cannot both be open ended numbers".

•LIMS verifies if the given comparison operator is a valid one; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Invalid operator…." along with the operator string.

•LIMS then evaluates if the entered condition comparing the first number against the second number is correct or not; if yes, then LIMS returns True, else False.

Example:

The following example evaluates the first input number 5.2 with respect to the second given number 4.7 using the comparison operator ">=".

evaluation = CompareNumbers(5.2, ">=", 4.7) returns:

True.

Returns:

If the comparison is correct, this function returns evaluation = true, otherwise false.

 

ConvertUnits    

Purpose:

The ConvertUnits function converts a value from one set of units to another using the base value and factor defined in the UNITS table.

Syntax:

newValue = ConvertUnits(oldValue, oldUnits, newUnits)

oldValue – input number associated with old units.

oldUnits – string representing the old units.

newUnits –string representing the new units.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the old value cannot be interpreted as a number, LIMS will trigger the error "Input value is not a number".

•LIMS retrieves the definition of the old units from the database.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified old units do not exist".

•LIMS retrieves the definition of the new units from the database.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified new units do not exist". There will also be an entry added to the error.log with the current date time stamp and a message like the following: Missing unit record: xx  (where xx is the name of the invalid units)

•LIMS converts the old value from the old units to the new units and returns the result.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example converts the value 4.5 from grams to milligrams.  If these units are configured correctly, the new value will be 4500.

newValue = ConvertUnits(4.5, "G", "MG")

Returns:

The function returns the converted value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

Correlation    

Purpose:

The Correlation function returns the correlation coefficient for a straight line based on known data points.  The function uses least squares to generate the best fit.

Syntax:

corr = Correlation(xArray, yArray)

xArray – one dimensional array of x-coordinate numeric values.

yArray – one dimensional array of y-coordinate numeric values.

Comments:

xArray and yArray are one-dimensional arrays. 

Example:

xArray[1] = 0

xArray[2] = 1

yArray[1] = 2

yArray[2] = 4

corr = Correlation(xArray, yArray)  

Returns:

corr = 1.0

 

Cos    

Purpose:

The Cos function returns the cosine of a given angle in radians.

Syntax:

x = Cos(number)

number – a numeric expression in radians.

Comments:

 

Example:

x = Cos(0.5)  

Returns:

x = 0.87758256

 

Exp    

Purpose:

The Exp function returns e raised to a power.

Syntax:

x = Exp(number)

number – power e is to be raise to.

Comments:

The constant e is approximately 2.718282.  The Exp function is also referred to as the antilogarithm.

Example:

x = Exp(4)  

Returns:

x = 54.59815

 

FormatExponential    

Purpose:

The FormatExponential function returns a number formatted in exponential notation.

Syntax:

expFormat = FormatExponential(number, places)

number – can be either numeric or a string representation of a number.

place – number of decimal places displayed

Comments:

 

 

expFormat is a string.

Example:

expFormat1 = FormatExponential(27.348,  2) 

expFormat2 = FormatExponential("27.348", 1) 

Returns:

expFormat1 = 2.73E1

expFormat2 = 2.7E1

 

GeomMean    

Purpose:

The GeomMean function returns the geometric mean of an array.

Syntax:

x = GeomMean(array)

array - a one-dimensional array of numbers.

Comments:

 

Example:

a[1] = 10

a[2] = 1000

x = GeomMean(a) 

Returns:

x = 100

 

HighWord    

Purpose:

The HighWord function returns the high word of an integer.

Syntax:

integer = HighWord(integer)

Comments:

 

Example:

x = HighWord(2556300)  

Returns:

x = 39

Log    

Purpose:

The Log function returns the natural (base e) logarithm of a number.

Syntax:

x = Log(number)

number must be a numeric expression greater than zero.

Comments:

 

Example:

x = Log(1)    

Returns:

x = 0

 

LogBase    

Purpose:

The LogBase function returns the log of a number for a specified base.

Syntax:

x = LogBase(base, number)

Example:

x = LogBase(2, 16) 

Returns:

x = 4

 

LowWord    

Purpose:

The LowWord function returns the low word of an integer.

Syntax:

integer = LowWord(integer)

Example:

x = LowWord(2556300)   

Returns:

x = 396

 

Max    

Purpose:

The Max function returns the larger of 2 numbers.

Syntax:

x = Max(number1, number2)

Example:

x = Max(4, 9)  

Returns:

x = 9

 

Median    

Purpose:

The Median function returns the median value of an array of values.

Syntax:

x = Median(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.  If the array contains an even number of entries, the returned value will be the average of the middle two values.

Example:

array[1] = 0

array[2] = 27

array[3] = 5

x = Median(array) 

Returns:

x = 5

 

Min    

Purpose:

The Min function returns the smaller of 2 numbers.

Syntax:

x = Min(number1, number2)

Example:

x = Min(4, 9)   

Returns:

x = 4

 

Percentile  

Purpose:

This function takes an array of numbers and a percentage value as input and returns the percentile value. In other words, it checks the array of input numbers and returns the value below which the given input percentage of numbers falls.

Syntax:

x = Percentile(array, percent)

array – an array of numbers.

percent – the percentage value for which the percentile value is to be calculated for the above input array of numbers between 0 and 100.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies if the array argument is an array.  If this fails, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Invalid input array".

•LIMS verifies if the entered percent value is a valid number between 0 and 100; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Percent must be a valid number between 0 and 100".

•LIMS checks the size of the array of numbers provided as input; if it is found to be empty, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "No values specified".

•LIMS then proceeds to arrange the numbers in the input array in a sorted order and calculates the percentile value.

Example:

The following example takes an array of 5 numbers as the input array and calculates the percentile value for the input percentage value of 25%.

numArray[1] = 10

numArray[2] = 20

numArray[3] = 30

numArray[4] = 40

numArray[5] = 50

 

x = Percentile(numArray, 25) returns:

x = 20.

Returns:

The calculated percentile value for the given array of numbers based on the given percentage value.  If invalid

 

 

Range    

Purpose:

The Range function returns the range of values in an array.

Syntax:

x = Range(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

a[1]=3

a[2]=8

a[3]=15

x = Range(a) 

Returns:

x = 12

 

Rnd    

Purpose:

The Rnd function returns a random number.  Refer to KnowlegeTrack KT01288 for more detail on this function.

Syntax:

x = Rnd(number)

Comments:

number is the seed for the random number.

Example:

x = Rnd(5)   

Returns:

x = 0.1318512

 

Round    

Purpose:

The Round function rounds a number to the nearest whole integer.

Syntax:

x = Round(number)

Comments:

.5 is rounded up to its nearest integer value.

Example:

x = Round(1.5)  

Returns:

x = 2

 

RoundCustom

Purpose:

The RoundCustom function is used to apply a composite rounding rule to a number. The type of rounding applied to the number depends on the magnitude of the number and the specified rounding.

Syntax:

string = RoundCustom(number, places1, places2, roundingRule)

number – the value to be rounded.

places1 – the number of decimal places to be used in the rounding.

places2 – the number of significant figures to be used in the rounding.

roundingRule –  the composite rounding rule to be applied (must be one of the composite rounding rules that combines Sig Figs and Decimal Places). Valid values are “1”and “2”.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  The system evaluates places1. If the value specified is not a valid integer, the lastError variable is set to “Places1 is not a valid integer:  places1”.

2.  The system evaluates places2. If the value specified is not a valid integer, the lastError is set to “Places2 is not a valid integer:  places2”.

3.  The system evaluates number. If the value specified is not a valid number, the lastError is set to “Number argument is not a valid number:  number”.

4.  The system evaluates the roundingRule. If an invalid rule is specified, the lastError is set to “Invalid rounding rule, must be 1 or 2: roundingRule”.

5.  The system applies the rounding to the number.

Example:

In this example the number 0.12345 is rounded using the “Sig. Figs. & Decimal Places w/0.5 Up” rounding rule, with the Decimal Places specified as 2 and the Significant Figures specified as 3.

number = 0.12345

places1 = 2

places2 = 3

roundingRule = "1"

string = RoundCustom(number, places1, places2, roundingRule)

Returns:

The string variable is set to the rounded value if the function completes successfully, else variable is set to ERROR.

 

RoundOddUp    

Purpose:

The RoundOddUp function rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places using the decimal odd up rule.

Syntax:

string = RoundOddUp(number, numFigs)

Comments:

0.5 rounding depends on the preceding digit. Refer to the rounding rule definitions in the analysis table for further detail.

Example:

string = RoundOddUp(16.445, 2)    

Returns:

string = 16.44

 

RoundSigFigs    

Purpose:

The RoundSigFigs function rounds a number to a specified number of significant figures using the 0.5 up rule.

Syntax:

string = RoundSigFigs(number, numFigs)

Comments:

.5 is rounded up to its nearest integer value. Refer to the rounding rule definitions in the analysis table for further detail.

Example:

string = RoundSigFigs(16.445, 4)  

Returns:

string = 16.45

 

RoundSigFigsOddUp    

Purpose:

The RoundSigFigsOddUp function rounds a number to a specified number

 

of significant figures using the odd up rule.

Syntax:

string = RoundSigFigsOddUp(number, numFigs)

Comments:

0.5 rounding depends on the preceding digit. Refer to the rounding rule definitions in the analysis table for further detail.

Example:

string = RoundSigFigsOddUp(16.445, 4)  

Returns:

string = 16.44

 

RoundTo    

Purpose:

The RoundTo function rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places using the decimal 0.5 up rule.

Syntax:

string = RoundTo(number, places)

Comments:

.5 is rounded up to its nearest integer value.

Example:

string = RoundTo(1.54321, 3)  

Returns:

string = 1.543

 

RoundToNoExp    

Purpose:

The RoundToNoExp function rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places using the decimal 0.5 up rule.  Unlike the RoundTo function, the RoundToNoExp function converts the specified number to plain decimal format (without scientific notation) before rounding.

Syntax:

string = RoundToNoExp(Number, Places)

Number – the number to be rounded.  The argument may be formatted as a string that begins with either "<" or ">", in which case the rounding is applied to the numeric value that follows this initial character.

Places – the number of decimal places to which the number should be rounded.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified number value is blank, LIMS will trigger the error "Number argument is not a valid number" and return an empty string.

•If the specified number value cannot be interpreted as a number, or if the specified places value cannot be interpreted as an integer, LIMS will trigger the error "Number argument is not a valid number" and return the number value as specified.

•LIMS converts the specified number to a format that includes no exponential component, rounds it, and returns the rounded value.

 NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example illustrates the difference between the RoundTo function and the RoundToNoExp function.

X = "2.4567e1"
y = RoundTo(x,2)
z = RoundToNoExp(x,2)

The value of y will be "2.46e1" and the value of z will be "24.57".

Returns:

The function returns the rounded number as a string.

 

Sin    

Purpose:

The Sin function returns the sine of an angle.

Syntax:

x = Sin(number)

Comments:

number is a numeric expression in radians.

Example:

x = Sin(0.5)    

Returns:

x = 0.47942554

 

Slope    

Purpose:

The Slope function returns the best fit slope for a straight line based on known data points.  Least squares is used to generate the best fit.

Syntax:

m = Slope(xArray, yArray)

Comments:

xarray and yarray are one-dimensional arrays.

Example:

xArray[1] = 0

xArray[2] = 1

yArray[1] = 2

yArray[2] = 4

m = Slope(xArray, yArray)   

Returns:

m = 2

 

Sqr    

Purpose:

The Sqr function returns the square root of a number.

Syntax:

x = Sqr(number)

Comments:

number can be any numeric greater than or equal to 0.

Example:

x = Sqr(4)    

Returns:

x = 2

 

Std    

Purpose:

The Std function returns the standard deviation of an array of values.

Syntax:

x = Std(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

array[1]=1

array[2]=4

array[3]=8

array[4]=12

x = Std(array)    

Returns:

x = 4.787

 

Sum    

Purpose:

The Sum function returns the sum of the values in the array.

Syntax:

x = Sum(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

array[1]=1

array[2]=4

array[3]=8

array[4]=12

x = Sum(array)    

Returns:

x = 25.0

 

Tan    

Purpose:

The Tan function returns the tangent of an angle.

Syntax:

x = Tan(number)

Comments:

number is a numeric expression in radians.

Example:

x = Tan(0.5)   

Returns:

x = 0.54630249

 

Trunc    

Purpose:

The Trunc function truncates the fractional portion of a number and returns the integer.

Syntax:

x = Trunc(number)

Example:

x = Trunc(1.627)  

Returns:

x = 1

 

Val    

Purpose:

The Val function returns the numeric value of a string of characters.

Syntax:

x = Val(string)

Comments:

string must be a sequence of characters that can be interpreted as a numeric value.

No spaces are allowed.

Example:

x = Val("1234")   

Returns:

x = 1234

 

Yintercept    

Purpose:

The Y-intercept function returns the Y intercept based on known data points.

Syntax:

yInter = Yintercept(xArray, yArray)

Comments:

xarray and yarray are one-dimensional arrays.

Example:

x[1] = 0

x[2] = 1

y[1] = 1

y[2] = 4

b= Yintercept(x, y)

Returns:

b = 1

 

 

28.3.2    Array Functions
 

Function
