ArrayConvertToSingleDim  

Purpose:

This function copies a specified column from a two dimensional array and creates a one dimensional array.

Syntax:

Status = ArrayConvertToSingleDim(inputArray, "outputArrayName", columnNumber)

inputarray – name of the two dimensional array input.

outputArrayName – name of the one dimensional array output.

columnNumber – column within the two dimensional array to copy to the output array.

 

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that the column number is valid.  If not, LIMS populates the LastError with:  "Invalid column number…"

•LIMS verifies that the input array is a one or two dimensional array.  If not, LIMS populates the lastError with:  'Invalid column number... only supports one or two dimensions'

•LIMS creates the new array and posts the values of the selected column to the array.

Example:

inputArray[1,1] = "A1"

inputArray[2,1] = "A2"

inputArray[3,1] = "A3"

inputArray[1,2] = "B1"

inputArray[2,2] = "B2"

inputArray[3,3] = "B3"

columnNumber = 2

status = ArrayConvertToSingleDim(inputArray, "outputArrayName", columnNumber)

returns:
outputArrayName[1] = "B1"

outputArrayName[2] = "B2"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayDistinctValues    

Purpose:

The ArrayDistinctValues function returns the number of distinct values in an array and the array of distinct values.

Syntax:

numDistinct = ArrayDistinctValues(inputArray, "outputArrayName")

inputArray is the name of the array that will be searched

outputArrayName is a string containing the name of the array containing the distinct values

Comments:

This function evaluate dsata types when determining distinct values.  As a result, the string "7" is considered distinct from the number 7.

Example:

xArray[1] = 1

xArray[2] = 2

xArray[3] = 1

xArray[4] = "2"

outputArrayName = "yArray"

numDistinct = ArrayDistinctValues(xArray, outputArrayName)

Returns:

 

numDistinct = 3

yArray[1] = 1 

yArray[2] = 2

yArray[3] = "2"

 

ArrayExtent

Purpose:

The ArrayExtent function returns the maximum row and column index values in a specified array.

Syntax:

status = ArrayExtent(array, "outputInfo")      

array – name of the array to check.  This can be a one dimensional or two dimensional array

outputInfo – name of the output array.  This is a one dimensional array with 2 entries.  Position 1 contains the number of rows and position 2 contains the number of columns

NOTE:  This function includes empty positions.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the array parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “ArrayExtent, unexpected data type”.

•The system checks the size of the array parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “ArrayExtent, missing argument”.

•The system checks the outputInfo parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: “ArrayExtent, missing output argument”.

•The system evaluates the array elements and returns the output array.

Example:

 

 

In the following example the outputInfo array would contain (2, 4):

anArray[1,1] = "1A"

anArray[1,2] = "1B"

anArray[1,4] = "1D"

anArray[2,1] = "2A"

anArray[2,3] = "2C"

anArray[2,4] = "2D"

 

status = ArrayExtent(anArray, "outputInfo")

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

 

ArrayFilter    

Purpose:

The ArrayFilter function filters an array according to a set of criteria and returns an array containing the filtered values.

Syntax:

status = ArrayFilter(inputArray, minValue, maxValue, match, "outputArrayName")

Comments:

Filters can be a minimum and/or maximum or an exact match.

Specify minValue and maxValue to filter number >=minValue and <= maxValue .

Specify exact match using match (minValue and maxValue  are not used in this case).

The system does not support the simultaneous use of both the match paramenter and the min/max parameters.  The simultaneous use of the min and max parameters is supported.

Example:

 

xArray[1] = 10

xArray[2] = 14

xArray[3] = 16

minValue = 10

maxValue = 15

outputArrayName = "yArray"

status = ArrayFilter(xArray, minValue, maxValue, , outputArrayName)

Returns:

 

yArray[1] = 10  

yArray[2] = 14

 

ArrayFilterIndex    

Purpose:

This function filters an array according to a set of criteria and returns an array containing the indexes of the filtered values in the specified array output format, i.e., "1D" or "2D".

Syntax:

status = ArrayFilterIndex(inputArray, minValue, maxValue, match, "outputArrayName", outputFormat)

inputArray – name of the array input, limited to maximum of two dimensions.

minValue – the minimum value of the filter, i.e., the lower limit of the filter. The function will search for values in the input array that are >= the specified min value.

maxValue – the maximum value of the filter, i.e., the upper limit of the filter. The function will search for values in the input array that are <= the specified max value.

match – the value to be used in the filter as an exact match. The function will search for the exact value specified as match in the input array.

outputArrayName – name of the array output.

outputFormat – the format of the output array, i.e. one-dimensional ("1D") or two-dimensional ("2D").

Comments:

Specified filters can be one of the following types:

A minimum and/or a maximum value (An exact match is not used in this case)

An exact match (minimum and maximum values are not used in this case)

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies if a minimum value, a maximum value and a matching value have all been specified. If yes, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with:  "Cannot specify both min / max and match values".

•LIMS checks if a minimum value of the filter has been specified; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the minimum value to search for in the input array.

•Subsequently, LIMS checks if a maximum value of the filter has been specified; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the maximum value to search for in the input array.

•If minimum and maximum values for the filter have not been specified, then LIMS checks if an exact match value has been specified as a filter; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the exact value to search for in the input array.

•LIMS then checks the specified output format, i.e., "1D" (for one-dimensional) or "2D" (for two-dimensional).  If not specified It defaults to 1D.

•Finally, LIMS searches the input array for values specified either within the minimum and maximum ranges OR as an exact match. If any matching values are found, LIMS populates the output array in the specified format (i.e., "1D" or "2D") with the index keys of the matching values of the input array.

Example:

 

 

The following example filters the given input array "inputArray" for values between 10 and 25 and returns the output array "outputArray" with the matching index values.

inputArray[1, 1] = 5

inputArray[1, 2] = 10

inputArray[2, 1] = 15

inputArray[2, 2] = 20

inputArray[3, 1] = 25

inputArray[3, 2] = 30

 

minValue = 10

maxValue = 25

 

status = ArrayFilterIndex(inputArray, minValue, maxValue, , "outputArray", "1D")

Returns:

outputArray[1] = 1,2

outputArray[2] = 2,1

outputArray[3] = 2,2

outputArray[4] = 3,1

Returns:

 

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayFromCSVFile   

Purpose:

This function reads the contents of a given CSV (Comma Separated Values) file and returns an array containing the data from the CSV file using the specified delimiters.

Syntax:

status = ArrayFromCSVFile(fileName, columnDelimiter, rowDelimiter, trimWhiteSpaces,

                                              "outputArrayName")

fileName – the name of the input csv file from which data is to be read.

columnDelimiter – the string to be used as a separator to identify data in each column in the input csv file.

rowDelimiter – a single character string to be used to identify the end of each row of data in the input csv file.

trimWhiteSpaces – a Boolean value; if set to True, then spaces before and after each element of data read from the csv file would be trimmed before inserting the value into the output array.

outputArrayName – name of the array output.  If the input file contains a single row then the output array will be a 1 dim array.  If there is more than 1 row then the output array will be a 2D array.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies if the specified file path exists. If not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with:  "File directory does not exist " along with the specified path and filename.

•LIMS verifies if the file name has been specified. If not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Invalid or no filename specified " along with the filename.

•LIMS reads the contents of the file; if the contents are found to be empty, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Empty file contents".

•LIMS verifies if the trimWhiteSpaces has been specified, if yes, then it sets the Boolean value to True so that spaces maybe trimmed from each element of data before populating the output array.

•LIMS verifies if a columnDelimiter value has been specified; if not, then the comma (",") is taken to be the standard separator to mark the end of a value in a column.

•LIMS verifies if a rowDelimiter value has been specified; if not, then the standard carriage return is taken to be the end of each row of data in the input csv file.

•LIMS then populates the output array with each element of data read from the input csv file.

Example:

A .csv file "myProjectCodes.csv" contains a few rows of data in two columns, listing project codes and their corresponding descriptions.

status = ArrayFromCSVFile("C:\myProjectCodes.csv", , , T, "projectArray")

Returns:

projectArray[i, 1] = <Project Code in the input csv file>

projectArray[i, 2] = <Description in the input csv file>

      for each row of data in the csv file (i = number of rows in the input csv file).

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayFromCSVString   

Purpose:

This function reads the contents of a given CSV (Comma Separated Values) string and returns an array containing the data from the CSV string using the specified delimiters.

Syntax:

status = ArrayFromCSVString(inputString, columnDelimiter, rowDelimiter,

                                                  trimWhiteSpaces, "outputArrayName")

inputString – the name of the input csv string from which data is to be read.

columnDelimiter – a single character string to be used as a separator to identify data in each column in the input csv string.

rowDelimiter – a single character string to be used to identify the end of each row of data in the input csv string.

trimWhiteSpaces – a Boolean value; if set to True, then spaces before and after each element of data read from the csv string would be trimmed before inserting the value into the output array.

outputArrayName – name of the array output.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks the size of the input csv string; if the string is found to be empty, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Empty csv string".

•LIMS verifies if the trimWhiteSpaces has been specified; if yes, then it sets the Boolean value to True so that spaces maybe trimmed from each element of data before populating the output array.

•LIMS verifies if a columnDelimiter value has been specified; if not, then the comma (",") is taken to be the standard separator to mark the end of a value in a column.

•LIMS verifies if a rowDelimiter value has been specified; if not, then the standard carriage return is taken to be the end of each row of data in the input csv string.

•LIMS then populates the output array with each element of data read from the input csv string.

Example:

A .csv string "inString" contains data separated by a comma and a "*" indicating the end of each line.

inString = "AN001, Analysis 001*AN002, Analysis 002*AN003, Analysis 003"

 

status = ArrayFromCSVString(inString, , "*", T, "outArray")

 

Returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "AN001"

outArray[1, 2] = "Analysis 001"

outArray[2, 1] = "AN002"

outArray[2, 2] = "Analysis 002"

outArray[3, 1] = "AN003"

outArray[3, 2] = "Analysis 003"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayIndexSubset   

Purpose:

This function reads the contents of an array and returns another array (a subset of the input array) containing the values at the specified indexes of the input array.

Syntax:

status = ArrayIndexSubset(inputArray1, rowsArray, colsArray, "outputArrayName")

inputArray1 – the name of the input array from which values are to be taken.

rowsArray – the name of a one dmensionalarray containing the row-numbers (integer values) of the input array from which values are to be taken.

colsArray – the name of a one dmensionalarray containing the column-numbers (integer values) of the input array from which values are to be taken.

outputArrayName – name of the array output.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks the size of the specified rows array, i.e., checks the number of integer values specified for rows. If none are specified, then LIMS would read values from all rows of the input array.

•LIMS checks the size of the specified columns array, i.e., checks the number of integer values specified for columns. If none are specified, then LIMS would read values from all columns for each specified row in the input array.

•LIMS reads the input array for each specified row and column, picks the corresponding values and populates the output array name with the same.

Example:

inputArray[1, 1] = "Row 1 Col 1"

inputArray[1, 2] = "Row 1 Col 2"

inputArray[2, 1] = "Row 2 Col 1"

inputArray[2, 2] = "Row 2 Col 2"

inputArray[3, 1] = "Row 3 Col 1"

inputArray[3, 2] = "Row 3 Col 2"

 

rowsArray[1] = 1

rowsArray[2] = 3

colsArray[1] = 1

colsArray[2] = 2

 

status = ArrayIndexSubset(inputArray, rowsArray, colsArray, "outArray")

[This reads the input array for values in the specified rows (1 & 3) and columns (1 & 2)] Returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "Row 1 Col 1" (the value of row1 & col1 of the input array)

outArray[1, 2] = "Row 1 Col 2" (the value of row1 & col2 of the input array)

outArray[2, 1] = "Row 3 Col 1" (the value of row3 & col1 of the input array)

outArray[2, 2] = "Row 3 Col 2" (the value of row3 & col2 of the input array)

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayInitialize   

Purpose:

This function returns a new array with the specified name and the specified number of rows and columns, with each element of the array initialized with the specified initial value.

Syntax:

status = ArrayInitialize("outputArray", numRows, numColumns, intialValue)

outputArray – the name of the new output array.

numRows – the number of rows to be created in the output array.

numColumns – the number of columns to be created in the output array.

initialValue – the value with which each element of the output array is to be initialized.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks for the specified initial value; if not specified, then LIMS would initialize each element of the new array with an empty string (‘’).

•LIMS creates elements within the new array for the specified number of rows and columns and initializes each element with the specified initial value.

Example:

The example below creates a new array with the name "outArray" of 3 rows and 2 columns (6 elements in all) and initializes each element with the string "InitVal".

status = ArrayInitialize("outArray", 3, 2, "InitVal")

 

Returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "InitVal"

outArray[1, 2] = "InitVal"

outArray[2, 1] = "InitVal"

outArray[2, 2] = "InitVal"

outArray[3, 1] = "InitVal"

outArray[3, 2] = "InitVal"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayJoin   

Purpose:

This function returns a new single array by joining the values of two input arrays either by rows or by columns.

Syntax:

status = ArrayJoin(inputArray1, inputArray2, "outputArrayName", joinBy)

inputArray1 – the name of the first input array.

inputArray2 – the name of the second input array.

outputArrayName – name of the array output containing the values of the above two input arrays.

joinBy – either "Row" or "Column", indicating whether the values from the input arrays are to be joined by rows or columns.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks if the first input is an actual array; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Parameter 1 is not an array.".

•LIMS checks if the second input is an actual array; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Parameter 2 is not an array.".

•LIMS checks the join By clause, i.e., for "Row" or "Column". If no join By clause is specified, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Invalid join by…".

•LIMS checks if the first input array is empty; if yes, then LIMS returns the output array as a copy of the second input array.

•LIMS checks if the second input array is empty; if yes, then LIMS returns the output array as a copy of the first input array.

•LIMS checks whether the first input array is a two-dimensional array. If true, then LIMS calculates the number of rows and columns in the first input array; if false, then LIMS calculates the number of rows in the first input array and takes the number of columns as 1.

•LIMS checks whether the second input array is a two-dimensional array. If true, then LIMS calculates the number of rows and columns in the second input array; if false, then LIMS calculates the number of rows in the second input array and takes the number of columns as 1.

•If the join By clause has been specified as "Row", then LIMS first checks whether both the input arrays have the same number of columns. If not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Error… input arrays must have the same number of columns". If the condition is satisfied, then LIMS reads the values of the first input array row-wise and populates the output array; then LIMS reads the values of the second input array row-wise and adds to the output array.

•If the join By clause has been specified as "Column", then LIMS first checks whether both the input arrays have the same number of rows. If not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Error… input arrays must have the same number of rows". If the condition is satisfied, then LIMS does the following:

•LIMS reads the values from the first row of the first input array and populates a new row of the output array.

•It then reads the values from the first row of the second input array and continues to populate the same row of the output array by adding columns.

•LIMS then goes to the next row and continues the above process till the maximum number of rows in each of the input arrays have been read and the output array populated.

Example:

 i) The following example reads the contents of the 2 given input arrays "inArray1" (2 rows & 2 columns) and "inArray2" (2 rows & 2 columns), joins them row-wise and generates a new output array "outArray" of 4 rows and 2 columns. In other words, this reads the contents of the first input array row-wise and populates the output array; then it reads the contents of the second input array row-wise and adds to the output array as follows.

inArray1[1, 1] = "A-Row1Col1"

inArray1[1, 2] = "A-Row1Col2"

inArray1[2, 1] = "A-Row2Col1"

inArray1[2, 2] = "A-Row2Col2"

 

inArray2[1, 1] = "B-Row1Col1"

inArray2[1, 2] = "B-Row1Col2"

inArray2[2, 1] = "B-Row2Col1"

inArray2[2, 2] = "B-Row2Col2"

 

status = ArrayJoin(inArray1, inArray2, "outArray", "Row")

Returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "A-Row1Col1"

outArray[1, 2] = "A-Row1Col2"

outArray[2, 1] = "A-Row2Col1"

outArray[2, 2] = "A-Row2Col2"

outArray[3, 1] = "B-Row1Col1"

outArray[3, 2] = "B-Row1Col2"

outArray[4, 1] = "B-Row2Col1"

outArray[4, 2] = "B-Row2Col2"

 

ii) In the following example, the same two input arrays mentioned above are joined column-wise. LIMS reads the values from the first row of the first input array and populates a new row of the output array; then reads the values from the first row of the second input array and continues to populate the same row of the output array by adding columns. This is done for every row of the input arrays and finally generates the new output array "outArray" of 2 rows and 4 columns as follows:

status = ArrayJoin(inArray1, inArray2, "outArray", "Column") returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "A-Row1Col1"

outArray[1, 2] = "A-Row1Col2"

outArray[1, 3] = "B-Row1Col1"

outArray[1, 4] = "B-Row1Col2"

outArray[2, 1] = "A-Row2Col1"

outArray[2, 2] = "A-Row2Col2"

outArray[2, 3] = "B-Row2Col1"

outArray[2, 4] = "B-Row2Col2"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayMax    

Purpose:

The ArrayMax function returns the largest value in an array.

Syntax:

x = ArrayMax(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

array[1]=1

array[2]=9

array[3]=14

x = ArrayMax(array)  

Returns:

x = 14

 

ArrayMerge   

Purpose:

This function returns a new single array by merging the values of two input arrays starting at a specified row and column. Specifically, this function replaces the values of one input array with those of a second input array, starting at a specified row and column.

Syntax:

status = ArrayMerge(inputArray1, inputArray2, "outputArrayName", startRow, startCol,

                                  ignoreValuesArray)

inputArray1 – the name of the first input array whose values are to be replaced.

inputArray2 – the name of the second input array whose values are to be merged into the first input array.

outputArrayName – name of the new output array.

startRow – the starting row position of the first input array from which the replacement is to be made.

startCol – the starting column position of the first input array from which the replacement is to be made.

ignoreValuesArray – A list of values from the second input array which are NOT to be replaced in the first input array, i.e., to be ignored for replacement.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS calculates the maximum number of rows and columns in the first input array; then does the same for the second input array.

•LIMS then creates an output array as a copy of the first input array, with the same number of rows and columns.

•LIMS checks if the specified starting row and column values (for beginning of the replacement) falls within the maximum number of rows and columns in the first input array.

•If the above condition is satisfied, LIMS takes the first value from the second input array and checks if this value matches any in the list of specified values to be ignored (if the "ignoreValues" has been specified).

•If not, then LIMS proceeds to populate the output array at the specified starting row and column position with the value taken from the second input array, replacing the original value. If this value matches any specified values to be ignored, then the replacement is not done.

•LIMS continues the above process for all rows and columns of the output array till the last row and column of either of the two input arrays has been reached, performing the checks and replacements as explained above.

 Example:

 i) The following example creates a copy of the first input array "inArray1" (3 rows and 2 columns) as the output array "outArray", reads the values from the second input array "inArray2" (2 rows and 2 columns) and replaces the values in the output array starting from the second row and first column of the output array. No ignorable values have been specified.

inArray1[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

inArray1[1, 2] = "A-1-2"

inArray1[2, 1] = "A-2-1"

inArray1[2, 2] = "A-2-2"

inArray1[3, 1] = "A-3-1"

inArray1[3, 2] = "A-3-2"

 

inArray2[1, 1] = "B-1-1"

inArray2[1, 2] = "B-1-2"

inArray2[2, 1] = "B-2-1"

inArray2[2, 2] = "B-2-2"

 

status = ArrayMerge(inArray1, inArray2, "outArray", 2, 1, ) returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

outArray[1, 2] = "A-1-2"

outArray[2, 1] = "B-1-1"

outArray[2, 2] = "B-1-2"

outArray[3, 1] = "B-2-1"

outArray[3, 2] = "B-2-2"

 

ii) In the following example, the same two input arrays mentioned above are merged, this time with a list of values to be ignored specified in the array "ignoreVal" as stated below.

ignoreVal[1, 1] = "B-1-2"

ignoreVal[1, 2] = "B-2-1"

 

status = ArrayMerge(inArray1, inArray2, "outArray", 2, 1, ignoreVal)

 

returns:

outArray[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

outArray[1, 2] = "A-1-2"

outArray[2, 1] = "B-1-1"

outArray[2, 2] = "A-2-2"

outArray[3, 1] = "A-3-1"

outArray[3, 2] = "B-2-2"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayMin    

Purpose:

The ArrayMin function returns the smallest value in an array.

Syntax:

x = ArrayMin(array)

Comments:

array is a one-dimensional array.

Example:

array[1]=1

array[2]=9

array[3]=14

x = ArrayMin(array) 

Returns:

x = 1

 

ArrayOfSubstrings    

Purpose:

The ArrayOfSubStrings function parses a string using a delimiter and returns an array of substrings.

Syntax:

numElements = ArrayOfSubstrings(inputString, delimiterCharacter,"outputArrayName", includeBlankStartEndElementsFlag)

inputString – string text to be parsed into an array.

delimiterCharacter – a single character that denotes separate entries in the array

outputArrayName – string representing the name of the generated array.

includeBlankStartEndElementsFlag – Boolean, if set to T, the system includes blank elements within the array if the delimiter is determined to be the first or last element of the entry at the beginning or end of the string.  If set to F, the system does not include blank entries in the array.  Default value is false.

Comments:

 

Example:

Example A:

inputString = "How are you? I am fine. And you?"

delimiterCharacter = "?"

outputArrayName = "substringArray"

numElements = ArrayOfSubstrings(inputString, delimiterCharacter, outputArrayName, "T")

Example B:

inputString = "How are you? I am fine. And you?"

delimiterCharacter = "?"

outputArrayName = "substringArray"

numElements = ArrayOfSubstrings(inputString, delimiterCharacter, outputArrayName, "F")

Returns:

Example A:

numElements = 3

substringArray[1] = ‘How are you’

substringArray[2] = ‘I am fine. And you’

substringArray[3] = ‘’

 

Example B:

numElements = 2

substringArray[1] = ‘How are you’

substringArray[2] = ‘I am fine. And you’

 

ArrayRandomize

Purpose:

The ArrayRandomize function randomizes the values in an array and returns a 1 dimensional array with the randomized values.

Syntax:

status = ArrayRandomize(array, "outputArrayName")    

array – name of the array to randomize.  This can be a one dimensional or two dimensional array

outputArrayName – name of the output array.  This is a one dimensional array.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the input array.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Parameter 1 is not an array”.

•The system checks the size of input array.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Input array is empty”.

•The system randomizes the array elements and returns them in a one dimensional array.

 

Example:

 

 

anArray[1,1] = "1A"

anArray[1,2] = "1B"

anArray[1,3] = "1C"

anArray[1,4] = "1D"

anArray[2,1] = "2A"

anArray[2,2] = "2B"

anArray[2,3] = "2C"

anArray[2,4] = "2D"

 

status = ArrayRandomize(anArray, "ArrayRandomized")

 

The output array is: ArrayRandomized[8] with the entries in random order.

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful, false otherwise.

 

ArrayRemoveNulls

Purpose:

The ArrayRemoveNulls function processes an array by column. If the specified column has any null values, the corresponding rows will be removed.

Syntax:

status = ArrayRemoveNulls (inputArray, "outputArrayName", column)    

inputArray – (Required) name of the array to process.  This can be a one dimensional or two dimensional array

outputArrayName – (Required) name of the output array.  This can be the same name as the input array, in which case the input array is overwritten with the processed array.

column – (Integer, Optional) this parameter identifies the column to check.  If not specified, then only a row that contains all nulls will be removed.

NOTE:  The term 'Nulls' refers to array values which when converted to a string have zero size.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Invalid input array”.

•The system checks the size of inputArray.  If it is larger than 2 dimensions, the system will trigger the error: “Only 1D or 2D arrays supported”.

•The system checks the outputArrayName parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: “Must specify outputArrayName”.

•The system checks the column parameter.  If present and it is not a string, the system will trigger the error: “If specified column must be integer”.

•The system removes the rows containing nulls in the specified column and returns the processed values in an array.

Example:

 

 

The following example does not specify a column so when the array is processed any row that contains all nulls is removed:

anArray[1,1] = "1A"

anArray[3,1] = "3A"

anArray[3,2] = "3B"

anArray[4,1] = "4A"

status = ArrayRemoveNulls(anArray, "outputArray",)

To demonstrate how the function works, the following figure shows the input array, anArray, on the left and the processed array, outputArray, on the right

 

 

If this example had specified column = 2, then rows 1,2 and 4 would all be removed.

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

ArraySearch

Purpose:

The ArraySearch function searches an array based on the specified minValue and/or maxValue or based on the specified match value.

Syntax:

status = ArraySearch(inputArray, minValue, maxValue, match, " outputArrayName ", outputFormat, rowsArray, columnsArray)

inputArray – (Required) name of the array to process.  This can be a one dimensional, two dimensional array or three dimensional array.

minValue (Optional) – the minimum value of the filter, i.e., the lower limit of the filter. The function will search for values in the input array that are >= the specified min value.

maxValue (Optional) – the maximum value of the filter, i.e., the upper limit of the filter. The function will search for values in the input array that are <= the specified max value.

match – (Optional) – the value to be used in the filter as an exact match. The function will search for the exact value specified in the input array.  If specified, the minValue and maxValue parameters should not have values.

outputArrayName – (Text, Required) name of the output array which will contain the row and column positions for the found entries. 

outputFormat – the format of the output array, i.e. one-dimensional ("1D") or two-dimensional ("2D").

rowsArray – (Array, Optional) One dimensional array specifying the row numbers that are to be searched.  If Empty then all rows are searched.

columnsArray – (Array, Optional) One dimensional array specifying the column numbers that are to be searched.  If Empty then all columns are searched.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS checks if a minimum value, a maximum value and a matching value have all been specified. If yes, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with:  "Cannot specify both min / max and match values".

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: "Input array not specified".

•LIMS then checks the specified output format.  If the inputArray is one-and the specified output format is two-dimensional, the system will trigger the error: "Cannot create a 2D array from a 1D input array"

•LIMS checks if a minimum value of the filter has been specified; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the minimum value to search for in the input array.

•LIMS checks if a maximum value of the filter has been specified; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the maximum value to search for in the input array.

•If minimum and maximum values for the filter have not been specified, then LIMS checks if an exact match value has been specified as a filter; if yes, then LIMS marks that as the exact value to search for in the input array.

•LIMS then checks the specified output format, i.e., "1D" (for one-dimensional) or "2D" (for two-dimensional).  If not specified it defaults to 1D.

•LIMS searches the input array for values specified either within the minimum and maximum ranges OR as an exact match. If any matching values are found, LIMS populates the output array in the specified format (i.e., "1D" or "2D") with the index keys of the matching values of the input array.

Example:

 

 

The following example The following example searches column 2 of the given input array "inputArray" for values between 15 and 30 and returns the output array "outputArray" with the matching index values.

 

inputArray[1, 1] = 5

inputArray[1, 2] = 10

inputArray[2, 1] = 15

inputArray[2, 2] = 20

inputArray[3, 1] = 25

inputArray[3, 2] = 30

minValue = 15

maxValue = 30

columnsArray[1] = 2

status = ArraySearch(inputArray, minValue, maxValue, , "outputArray", "2D", , columnsArray)

 

This search found entries in rows 2 and 3 of column 2:

outputArray[1,1] = 2

outputArray[1,2] = 2

outputArray[1,1] = 3

outputArray[1,2] = 2

 

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

ArraySearchColumns   

Purpose:

The ArraySearchColumns function returns an array of indexes based on matches determined by the rowsArray and valuesArray.  Each reported match must match all the values specified in the valuesArray for the rows specified in the rowsArray. 

Syntax:

status = ArraySearchColumns(inputArray, "outputArrayName", rowsArray, valuesArray)

inputArray – name of the 2D array that will be searched for the specified values.

outputArrayName – string value of the name of the output array that contains the column numbers with matches for the specified values.

rowsArray – an array containing the row numbers to be checked when searching the input array.

valuesArray – an array containing values corresponding to the specified row numbers that will be used for searching the array.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks whether the sizes of the specified rowsArray and valuesArray are equal; if not, then LIMS will trigger the error "Error: rows and values arrays must be the same size".

•LIMS searches the input array and outputs the results in the outputArrayName.

 Example:

The following example searches the input array for the text "Five" in the second row and the text "three" in the third row.

 

inputArray[1,1] = "One"

inputArray[1,2] = "Two"

inputArray[2,1] = "Three"

inputArray[2,2] = "Five"

inputArray[3,1] = "Five"

inputArray[3,2] = "Three"

 

rowsArray[1] = 2

valuesArray[1] = "Five"

rowsArray[2] = 3

valuesArray[2] = "Three"

 

status = ArraySearchColumns (inputArray, "outputArrayName", rowsArray, valuesArray)

Returns:

outputArrayName[1] = 2

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false. 

 

ArraySearchRows   

Purpose:

The ArraySearchRows function returns an array of indexes based on matches determined by the columnsArray and valuesArray.  Each reported match must match all the values specified in the valuesArray for the columns specified in the columnsArray. 

Syntax:

status = ArraySearchRows(inputArray, "outputArrayName", columnsArray, valuesArray)

inputArray – name of the 2D array that will be searched for the specified values.

outputArrayName – string value of the name of the output array that contains the row numbers with matches for the specified values.

columnsArray – an array containing the column numbers to be checked when searching the input array.

valuesArray – an array containing values corresponding to the specified column numbers that will be used for searching the array.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks whether the sizes of the specified columnsArray and valuesArray are equal; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Error: columns and values arrays must be the same size".

•LIMS searches the input array and outputs the results in the outputArrayName

 Example:

The following example searches the input array for the text "three" in the first column and the text "Five" in the second column.

 

inputArray[1,1] = "One"

inputArray[1,2] = "Two"

inputArray[2,1] = "Three"

inputArray[2,2] = "Five"

inputArray[3,1] = "Five"

inputArray[3,2] = "Three"

 

columnArray[1] = 2

valuesArray[1] = "Five"

columnArray[2] = 1

valuesArray[2] = "Three"

 

status = ArraySearchRows(inputArray, "outputArrayName", columnArray, valuesArray)

Returns:

outputArrayName[1] = 2

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false. 

 

ArraySort

Purpose:

The ArraySort function sorts an array based on the specified column.

Syntax:

status = ArraySort(inputArray, "outputArrayName", column)    

inputArray – (Required) name of the array to process.  This can be a one dimensional or two dimensional array

outputArrayName – (Text, Optional) name of the output array.  If not specified, the input array is overwritten with the new sort order.

column – (Integer, Optional) this parameter identifies the column to be used as the basis of the sort.  If not specified, the default value is 1.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: “Input array not specified”.

•The system sorts the array elements and returns them in an array.

Example:

 

 

The following example will sort the array,anArray base on the values in column 1 and return the values in the array, sortedArray.

anArray[1,1] = "1A"

anArray[2,1] = "3B"

anArray[3,1] = "1C"

anArray[1,2] = "W"

anArray[2,2] = "W"

anArray[3,2] = "O"

 

column = 1

status = ArraySort(anArray, " sortedArray ", column)

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

ArraySortIndex    

Purpose:

The ArraySortIndex function sorts an array of values by index and returns the sorted indices in an array.

Syntax:

status = ArraySortIndex(inputArray, "outputArrayName")

Comments:

Indices are sorted in ascending order.

Example:

xArray[1] = 10

xArray[2] = 18

xArray[3] = 14

outputArrayName = "yArray"

status = ArraySortIndex(xArray, outputArrayName)

Returns:

 

 

yArray[1] = 1 

yArray[2] = 3

yArray[3] = 2

 

ArraySplit   

Purpose:

This function returns two new output arrays as subsets of a given input array by splitting the input array from the given splitting row or splitting column positions.

Syntax:

status = ArraySplit(inputArray, "outputArrayName1", "outputArrayName2", splitRowIndex,

                               splitColumnIndex)

inputArray –name of the input array.

outputArrayName1 – name of the first new output array.

ouputArrayName2 – name of the second new output array.

splitRowIndex – the row position of the input array from which the splitting is to be done.

splitColumnIndex – the column position of the input array from which the splitting is to be done.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks whether a splitting row index or a splitting column index value have been specified; if neither have been specified, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Must specify either a row or column split index".

•LIMS calculates the maximum number of rows and columns in the input array.

•LIMS checks if a splitting row index has been specified; if yes, then LIMS creates the first output array and populates the same with values from the input array beginning with the first value of the input array. This is done till the specified splitting row index value is reached.

•Once the splitting row index value is reached, then LIMS creates the second output array and populates the same with values from the input array beginning with the value at the specified splitting row index.

•If a splitting row index value has NOT been specified, then LIMS checks the specified splitting column index value, creates the first output array and populates the same with values from all rows of the input array where the column is less than the specified splitting column index value; it then proceeds to create the second output array and populates the same with values from all rows of the input array where the column is greater than or equal to the specified column index value.

•If both splitting row index value and splitting column index value have been specified, then LIMS considers only the splitting row index value; the splitting column index value is ignored.

 Example:

i) The following example splits the input array "inArray" (3 rows and 2 columns) from the 3rd row and returns 2 output arrays "outArray1" (2 rows and 2 columns) and "outArray2" (1 row and 2 columns).

inArray[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

inArray[1, 2] = "A-1-2"

inArray[2, 1] = "B-2-1"

inArray[2, 2] = "B-2-2"

inArray[3, 1] = "C-3-1"

inArray[3, 2] = "C-3-2"

 

status = ArraySplit(inArray, "outArray1", "outArray2", 3,  )

 

Returns:

outArray1[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

outArray1[1, 2] = "A-1-2"

outArray1[2, 1] = "B-2-1"

outArray1[2, 2] = "B-2-2"

 

outArray2[1, 1] = "C-3-1"

outArray2[1, 2] = "C-3-2"

 

ii) The following example splits the same input array mentioned above ("inArray") from the second column and returns 2 output arrays "outArray1" and "outArray2", each one having 3 rows and 1 column.

status = ArraySplit(inArray, "outArray1", "outArray2", , 2  ) returns:

outArray1[1, 1] = "A-1-1"

outArray1[2, 1] = "B-2-1"

outArray1[3, 1] = "C-3-1"

 

outArray2[1, 1] = "A-1-2"

outArray2[2, 1] = "B-2-2"

outArray2[3, 1] = "C-3-2"

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArraySubset    

Purpose:

The ArraySubset function returns a subset of an array based on selection criteria.

Syntax:

status = ArraySubset(inputArray, "subArrayName", rowStart, numRows, columnStart, numColumns)

inputArray – string defining the name of the parent array.

subArrayName – string defining the name of the generated array.

rowStart – integer defining the first row of the parent array to include in the generated array.

numRows – integer defining the number of rows to include in the generated array.

columnStart – integer defining the first column of the parent array to incluce in the generated array

numColumns – integer defining the number of columns to include in the generated array.

Comments:

This function returns last error when subset is outside of range.

Example:

inputArray[1, 1] = a
inputArray[1, 2] = b
inputArray[1, 3] = c
inputArray[2, 1] = d
inputArray[2, 2] = e
inputArray[2, 3] = f

rowStart = 1
numRows = 2
columnStart = 2
numColumns = 2
subArrayName = "subArray"

status = ArraySubset(inputArray, subArrayName, rowStart, numRows, columnStart, numColumns)

Returns:

subArray[1,1] = b

subArray[1,2] = c

subArray[2,1] = e

subArray[2,2] = f

 

ArrayToCSV    

Purpose:

The ArrayToCSV function creates a CSV (comma separated values) file from the data in the input array.

Syntax:

status = ArrayToCSV(inputArray, delimiter, dataBracketCharacter, filename, unicodeFlag)

inputArray – the array to be written out as a CSV file

delimiter – a single character string to be used to separate data values in the file

dataBracketCharacter – the string to be placed just before and after each data value

filename – the name of the CSV file to be created

unicodeFlag – Boolean; determines if the generated file is written in Unicode.  The default value is false.

Comments:

The default value for the delimiter is a comma.  The default value for the bracket is an empty string.  The bracket argument is provided to accommodate programs that require that data elements be enclosed within double quotes.

The function performs the following steps.

•If the input array contains no values, LIMS will trigger the error "Input array has no data".

•If the file name is blank, LIMS will trigger the error "File name not specified".

•If the delimiter is blank, LIMS will use the value "," (a comma) instead.

•If the file name specifies a directory that does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "File directory does not exist".

•If the input array has more than two dimensions, LIMS will trigger the error "Input array has more than two dimensions".

•LIMS creates the CSV file using the specified file name.  If this fails, LIMS will  trigger the error "File could not be created".

•LIMS writes the data elements out to the CSV file one row at a time.  The first dimension of the input array is interpreted as the row number, and the second dimension (if present) is interpreted as the column number.  Each data value is enclosed using the specified bracket character.  The specified delimiter is inserted between each pair of data values.  An empty string is used to represent any data value that is not present in the input array.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

inputArray[1,1] = "Row1Col1"
inputArray[1,3] = "Row1Col3"
inputArray[2,1] = "Row2Col1"
inputArray[2,2] = "Row2Col2"

status = ArrayToCSV(inputArray, "", "", "c:\myfolder\myfile.csv")

Writes the values in the input array to a comma-delimited values file named myfile.csv in the directory name myfolder on the C drive of the user’s computer.  Values are delimited by commas and are not enclosed.  Note that blank entries will be shown for row 1, column 2 and for row 2, column 3.

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ArrayToCSVString    

Purpose:

The ArrayToCSVString function converts an array to a delimited string.

Syntax:

csvString = ArrayToCSVString(inputArray, delimiter)

inputArray – the name of the one or two dimensional array to be coverted to a string.

Delimiter –  a single character used within the string to denote separate array values.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS evaluates the input array.  If the array is empty, LIMS returns a null value with the Last Error: Input Array has no data.

•LIMS evaluates the input delimiter.  If the size is 0, LIMS defaults the delimiter to ‘,’.

•LIMS determines the number of dimensions in the array:

•If one dimensional, LIMS assembles the string by posting each value in the array into the string separated by the delimiter.

•If two dimensional, LIMS assembles the string by posting each value in the first column of the array into the string separated by the delimiter.  At the end of the column, LIMS inserts a hard return, then posts each value in the second column of the array into the string separated by the delimiter.

•If there are more that two dimentions in the array, LIMS returns a null value with the Last Error: Input array has more than two dimensions.

NOTE: This function does not save to the database.

Example:

The following example converts the two dimensional array: DEMO into a CSV String using ‘,’ as the delimiter.

DEMO[1,1] = "Apple"
DEMO[1,2] = "Carrot"
DEMO[1,3] = "Steak"
DEMO[2,1] = "Fruit"
DEMO[2,3] = "Meat"
Delimiter = "*"
csvString = ArrayToCSVString(DEMO, delimiter)
csvString = "Apple*Carrot*Steak||Fruit**Meat||"
(Where || represent hard returns defining the end of a data column in the array)

Returns:

The function returns the csvString if successful.  If an error is detected, a null value is returned and the appropriate Last Error is set.

 

ArrayTranspose   

Purpose:

This function reads a given input array and returns an array by transposing the rows and columns of the input array.

Syntax:

status = ArrayTranspose(inputArray, "outputArrayName")

inputArray –name of the input array.

outputArrayName – name of the output array.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS calculates the maximum number of rows and columns in the input array.

•LIMS then proceeds to create the new output array and creates as many columns as the number of rows in the input array and as many rows as the number of columns in the output array.

•LIMS then picks up values from the input array and populates the output array by transposing the values of the rows and columns.

 Example:

The following example transposes the values of the input array "inArray" (2 rows and 5 columns) and returns an output array "outArray" (5 rows and 2 columns).

inArray[1, 1] = 11

inArray[1, 2] = 12

inArray[1, 3] = 13

inArray[1, 4] = 14

inArray[1, 5] = 15

inArray[2, 1] = 21

inArray[2, 2] = 22

inArray[2, 3] = 23

inArray[2, 4] = 24

inArray[2, 5] = 25

 

status = ArrayTranspose(inArray, "outArray")

 

Returns:

outArray[1, 1] = 11

outArray[1, 2] = 21

outArray[2, 1] = 12

outArray[2, 2] = 22

outArray[3, 1] = 13

outArray[3, 2] = 23

outArray[4, 1] = 14

outArray[4, 2] = 24

outArray[5, 1] = 15

outArray[5, 2] = 25

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

ArrayWithName    

Purpose:

The ArrayWithName function returns the contents of an array.

Syntax:

array  = ArrayWithName(arrayName)

arrayName – the name of the array that is to be read

Comments:

NOTE: The contents of the arrayName variable must be all lowercase e.g. in the following example if the array was called XYZ, the arrayname used would still be "xyz"


Example:

arrayName = "xyz"

xyz[1] = 899

xyz[2] = "boy"

xyz[3] = "girl"

array = ArrayWithName(arrayName)

Returns:

array[1] = 899

array[2] = ‘boy’

array[3] = ‘girl’

 

ClearArray    

Purpose:

The ClearArray function clears the contents of an array.

Syntax:

status = ClearArray(arrayName)

arrayName – the name of the array that is to be cleared

Comments:

This function is useful to clear the contents of an array between calls to functions.

Example:

‘ Sort indices from array x into array y

xArray[1] = 10

xArray[2] = 18

xArray[3] = 14

outputArrayName = "yArray"

status = ArraySortIndex(xArray, outputArrayName)

status = ClearArray(outputArrayName)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise. The function returns true if the array does not exist.

 

ContextTranslateArray

Purpose:

The ContextTranslateArray function uses the ContextSubstitutionEngine (CSE).  Like the ContextTranslate function, this function will process the input array as input (either one dimensional array or two dimensional array) and outputs an equivalent array with all values translated.  Refer to section 6.6.6 Context Substitution for more on using context substitution.

Syntax:

status = ContextTranslateArray(inputArray, "outputArrayName", sqlFlag, indexesToTranslate)    

inputArray – name of the array to translate.  This can be a one dimensional or two dimensional array

outputArrayName – name of the output array. 

sqlFlag – (Boolean) this parameter determines the mode of translation. If set to T' or 'Y' then the function works like the ContextTranslateSQl function where unresolved tags will be left as-is and dates are converted to {ts .. } format,etc.  Otherwise the function works like the ContextTranslate function where unresolved tags are replaced by blanks.

indexesToTranslate – (string)  a comma separated list of array indexes to translate. This may contain individual index numbers or ranges of index numbers.  For single dimensional arrays, this corresponds to the index numbers.  For two dimensional arrays, this corresponds to column numbers.

NOTE:  The function filters the indexesToTranslate list to only numbers, commas, and dashes.  Everything else is ignored.  For instance, "1.5-2" is treated the same as "15-2".

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Invalid input array”.

•The system checks the size of inputArray.  If it is larger than 2 dimensions, the system will trigger the error: “Input array cannot be more than 2 dimensions”.

•The system checks the outputArrayName parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: “No output array name specified...”.

•The system checks the indexesToTranslate parameter.  If present and it is not a string, the system will trigger the error: “If defined, the indexesToTranslate argument must be a string”.

•The system translates the array elements and returns them in an array.

Example:

 

 

The following example will process the tags in columns 2 and 3 and return the processed array.  The entries in positions [1,1], [2,1] and [2,3] will be the same as the original array.

smp_1 = 162

status = SelectSample(smp_1, "T", "F")

inputArray[1,1] = "{CLIENT_DIR}"

inputArray[1,2] = "{DATE}"

inputArray[2,1] = "{SAMPLE.SAMPLE_NUMBER}"

inputArray[2,2] = "{smp_1}"

inputArray[2,3] = "{SUBROUTINE:INVALID}"

sqlFlag = "T"

indexesToTranslate = "2-3"

status = ContextTranslateArray(inputArray, "aOutputArrayName", sqlFlag, indexesToTranslate)

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

EmptyArray

Purpose:

The EmptyArray function creates an empty array with the specified name.

Syntax:

value = EmptyArray()   

value – should be the name of the array to create.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system sets the value as an empty array. If an existing array name is specified that array will be cleared.

Example:

 

The following example creates an empty array named ‘myArray’:

myArray = EmptyArray() 

Returns:

 

The function sets the value as an empty array. 

 

GetArrayColumnNames

Purpose:

The GetArrayColumnNames function evaluates an array and returns a 1 dimensional array with the column names associated with the input array.

Syntax:

status = GetArrayColumnNames(inputArray, "outputArray")

inputArray – name of the array to check for column names.  This must be a two dimensional array

outputArray – name of the output array which will contain the column names that are found.  This is a one dimensional array.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Input array must be an array”.

•The system checks for headers associated with inputArray.  If it no headers are found, the system will trigger the error: “Input array has no column names defined”.

•The system checks the outputArrayName parameter.  If it is not present, the system will trigger the error: “No output array name specified”.

•The system processes the column names and returns them in a one dimensional array.

Example:

 

 

The following example returns an output array with (NAME, DESCRIPTION):

 

queryString = "Select NAME, DESCRIPTION from PRODUCT order by NAME"

status = SQL(queryString, "arrayProdNames", 15, "F", , )

status = GetArrayColumnNames(arrayProdNames, "outputArray")

 

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message and no output array will be created.

 

 

GetMaxArrayIndex

Purpose:

This function gets the maximum size of the array by the specified dimension

Syntax:

maxIndex = GetMaxArrayIndex(array, dimension)

array – Input array

dimension – number corresponding to the array dimension to be checked

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the input array.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Parameter 1 is not an array”.

•The system checks if the specified dimension is valid (i.e. the dimension specified is an integer value).  If it is not, the system will trigger the error: “The specified dimension is not valid: dimension”

•The system checks if the specified dimension exists in the array.  If it is larger, the system will trigger the error: “The specified dimension is larger than the array dimensions: dimension”. Otherwise, the system returns the maxIndex for the specified dimension.

Example:

 

 

anArray[1,1] = "1A"

anArray[1,2] = "1B"

anArray[1,3] = "1C"

anArray[1,4] = "1D"

anArray[2,1] = "2A"

anArray[2,2] = "2B"

anArray[2,3] = "2C"

anArray[2,4] = "2D"

 

maxIndex1 = GetMaxArrayIndex(array2, 1)

 

maxIndex2 = GetMaxArrayIndex(array2, 2)

 

maxIndex1 returns ‘2’

maxIndex2 returns ‘4’

 

Returns:

 

Returns the largest number for the dimension specified of the input array if successful, otherwise returns ERROR.

 

PrintArray    

Purpose:

The PrintArray function prints the contents of a one-dimensional or two-dimensional array on a printer. 

Syntax:

status = PrintArray(array, headers, description,printerName)

array – the array whose contents are to be printed

headers – the column headers to be printed across the top

description – string dispayed above the printed array.

printerName – the name of the printer on which the array is to be printed.  If set to "DEFAULT" or an invalid printer name then the Windows default printer is used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no array is specified, or if the array has no elements, LIMS triggers the array "No data to print".

•If the array has more than two dimensions, LIMS will trigger the error "Input array has more than two dimensions".

•LIMS prints the array on the specified printer.  Missing rows are skipped.  Missing columns are filled with blanks.  LIMS prints a header and a footer.  The header consists of the specified description.  The footer consists of the current date and time and the full name of the current user.

NOTE: The headers array is interpreted as a simple list.  Array indices are ignored in this array.  See the example below.


Example:

The following example prints an array on the "LAB108" printer.

myArray[1,1] = "X"
myArray[3,3] = "Y"
myHeaders[1] = "H-A"
myHeaders[2] = "H-B"
myHeaders[3] = "H-C"
status = PrintArray(myArray,  myHeaders, "Example", "LAB108")

The output is similar to the following.

Example

 

H-A

H-B

H-C

1

X

 

 

2

 

 

Y


Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetArrayColumnNames

Purpose:

The SetArrayColumnNames function associates the column names from an array with the specified array.

Syntax:

status = SetArrayColumnNames(inputArray, colNamesArray)   

inputArray – name of the array to receive the column names.  This must be a two dimensional array

colNamesArray – name of the array with the column headers.  This is a one dimensional array.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the inputArray parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Input array must be an array”.

•The system checks the inputArray dimensions.  If it is not a 2D array, the system will trigger the error: “Input array must have exactly 2 dimensions”.

•The system checks the colNamesArray parameter.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “Column names array must be an array”. If it is not a 1D array, the system will trigger the error: “Column names array must have exactly 1 dimension”.

•The system checks the colNamesArray parameter size. If it is not the same size as the inputArray, the system will trigger the error: “Column names array must match the number of input array columns”.

•The system processes the column names and associates them with the inputArray.

Example:

 

 

The following example sets the column names as Sample Number and Description for the samplesArray.  The column names do not become part of the samplesArray, however, the values associated with the array can be retrieved by the GetArrayColumnNames function.

samplesArray[1,1] = 6

samplesArray[1,2] = "First sample"

samplesArray[2,1] = 7

samplesArray[2,2] = "Second Sample"

colNamesArray[1] = "Sample Number"

colNamesArray[2] = "Description"

status = SetArrayColumnNames(samplesArray, colNamesArray)

Returns:

 

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

Ubound    

Purpose:

The Ubound function returns the number of non null entries in a column in an array.

Syntax:

x = Ubound(array, colNum)

Comments:

If colNum is not specified, then ubound returns the total number of non null entries in the array.

NOTE:  The Len function can be used to determine the number of defined entries in an array- unlike the Ubound function it will include nulls if the array position has been defined as null.


Example:

array[1, 1] = 10

array[2, 1] = 20

array[3, 1] = 30

array[1, 2] = 10

array[2, 2] = 20

col1 = Ubound(array, 1)

col2 = Ubound(array, 2)

NoCol = Ubound(array)

Returns:

col1 = 3

col2 = 2

NoCol = 5

 

UboundArray

Purpose:

This function gets the size of the array for the specified dimension, regardless of the number of values contained in the input array.

Syntax:

maxIndex = UboundArray(array, dimension)

array – the name of the array to be checked

dimension – number corresponding to the array dimension to be checked

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the size of array.  If it is 0, the system will trigger the error: “inputArray is empty”.

•The system checks the array.  If it is not an array, the system will trigger the error: “inputArray is not an array”.

•The system checks if the specified dimension is valid (i.e. the dimension specified is an integer value).  If it is not, the system will trigger the error: “dimension is not an integer”

•The system checks if the specified dimension exists in the array.  If it is larger, the system will trigger the error: “dimension does not exist”. Otherwise, the system returns the size for the specified dimension.

Example:

 

 

myArray[2,20] = "value 1"

myArray[10,5] = "value 2"

maxIndex = UboundArray(myArray, 1) ' Answers 10 (max index in 1st array dimension)

maxIndex = UboundArray(myArray, 2) ' Answers 20 (max index in 2nd array dimension)

Returns:

 

Returns the largest number for the dimension specified of the input array if successful, otherwise returns ERROR.

 

 

28.3.3    Audit Report Functions
 

Function

AuditBatchReport

AuditInventoryReport

AuditInvestigationReport

AuditLotReport

AuditProjectReport

AuditProtocolReport

AuditSampleReport

AuditTableReport

 

AuditBatchReport    

Purpose:

The AuditBatchReport function generates an audit report for a batch.

Syntax:

status = AuditBatchReport(batchName, eventTypesArray, fieldsArray, appendRecords, reportQueryTagName, viewReportFlag, printFlag)

batchName – the name of the batch for which the audit report is to be generated

eventTypesArray – an array of the audit types to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all audit types will be included.

fieldsArray – an array of the database fields to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all database fields will be included.  Elements of this array must be specified in the form tablename.fieldname.  Elements that do not contain a period are ignored.

appendRecords – the value true if records should be appended to any existing records already present in the audit reporting tables; the value false if any existing records for the specified batch should be deleted before generating new audit report records (optional; the default is false).  The value true is valid only when no eventTypesArray and no fieldsArray are specified.

reportQueryTagName – the name of the query tag to be used for generating the report (optional; the default is blank, i.e., no report will be generated)

viewReportFlag – the value true if the report should be shown in a window (optional; the default is false)

printFlag – the value true if the report should be sent to the printer (optional; the default is false)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the batch for which the audit report is to be generated.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Batch does not exist".

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if either or both of the eventsTypesArray and the fieldsArray have at least one element, LIMS will trigger the error "Can only append records when all records are requested".

•LIMS retrieves all records from the audit trail for the specified batch.  If none are found, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•If the value of appendRecords is false, LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified batch.

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if the audit reporting tables contain partial records (i.e., records created from a prior call to this function with at least one element for eventTypesArray or fieldsArray), LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified batch.

•LIMS adds the retrieved audit trail records to the audit reporting tables.  If the value of appendRecords is true, LIMS will only add those records whose audit timestamp is greater than the maximum audit timestamp already present in the audit reporting tables for the specified batch.  If an eventTypesArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose audit types matches one of the elements of that array.  If a fieldsArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose field name matches one of the elements in that array.

•If any insert fails, LIMS will roll back the database inserts and trigger the error "Error writing to the BATCH_AUDIT_RPT table"  Otherwise, LIMS will commit the inserts to the database.

•If no value is specified for reportQueryTagName, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•LIMS retrieves the query tag with the specified name.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Report does not exist".

•LIMS generates the report. LIMS passes the batch name as a formula field named "BATCH_AUDIT_RPT.BATCH_NAME".  If the value of viewReportFlag is false, the report will be generated in the background.  If the value of printFlag is true, the report will be sent to the printer.

The source audit trail table for this function is the table named BATCH_AUDIT_LOG.

The audit reporting tables for this function are the tables named BATCH_AUDIT_OLD, BATCH_AUDIT_CURRENT, and BATCH_AUDIT_RPT.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example populates the audit reporting tables with all audit trail records for the batch named "BATCH1" without generating a report.

Status = AuditBatchReport("BATCH1", "", "", false, "", "", "")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

AuditInventoryReport    

Purpose:

The AuditInventoryReport function generates an audit report for an inventory item.

Syntax:

status = AuditInventoryReport(itemNumber, eventTypesArray, fieldsArray, appendRecords, reportQueryTagName, viewReportFlag, printFlag)

itemNumber – the item number of the inventory item for which the audit report is to be generated

eventTypesArray – an array of the audit types to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all audit types will be included.

fieldsArray – an array of the database fields to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all database fields will be included.  Elements of this array must be specified in the form tablename.fieldname.  Elements that do not contain a period are ignored.

appendRecords – the value true if records should be appended to any existing records already present in the audit reporting tables; the value false if any existing records for the specified inventory item should be deleted before generating new audit report records (optional; the default is false).  The value true is valid only when no eventTypesArray and no fieldsArray are specified.

reportQueryTagName – the name of the query tag to be used for generating the report (optional; the default is blank, i.e., no report will be generated)

viewReportFlag – the value true if the report should be shown in a window (optional; the default is false)

printFlag – the value true if the report should be sent to the printer (optional; the default is false)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the inventory item for which the audit report is to be generated.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Inventory Item does not exist".

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if either or both of the eventsTypesArray and the fieldsArray have at least one element, LIMS will trigger the error "Can only append records when all records are requested".

•LIMS retrieves all records from the audit trail for the specified inventory item.  If none are found, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•If the value of appendRecords is false, LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified inventory item.

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if the audit reporting tables contain partial records (i.e., records created from a prior call to this function with at least one element for eventTypesArray or fieldsArray), LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified inventory item.

•LIMS adds the retrieved audit trail records to the audit reporting tables.  If the value of appendRecords is true, LIMS will only add those records whose audit timestamp is greater than the maximum audit timestamp already present in the audit reporting tables for the specified inventory item.  If an eventTypesArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose audit types matches one of the elements of that array.  If a fieldsArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose field name matches one of the elements in that array.

•If any insert fails, LIMS will roll back the database inserts and trigger the error "Error writing to the INVENTORY_AUDIT_RPT table"  Otherwise, LIMS will commit the inserts to the database.

•If no value is specified for reportQueryTagName, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•LIMS retrieves the query tag with the specified name.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Report does not exist".

•LIMS generates the report. LIMS passes the inventory item number as a formula field named "INVENTORY_AUDIT_RPT.ITEM_NUMBER".  If the value of viewReportFlag is false, the report will be generated in the background.  If the value of printFlag is true, the report will be sent to the printer.

The source audit trail table for this function is the table named INVENTORY_LOG.

The audit reporting tables for this function are the tables named INV_AUDIT_OLD, INV_AUDIT_CURRENT, and INVENTORY_AUDIT_RPT.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example populates the audit reporting tables with all audit trail records for the inventory item with item number 701 without generating a report.

Status = AuditInventoryReport(701, "", "", false, "", "", "")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.

AuditLotReport    

Purpose:

The AuditLotReport function generates an audit report for a lot.

Syntax:

status = AuditLotReport(lotNumber, eventTypesArray, fieldsArray, appendRecords, reportQueryTagName, viewReportFlag, printFlag)

lotNumber – the lot number of the lot for which the audit report is to be generated

eventTypesArray – an array of the audit types to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all audit types will be included.

fieldsArray – an array of the database fields to be included in the audit report.  If no array or an empty array is specified, all database fields will be included.  Elements of this array must be specified in the form tablename.fieldname.  Elements that do not contain a period are ignored.

appendRecords – the value true if records should be appended to any existing records already present in the audit reporting tables; the value false if any existing records for the specified lot should be deleted before generating new audit report records (optional; the default is false).  The value true is valid only when no eventTypesArray and no fieldsArray are specified.

reportQueryTagName – the name of the query tag to be used for generating the report (optional; the default is blank, i.e., no report will be generated)

viewReportFlag – the value true if the report should be shown in a window (optional; the default is false)

printFlag – the value true if the report should be sent to the printer (optional; the default is false)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the lot for which the audit report is to be generated.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Lot record does not exist for the specified lot number".

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if either or both of the eventsTypesArray and the fieldsArray have at least one element, LIMS will trigger the error "Can only append records when all records are requested".

•LIMS retrieves all records from the audit trail for the specified lot.  If none are found, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•If the value of appendRecords is false, LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified lot.

•If the value of appendRecords is true, and if the audit reporting tables contain partial records (i.e., records created from a prior call to this function with at least one element for eventTypesArray or fieldsArray), LIMS will delete all records from the audit reporting tables for the specified lot.

•LIMS adds the retrieved audit trail records to the audit reporting tables.  If the value of appendRecords is true, LIMS will only add those records whose audit timestamp is greater than the maximum audit timestamp already present in the audit reporting tables for the specified lot.  If an eventTypesArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose audit types matches one of the elements of that array.  If a fieldsArray is specified, LIMS will only add those records whose field name matches one of the elements in that array.

•If any insert fails, LIMS will roll back the database inserts and trigger the error "Error writing to the LOT_AUDIT_RPT table"  Otherwise, LIMS will commit the inserts to the database.

•If no value is specified for reportQueryTagName, LIMS will stop and return the value true.

•LIMS retrieves the query tag with the specified name.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Report does not exist".

•LIMS generates the report. LIMS passes the lot name as a formula field named "LOT_AUDIT_RPT.LOT_NUMBER".  If the value of viewReportFlag is false, the report will be generated in the background.  If the value of printFlag is true, the report will be sent to the printer.

The source audit trail table for this function is the table named LOT_AUDIT_LOG.

The audit reporting tables for this function are the tables named LOT_AUDIT_OLD, LOT_AUDIT_CURRENT, and LOT_AUDIT_RPT.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example populates the audit reporting tables with all audit trail records for the lot with lot number 6627 without generating a report.

Status = AuditLotReport(6627, "", "", false, "", "", "")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.
