CurlEasyCleanup

Purpose:

Closes the libcurl session named curlSessionName and frees any resources associated with it, including any SLists and / or files specified from CurlEasySetReadFile() or CurlEasySetWriteFile().  This must be called for each libcurl session that is created.

NOTE: If the session has not been cleaned up, any subsequent sessions initiated with the same curlSessionName will fail.


Syntax:

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1"

curlOption = "CURLOPT_URL"

optionValue = "http://www.example.com"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

'** Perform some actions (Actions omitted from this example)

 

'** Clean up Session when completed

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

CurlEasyEscape

Purpose:

Converts the given string into a URL encoded string. Any input characters which are not a-z, A-Z, 0-9, dash (-), period (.), underscore (_), or tilde (~) are converted to their URL escaped version (%NN where NN is a two-digit hexadecimal number).

Syntax:

status = CurlEasyEscape(curlSessionName, inputString, "outputString")

curlSessionName – (string) - The name of the curl session.

inputString – (string) - a string to be encoded.

outputString – (string) - The variable name to receive the encoded string.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if inputString is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument inputString must be a string”

3.  LabWare checks if outputString is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument outputString must be a string”

4.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1selectFileName

inputString = "http://www.ex&ample$.com"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

status = CurlEasyEscape(curlSessionName, inputString, "outputString")

 

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

msgbox(outputString)

(The message box will display: http%3A%2F%2Fwww.ex%26ample%24.com)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if unsuccessful and sets lastError with an appropriate error message.

 

CurlEasyGetInfo

Purpose:

Used to retrieve option values from a libcurl easy session.  Options are defined in the libcurl documentation for curl_easy_getinfo(). (https://curl.haxx.se/libcurl/c/curl_easy_getinfo.html).

Syntax:

value = CurlEasyGetInfo(curlSessionName, curlOption)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

curlOption – (string) – A valid libcurl easy option constant.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if curlOption is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlOption must be a string”

3.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

'** Perform some actions (Actions omitted from this example)

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

 

'** Get info about the session

value = CurlEasyGetInfo(curlSessionName, "CURLINFO_EFFECTIVE_URL")

value = CurlEasyGetInfo(curlSessionName, “CURLINFO_CONTENT_TYPE”)

 

'** Cleanup

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers the value if successful.  Answers ERROR if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

CurlEasyInit

Purpose:

Creates a named libcurl session named curlSessionName.  When finished with the session, CurlEasyCleanup() should be called.  This paradigm is very similar to the one used by DLLOpen() and DLLClose().

Syntax:

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the libcurl easy session to create.  This can be any unique string (case insensitive).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if the curl session is already present.  If so, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name already exists:“

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Perform some actions (Actions omitted from this example)

'** Clean up Session when completed

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

 

CurlEasyPerform

Purpose:

Performs the libcurl easy session. 

Syntax:

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set Options, then perform the session (Options omitted from this example)

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

 

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

NOTE: If CurlEasySetWriteData() was used, any return value is stored in the supplied variable.  Similarly, if CurlEasySetWriteFile() was used, the return value is stored in the file.


Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

 

CurlEasySetOption

Purpose:

Used to set options on a libcurl easy session.  Options are defined in the libcurl documentation for curl_easy_setopt() (https://curl.haxx.se/libcurl/c/curl_easy_setopt.html).  Only options which set values to strings, numbers, booleans, and slists are supported.  Callback functions are not supported except for the ones that have special LIMS Basic functions.

Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, curlOption, optionValue, optionConstant, optionSList)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

curlOption - (string) - A valid libcurl easy option constant.

optionValue - (string or boolean) - The value for the option.  The value must be a string, but can be TRUE or FALSE if the option is a Boolean.

NOTE: TRUE and FALSE are converted to the appropriate libcurl value (1 or 0).

optionConstant – (string) - the name of a libcurl constant

optionSList – (string) - the name of a curl easy slist

NOTE: The function parameters optionValue, optionConstant, and optionSList are mutually exclusive.  Only one can be passed at a time.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if curlOption is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlOption must be a string”

3.  LabWare checks if optionConstant is present and is a string. If present and not a string, the following error is returned: “If specified, argument optionConstant must be a string”

4.  LabWare checks if optionSList is present and is a string. If present and not a string, the following error is returned: “If specified, argument optionSList must be a string”

5.  If more than one of optionValue, optionConstant, or optionSList are defined, the following error is displayed: “Arguments optionValue, optionConstant, and optionSList are mutually exclusive”

6.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Set Mail Info

SMTPPassword = InputPassword("Password for Mail")

mailFrom = "mailfrom.example@example.com"

mailto = "mailto.example@ example.com"

mailCc = "mailcc.example@ example.com"

'** for simplicity of this example, the mail message is reduced to a variable

payload = theMailMessageFormatted

 

'** Initiate the session

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set the options:

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_USERNAME", mailFrom)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_PASSWORD", SMTPPassword) 

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", "smtp://smtp.example.com:587") 

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_USE_SSL",,"CURLUSESSL_ALL")

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_MAIL_FROM", mailFrom) 

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailTo)

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailCc)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_MAIL_RCPT", , ,"recipients") 

status = CurlEasySetReadData(curlSessionName, payload)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_UPLOAD", TRUE ) 

 

'** Perform and Cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName) 

0Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

 

CurlEasySetOptions

Purpose:

Sets one or more curl options.  This is very similar to CurlEasySetOption(), with the difference being that multiple options can be set in a single call.  Internally this will call CurlEasySetOption() for every row in inputArray.  The statuses and lastError values for each call are stored in the variable named outputArray.

Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetOptions(curlSessionName, inputArray, "outputArray")

curlSessionName – (string) - The name of the curl session.

inputArray – (2 dimensional array) - This is the set of options to set.  There is one row per option and the columns are: <curlOption>, <optionValue>, <optionConstant>, and <optionSList>.  These are passed unmodified to CurlEasySetOption, so refer to its documentation for allowed values.

outputArray - (optional)  (string) - The array variable name to store the status and lastError values from each option.  When defined, a two dimentional array will be populated with the columns <status> and <lastError> corresponding to the option with the same index.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if inputArray is an array. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument inputArray must be an array”

3.  LabWare checks if inputArray is a 2 dimentional array. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument inputArray must be a two dimensional array”

4.  Labware checks if inputArray has at least two columns. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument inputArray must have at least two columns”

5.  Labware checks if inputArray has at more than four columns. If so, the following error is returned: “Argument inputArray must have at most four columns”

6.  LabWare checks if outputArray is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument outputArray must be a string”

7.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Set Mail Info

SMTPPassword = InputPassword("Password for Mail")

mailFrom = "mailfrom.example@gmail.com"

mailto = "mailto.example@gmail.com"

mailCc = "mailcc.example@gmail.com"

 

'** for simplicity of this example, the mail message is reduced to a variable

payload = theMailMessageFormatted

 

'** Set Recipient SList

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailTo)

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailCc)

 

'** Initiate the session

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set the options – Note the positions in the arrays for CURLOPT_USE_SLL (the value is a constant, so uses column 3), and CURL_MAIL_RCPT (the value is a SList, so uses column 4)

optionArray[1,1] = "CURLOPT_USERNAME"

optionArray[1,2] = "labware.test@gmail.com"

optionArray[2,1] = "CURLOPT_PASSWORD"

optionArray[2,2] = password

optionArray[3,1] = "CURLOPT_URL"

optionArray[3,2] = "smtp://smtp.gmail.com:587"

optionArray[4,1] = "CURLOPT_USE_SSL"

optionArray[4,3] = "CURLUSESSL_ALL"

optionArray[5,1] = "CURLOPT_MAIL_FROM"

optionArray[5,2] = mailFrom

optionArray[6,1] = "CURLOPT_MAIL_RCPT"

optionArray[6,4] = "recipients"

optionArray[7,1] = "CURLOPT_UPLOAD"

optionArray[7,2] = TRUE

status = CurlEasySetOptions(curlSessionName, optionArray, "outputArray")

 

status = CurlEasySetReadData(curlSessionName, payload)

 

'** Perform and Cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName) 

Returns:

Returns true if all options were set successfully.  If at least one option fails for any reason, false will be returned and lastError will contain the last error encountered (if multiple options failed, the lastError variable will contain the error from the last failure.

 

 

CurlEasySetReadData

Purpose:

Used to set the CURLOPT_READFUNCTION / CURLOPT_READDATA options based on the given string data payload.

NOTE: Libcurl considers READ_DATA to be data read locally and written to the remote server.  This is considered the payload from the curl operation.


Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetReadData(curlSessionName, payload, binaryFlag)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

payload - (string) - The text data to send to the server as a payload.

binaryFlag – (Boolean) - If binaryFlag is true, the data will not be converted to UTF-8 before sending. Default value is True.

CAUTION: The Binary flag should be set to false only if the file is a text file and conversion to UTF-8 is required. If the binaryFlag is set to false for any other file type, the data may not be readable.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if payload is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument payload must be a string”

3.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Set variable information

curlUrl = "ftp://ftp.example.com/readdatatest.txt"

curlSessionName = "ReadData1"

payload = PromptForLongText("Information to Upload", "", 100)

 

'** Initiate the session

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set the URL to the ftp site/file name and send ftp login info

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", curlUrl)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_USERNAME", "ftptest")

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_PASSWORD", "ftppassword")

 

'** allow upload and upload data

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_UPLOAD", TRUE)

status = CurlEasySetReadData(curlSessionName, payload, "F")

 

'** Perform and cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

CurlEasySetReadFile

Purpose:

Used to set the CURLOPT_READFUNCTION / CURLOPT_READDATA options based on the given fileName.

NOTE: Libcurl considers READ_DATA to be data read locally and written to the remote server.  This is considered the payload from the curl operation.


Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetReadFile(curlSessionName, fileName, binaryFlag)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

fileName - (string) - The file containing the data to send to the server as a payload.

binaryFlag – (Boolean) - If binaryFlag is true, the data will not be converted to UTF-8 before sending. Default is True.

CAUTION: The Binary flag should be set to false only if the file is a text file and conversion to UTF-8 is required. If the binaryFlag is set to false for any other file type, it may be corrupted when transferred to the remote server.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if fileName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument fileName must be a string”

3.  LabWare checks if fileName exists. If not, the following error is returned: “File name does not exist:”

4.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Set variable information

curlUrl = "ftp://ftp.example.com/myFile.txt"

curlSessionName = "ReadFile1"

selectFileName = “C:\myFile.txt”

 

'** Initiate the session

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set the URL to the ftp site/file name and send ftp login info

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", curlUrl)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_USERNAME", "ftptest")

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_PASSWORD", "ftppassword")

 

'** allow upload and upload data

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_UPLOAD", TRUE)

 

'** set the file name to upload to the selected file

status = CurlEasySetReadFile(curlSessionName, selectFileName, "T")

 

'** Perform and cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message

 

 

CurlEasySetWriteData

Purpose:

Used to set the CURLOPT_WRITEFUNCTION / CURLOPT_WRITEDATA.  This stores the write data to the named variable curlWriteData.

NOTE: Libcurl considers WRITE_DATA to be data read from the remote server and written locally. This could be considered the return value from the curl operation.


Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetWriteData(curlSessionName, "curlWriteData",binaryFlag)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

curlWriteData - (string) - The name of the LIMS Basic variable to receive the return value from the CurlEasyPerform() call.

binaryFlag – (Boolean) - If binaryFlag is true, the data will not be converted from UTF-8 (libcurl’s Unicode format) to UTF-16 (LabWare’s Unicode format). Default value is True.

CAUTION: The Binary flag should be set to false only if the returned data will be text and conversion to UTF-16 is required. If the binaryFlag is set to false for any other file type, the data may not be readable.  If the binaryFlag is set to true and Unicode text is returned, the text may appear garbled due to the lims treating UTF-8 data as ascii.

 

NOTE: See Note in CurlEasySetWriteFile() for more details on the binary flag setting.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if curlWriteData is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlWriteData must be a string”

3.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Initiate Session and set URL

curlURL = "http://www.example.com"

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", curlUrl)

 

'** Set Write Data Option

status = CurlEasySetWriteData(curlSessionName, "getURLData")

 

'** Perform and Cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

 

'** Display Data

msgboxHtml(getURLData,1000,800)

‘** Message box will display the example.com page contents

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

CurlEasySetWriteFile

Purpose:

Used to set the CURLOPT_WRITEFUNCTION / CURLOPT_WRITEDATA.  This stores the write data to the file fileName.

NOTE: Libcurl considers WRITE_DATA to be data read from the remote server and written locally. This could be considered the return value from the curl operation


Syntax:

status = CurlEasySetWriteFile(curlSessionName, fileName, binaryFlag)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

fileName - (string) - The name of the file to receive the return value from the CurlEasyPerform() call.

binaryFlag - (Boolean) - If binaryFlag is true, the data will not be converted from UTF-8 (libcurl’s Unicode format) to UTF-16 (LabWare’s Unicode format). Default value is True.

CAUTION: The Binary flag should be set to false only if the returned data will be text and conversion to UTF-16 is required. If the binaryFlag is set to false for non-Unicode data, the data may not be readable.  If the binaryFlag is set to true and Unicode text is returned, the text file will be encoded with UTF-8 instead of UTF-16.

 

NOTE: For both CurlEasySetWriteData() and CurlEasySetWriteFile(), if the binary flag is set to false, the data/file is converted to UTF-16. Unicode data from libcurl is UTF-8; however as the LabWare system uses UTF-16, the data is further converted before being saved.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if fileName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument fileName must be a string”

3.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

'** Initiate Session and set URL

curlURL = "http://www.example.com/images/"

fileName = "myImage.gif"

 

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

optionValue = curlUrl & fileName

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", optionValue)

 

'** Set Write file location

writeDir = PromptForDirectory("Select Location For File")

writeFileName = writeDir & "\" & fileName

status = CurlEasySetWriteFile(curlSessionName, writeFileName)

 

'** Perform and cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

CurlEasyUnescape

Purpose:

Converts the given URL encoded string back into a normal string

Syntax:

status = CurlEasyUnescape(curlSessionName, inputString, "outputString")

curlSessionName – (string) - The name of the curl session.

inputString – (string) - a URL encoded string to be decoded.

outputString – (string) - The variable name to receive the decoded string.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if inputString is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument inputString must be a string”

3.  LabWare checks if outputString is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument outputString must be a string”

4.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs2"

inputString = " http%3A%2F%2Fwww.ex%26ample%24.com "

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyUnescape(curlSessionName, inputString, "outputString")

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

msgbox(outputString)

(The message box will display: http://www.ex&ample$.com)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if unsuccessful and sets lastError with an appropriate error message.


 

 

 

CurlSListAppend

Purpose:

Appends the string value stringValue to the SList named curlSListName on the curl session curlSessionName. If there is no SList named curlSListName, it will first be created before appending the string value stringValue.

NOTE: An SList is an array of string values used by libcurl. These SLists are often passed as options which allow multiple values (e.g. CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER). SLists will remain defined until CurlEasyCleanUp() is called for curlSessionName.


Syntax:

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, curlSListName, stringValue)

curlSessionName - (string) - The name of the curl session.

curlSListName – (string) - The name of the SList.

stringValue – (string) - The value to append to the SList.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if curlSessionName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSessionName must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if curlSListName is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument curlSListName must be a string”

3.  LabWare checks if stringValue is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument stringValue must be a string”

4.  If there is no session named curlSessionName, the following error is returned: “CurlSession with name not defined:”

A Database commit is NOT a part of this function

Example:

curlSessionName = "cs1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set Options – omitted from this example (See CurlEasySetOption above for full example)

 

'** Set SList values

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailTo)

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "recipients", mailCc)

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_MAIL_RCPT",,,"recipients") 

 

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if not successful and sets lastError to an appropriate error message.

 

 

28.3.8    Database Functions
 

Function

SQL

SQLSelect

UpdateIncrementsFromSequences

 

The database functions include the functions used to query the database.  These commands are useful to get information that is not in context.  These are SQL commands and use the standard SQL syntax..  A few things to know about the query string:

 

The query string should be all on one line

The query string must be of the form:

 Select Fieldname1, Fieldname2, … From Tablename where Fieldname = value

(the where clause is optional)

The query string is a string variable and is defined by using double quotation marks "". For example, the following query string will find the name of all analyses with the group name default:

Query1 = "select name from analysis where group_name = 'default'"

Variables can be included in the query string the same way as they are included in a string variable. For example, the following string includes a string variable and requires a slightly different structure:

Query2 = "select owner from Project where name = '" + myProject + "'"

The string variable must have single quotation marks on either side of the variable (no spaces) in the query string the same way as the word default had to have single quotation marks in the previous example.

The following example includes a numeric variable in the query string

Query3 = "select description from sample where sample_number = " + Str(SampNumber)

Notice no single quotation marks are required, and the use of the Str function to convert the number to a string.

 

 

SQL    

Purpose:

The SQL function sends an SQL query to the database and returns the records in an array.

Syntax:

status = SQL(selectQueryString, "arrayName", maxRecords, cacheFlag, translateConcatenationOperator, autoLanguagePrefix)

selectQueryString - The selectQueryString must be an SQL select statement.

arrayName is a two-dimensional array.  If the SQL fails this array will be cleared automatically.

maxRecords is the maximum number of records returned by the query.  If maxRecords is zero or not specified, the maximum number of records returned is 240,000. 

cacheFlag is a Boolean that controls whether the results of the query are cached in memory.  If the results of the query will not change unless there is a change in static data, caching the results may show an improvement in response time.  Caching will only take place for LIMS Basic used in template fields and is especially useful in populating drop down lists.  If the cacheFlag is not specified, the results of the query are not cached.

translateConcatenationOperator –Boolean.  If True, LIMS determines the Concatinator Operator for the current database and replaces the ‘+’ within the SQL statements.  If False, LIMS executes the SQL statement as specified.  Default value is True.

autoLanguagePrefix –  Boolean.  If true, LIMS returns fields configured with the language prefix replacing the specified field.  If false, LIMS returns the specified field.  Default value is False.

Comments:

This function can be used to update user added tables.

This function will support concatenation of fields in the SELECT statement as shown in the following example (You can use the syntax shown below, even if you are using an Oracle database.  The SQL function will translate the syntax to syntax appropriate for the LIMS database for known database types.)

 

query = "SELECT user_name + '  [' + full_name + ']' FROM lims_users "

 

which will return the User Name with the Full Name in square brackets after it. However, if the Full Name is blank, the array will return nil for that user even if the user name has a value.

Example:

myCustomer = "LABWARE"

query = "select description from customer where name = '" + myCustomer + "'"

status = SQL(query, "descriptionArray", 1, "T")

if (not(status)) then

msgbox("Query failed.")

endif

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

Thus function also returns the arrayName populated by the SQL query.

 

SQLSelect    

Purpose:

The SQLSelect function displays a multi-select prompt based on an SQL query and returns the records selected by the user in an array.  There is an optional argument that cam limit the number of records that the user can select.

Syntax:

number = SQLSelect(queryString, titleString, "arrayName", multiSelectFlag, translateConcatenationOperator, maxSelected)

queryString is the SQL query sent to the database.  The query must be an SQL select.  Multiple fields (maximum of 25) can be selected in the query.

titleString – string displayed on the SQL Select dialog. 

arrayName – name of the array that contains the selected value.

multiSelectFlag – Boolean.  Allows the user to select multiple records.  If the user selects the Cancel button, the variable sqlselectcanceled will be true. This parameter is optional.  If not specified, it defaults to True.

translateConcatenationOperator – Boolean.  If translateConcatinationOperator is set to True, +'s in the queryString are converted to the database specific concatenation operators.  The default value for this parameter is True. This parameter is optional.

maxSelected – An integer value which sets the limit for the maximum # of records that can be selected.  This maximum will be displayed along with the number of selected records.  If the user tries to select more than the allowed records, only the records occurring first in the selection up to the maximum will be selected. This parameter is optional.

Comments:

If an NLS translation for a particular column name is available, the translation will be used as the heading for that column in the grid.

If an NLS translation is not available, the system will create a column heading by converting the database column name to mixed case with underscores removed.

This function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the queryString parameter.  If the query is not SELECT or EXECUTE query, the system will trigger the error: “Only Select and Execute queries are allowed”.

•If translateConcatinationOperator is specified the query will be converted otherwise the query is processed as entered.  If the query is successful, the results of the query are displayed in a Query Select Dialog.

Example:

queryString = "select name, description from analysis"

titleString = "Select an analysis:"

number = SQLSelect(queryString, titleString, "analysisArray", "T", , 2)

Returns:

In this example, user would only be able to select 2 of the analysis records in the SQL Select dialog. The following records could be returned in the SQL analysisArray array:

analysisArray[1, 1] = "MOISTURE"

analysisArray[1, 2] = "Moisture analysis"

analysisArray[2, 1] = "PH"

analysisArray[2, 2] = "pH analysis"

 

The Query Select Dialog opened when using the SQLSelect Lims Basic Function can search text in the displayed data. For example, the below Query Select Dialog was called from the SQLSelect function to select a Search Template. The Description field was filtered using the dialog box on the upper right with “sea” in the Description.

Figure 28-1   Query Select Dialog Search Description



 

The description field can be any text, list, Boolean, or number type field within the query and does not have to be defined in TABLE_MASTER.

If multiple fields are defined in the query, all description type fields will be searched on. In the below example, for “decs” any record matching in either the Display Fields OR the Description field will be displayed.

Figure 28-2   Query Select Dialog Search Description – Any Field



 

 

 

UpdateIncrementsFromSequences   

Purpose:

The updateIncrementsFromSequences function allows the LabWare Increments table to be updated with the values of the Oracle Sequences.   This function would be used if the LIMS will no longer be looking to Oracle Sequences for the next increment value and instead will revert to the standard LabWare Increment functionality, such as when making an Access copy of the database.

Syntax:

status = UpdateIncrementsFromSequences(dropSequencesFlag)

dropSequencesFlag – A Boolean value if set to True, the corresponding sequences in Oracle would be deleted after updating the increments.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS checks that the current database type supports sequences.  If this fails, the LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Current database does not support sequences’.

•LIMS updates the increments.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger an error.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example updates the increments and deletes the Oracle Sequences.

status = updateIncrementsFromSequences("T")

Returns:

true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

NOTE:  These Date functions do not perform actions on the database therefore there are no changes to save when these functions are used.

 

CompareDates  

Purpose:

This function evaluates two given dates (either of data type "Date" or "DateTime") with respect to a given comparison operator and returns whether the evaluation is True or False.

Syntax:

evaluation = CompareDates(date1, comparisonOperator, date2, dataType)

date1 – the first given date to be evaluated.

comparisonOperator – the given comparison operator with which to evaluate the dates. Valid comparison operators are:

< 

<=

=

> 

>=

<> 

LT

LTE

EQUAL

GT

GTE

NOTEQUAL

date2 – the second given date with respect to which the first given date is to be evaluated using the comparison operator.

dataType – the data type of the given dates, either "Date" or "DateTime".

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verified if a data type has been specified; if none has been specified, then LIMS takes the data type to be as "DATE".

•If the specified data type is "Date", and the inputs are of mixed type (Date/DateTime)  then LIMS will evaluate based solely upon Date with no error generation.

•If the specified data type is "DateTime", and the inputs are of mixed type (Date/DateTime) then LIMS will assign a time of midnight to the Date input and evaluate based on DateTime with no error generation.

•LIMS verifies if the given comparison operator is a valid one; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "Invalid operator…." along with the operator string.

•LIMS then evaluates if the entered condition comparing the first date against the second date is correct or not; if yes, then LIMS returns True, else False.

Example:

The following example evaluates the first input date "05-21-2011" with respect to the second given date "10-31-2011" using the comparison operator "<=".

evaluation = CompareDates("05-21-2011", "<=", "10-31-2011", "Date") returns: True.

Returns:

If the comparison is correct, this function returns evaluation = true, otherwise false.

 

ComputeAge    

Purpose:

The ComputeAge function returns an age based on the two dates entered.

Syntax:

years = ComputeAge(aDate, asOfDate)

aDate – a date representing a birthdate.

asOfDate – the date used to determine the current age.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

LIMS verifies that the aDate is a valid date.  If it is not a valid date, LIMS returns the error: "Date argument is not a valid date:" followed by the input value.

LIMS verifies that the asOfDate is a valid date.  If it is not a valid date, LIMS will return the error: "As-Of date argument is not a valid date:" followed by the input value.

LIMS calculates the current age and returns the value by populating the variable: years.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example returns the age of a person born on August 5, 1984.

aDate = "08/05/1984"
asOfDate = Date()
years = ComputeAge(aDate, asOfDate)

Returns:

The function returns age in years if it completes successfully.  It returns the value ERROR if an error is triggered.

 

 

CvDate    

Purpose:

The CvDate function converts a string expression to a date data type. 

Syntax:

x = CvDate(string, limsDateFormat)

string – Date string to be converted.

limsDateFormat – Format of the data string to be converted.  Valid date formats are:  "MDY", "YMD", "DMY"

Comments:

dateFormat is "MDY", "YMD", or  "DMY".  Month is the three letter  abbreviation or spelled out. Year format is yy or  yyyy. Month cannot be spelled out in dateFormat "YMD". 

If the limsdateformat is not specified to then the current format defined in the LIMS is used.

NOTE: The LIMS date format is defined by the NLS key for regional settings.  If the regional key is missing or is equal to  ""  then the Windows regional setting where the server is running is used.


Example:

x = CvDate("10 July 1994", "DMY")

y = CvDate("04/14/99", "MDY")

z = CvDate("00/01/01", "YMD")

Returns:

x = 07/10/1994

y = 04/14/1999

z = 01/01/2000

 

CvDateTime    

Purpose:

The CvDateTime function converts a string to a datetime data type.

Syntax:

x = CVDateTime(string)

string – Date/time string to be converted.

Comments:

string format is based on the current LIMS date format.

Example:

x = CvDateTime("04/15/98 11:13:00 AM")

Returns:

x = 04/15/98 11:13:00 AM

 

CvTime    

Purpose:

The CvTime function converts a string to a time data type.

Syntax:

x = CvTime(string)

string – time string to be converted.

Comments:

string format is hh:mm:ss.

Example:

x = CvTime("06:00:00")

Returns:

x = 06:00:00

 

Date    

Purpose:

The Date function returns the current system date.

Syntax:

today = Date()

Comments:

today is a date data type.

Example:

today = Date()   

Returns:

today = 07/22/2000

 

DateAdd    

Purpose:

The DateAdd function adds a number of days to a date.

Syntax:

newDate = DateAdd(startDate, days)

startDate – a date data type or string representations of a date.

days – integer representing the number of days to add to the startDate.

Comments:

 

Example:

newDate = DateAdd("01 JUL 1994", 3)

Returns:

newDate = 07/04/1994

 

DateCentury    

Purpose:

The DateCentury function returns the century number for a specified date.

Syntax:

centuryNumber = DateCentury(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("29 Jun 1999", "DMY")

centuryNumber = DateCentury(aDate)

Returns:

centuryNumber = 19

 

DateDayOfMonth    

Purpose:

The DateDayOfMonth function returns the day of the month index for a  specified date.

Syntax:

index = DateDayOfMonth(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Feb 2000", "DMY")

index = DateDayOfMonth(aDate)

Returns:

index = 1

Purpose:

The DateDayOfWeek function returns the day of the week index for a specified date.

Syntax:

index = DateDayOfWeek(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

The first day of the week is Monday (i.e. index = 1).

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Jan 2000", "DMY")

index = DateDayOfWeek(aDate)

Returns:

index = 6

 

DateDayOfYear    

Purpose:

The DateDayOfYear function returns the day of the year index for a specified date.

Syntax:

index = DateDayOfYear(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Mar 2000", "DMY")

index = DateDayOfYear(aDate)

Returns:

index = 61  (This is also known as the Julian date.)

 

DateDaysInMonth    

Purpose:

The DateDaysInMonth function returns the number of days in the month for a specified date.

Syntax:

dayNumber = DateDaysInMonth(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Feb 2000", "DMY")

dayNumber = DateDaysInMonth(aDate)

Returns:

dayNumber = 29

 

DateDaysInYear    

Purpose:

The DateDaysInYear function returns the number of days in a year for a specified date.

Syntax:

dayNumber = DateDaysInYear(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Feb 2000", "DMY")

dayNumber = DateDaysInYear(aDate)

Returns:

dayNumber = 366

 

DateDifference    

Purpose:

The DateDifference function returns the difference in days between two dates.

Syntax:

days = DateDifference(firstDate, secondDate)

firstDate - subtracted from secondDate; date data types or string representations of dates

secondDate - date data types or string representations of dates.

Comments:

Negative days may be returned.

Example:

days = DateDifference("02 JUL 1997", "06 JUL 1997")

Returns:

days = 4

Purpose:

The DateMonthOfYear function returns the month of the year index for a specified data.

Syntax:

index = DateMonthOfYear(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

 

aDate = CvDate("01 Mar 2000", "DMY")

index = DateMonthOfYear(aDate)

Returns:

index = 3

 

DateTime    

Purpose:

The DateTime function returns the current system date and time.

Syntax:

now = DateTime()

Example:

now = DateTime()    

Returns:

now = 02/24/2000 03:50:02 PM

 

DateTimeAdd    

Purpose:

The DateTimeAdd function adds a number of seconds to a date and time.

Syntax:

newDateTime = DateTimeAdd(startDateTime, seconds)

startDateTime - a datetime data type or a string representation of a datetime such as mm/dd/yy hh:mm:ss. 

seconds - may be a positive or negative number.

Comments:

 

Example:

aDateTime = CvDateTime("04/15/98 11:00:00 AM")

newDateTime = DateTimeAdd(aDateTime, 180)

Returns: 

newDateTime = 04/15/98 11:03:00 AM

 

DateTimeDifference    

Purpose:

The DateTimeDifference function returns the difference in seconds between two date and times.

Syntax:

seconds = DateTimeDifference(firstDateTime, secondDateTime)

firstDateTime - subtracted from secondDateTime; datetime data types or string representations of a datetime such as mm/dd/yy hh:mm:ss.

secondDateTime - datetime data types or string representations of a datetime such as mm/dd/yy hh:mm:ss.

Comments:

Negative seconds may be returned. 

Example:

seconds = DateTimeDifference("04/15/98 11:00:00 AM", "04/15/98 11:03:00 AM")

Returns:

seconds = 180

 

DateTimeFromSeconds    

Purpose:

The DateTimeFromSeconds function returns a datetime variable that represents the date and time as of the specified number of seconds since January 1, 1901.

Syntax:

dateTm = DateTimeFromSeconds(anInteger)

anInteger – the number of seconds that elapsed since January 1, 1901.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the passed argument anInteger cannot be interpreted as an integer, LIMS will trigger the error "Argument is not a valid seconds".

•LIMS converts the number of seconds to a datetime variable.  LIMS takes into account leap years but not leap seconds.  In other words, a leap year is assumed to contain exactly 366 * 24 * 60 * 60 seconds while any other year is assumed to contain exactly 365 * 24 * 60 * 60 seconds.

•LIMS returns the result.

Example:

The following example shows the datetime value 02/09/2004 06:59:04 AM in a message box.  (The format of the displayed string depends on the local date and time format settings.)

dateTm = DateTimeFromSeconds(3253762744)
Msgbox( dateTm )

Returns:

The function returns a datetime variable if it completes successfully.  Otherwise it returns the string "ERROR".

 

DateTimeHours    

Purpose:

The DateTimeHours function returns the hour for a specified date and time.

Syntax:

hoursNumber = DateTimeHours(aDateTime)

aDateTime – a date time data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

aDateTime = CvDateTime("02/14/2000 04:15:30 AM")

hoursNumber = DateTimeHours(aDateTime)

Returns:

hoursNumber = 4

 

DateTimeMinutes    

Purpose:

The DateTimeMinutes function returns the minutes for a specified date and time.

Syntax:

minutesNumber = DateTimeMinutes(aDateTime)

aDateTime – a date time data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

aDateTime = CvDateTime("02/14/2000 04:15:30 AM")

minutesNumber = DateTimeMinutes(aDateTime)

Returns:

minutesNumber = 14

 

DateTimeSeconds    

Purpose:

The DateTimeSeconds function returns the seconds for a specified date and time.

Syntax:

secondsNumber = DateTimeSeconds(aDateTime)

aDateTime – a date time data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

aDateTime = CvDateTime("02/14/2000 04:15:30 AM")

secondsNumber = DateTimeSeconds(aDateTime)

Returns:

secondsNumber = 30

 

DateWeekOfYear    

Purpose:

The DateWeekOfYear function returns the week number of a given date.  The week number is 0 for January 1 to 6,  the week number is 1 for January 7 to 13; and so on.

Syntax:

weekNumber = DateWeekOfYear(aDate)

aDate – the date whose week number is to be returned

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified value is not a valid date or a valid date-time, LIMS will trigger the error "Date argument is not a valid date or datetime" and return the value 0.

•LIMS returns the week number as specified above.

Example:

If today’s date is January 27, 2009, the following example will return the number 3.

x = Date()
y = DateWeekOfYear(x)

NOTE:  This function calculates the week of the year by dividing the day of the year by 7 and dropping the remainder.


Returns:

The function returns an integer value.

 

DateYear    

Purpose:

The DateYear function returns the year for a specified date.

Syntax:

yearNumber = DateYear(aDate)

aDate – a date data type.

Comments:

 

Example:

aDate = CvDate("02/14/00", "MDY")

yearNumber = DateYear(aDate)

Returns:

yearNumber = 2000

 

DaylightSavingsOffset

Purpose:

Checks the Daylight Savings time offset for an entered Date/Time and returns the value in seconds if Daylight Savings is in effect.  If it is not it will return 0.

Syntax:

seconds = DaylightSavingsOffset(DSTName, aDateTime)  

DSTName - The name of the Daylight Savings Time record to check the data against

aDateTime – The DateTime to check the Daylight savings time offset

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LabWare checks if the DSTName is valid Daylight Savings Time Record from the DAYLIGHT_SAVINGS table.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error "Invalid rec name: DSTName ".

•LabWare checks if the aDateTime is a valid Date Time.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error "Argument is not a valid date time: aDateTime".

•The system will evaluate the date to determine if Daylight savings is in effect.  If it is in effect then the system will return the time offset from the Daylight saving record in seconds.

Example:

 

DSTName = "US"

StrDateTime1 = "02/01/2012 1:30 AM"

aDateTime1  = CVDateTime(StrDateTime1)

Seconds1 = DaylightSavingsOffset(DSTName, aDateTime1)

 

StrDateTime2 = "05/01/2012 1:30 AM"

aDateTime2  = CVDateTime(StrDateTime2)

Seconds2 = DaylightSavingsOffset(DSTName, aDateTime2)

 

Seconds1 will return ‘0’ as Daylight Savings Time was not in effect March 1st, 2012

Seconds2 will return ‘3600’ as Daylight Savings Time was in effect May 1st, 2012

 

Returns:

 

Returns ‘0’ if Daylight Savings time was not in effect and the daylight savings time offset in the number of seconds, if it was. If there was an error, it returns ERROR with LastError containing a description of the error.

 

DayOfWeek    

Purpose:

The DayOfWeek function returns the day of the week for a specified date.

Syntax:

dayName = DayOfWeek(aDate)

aDate – a date data type or a string representation of a date.

Comments:

 

Example:

dayName = DayOfWeek("15 APR 1998")

Returns:

dayName = Wed.

 

GetSeasons  

Purpose:

This function evaluates a given date and returns an array of seasons (defined in the LIMS "SEASON" table) the date qualifies for.

Syntax:

status = GetSeasons(aDate, "seasonsNameArray")

aDate – the date to be evaluated in valid dae  format.

seasonsNameArray – the name of the output array containing the names of the seasons the date qualifies for.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies if the given date is of a valid date data type; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with: "An invalid date was specified: " along with the specified date.

•LIMS verifies if an output array name has been specified to return the season names; if not, then LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with:  "No seasons array name specified".

•LIMS then reads the database records defined in the Seasons table; if records exist, then LIMS checks if the given date falls within any of the defined seasons. If any such season records are found, then LIMS populates the output array name with the names of the defined seasons.

Example:

The following example evaluates the input date "12-31-2011" with respect to the defined seasons.

aDate = CvDate("12-31-2011", "MDY")

status = GetSeasons(aDate, "SeasonArray") returns:

 

SeasonArray[1] = "WINTER"

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully, else, it returns false if an error is triggered.

 

Interval    

Purpose:

The Interval function returns the specified string if it is an interval.

Syntax:

x = Interval(string)

string – text string to be tested to determine if it is an interval or not.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the string is a valid interval.  If it is not a valid interval, LIMS returns the error: "Error in interval format:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS sets the output variable: x to the tested string.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example returns the input string as a valid interval.

String = "11:06:23"
x = Interval(string)

The following example returns ERROR since the input string is not a valid interval.

String = "11:06:83"
x = Interval(string)

Returns:

The function returns the input string if it is an interval.  The function returns ERROR if the input string is not an interval

 

IntervalSeconds    

Purpose:

The Intervalseconds returns the number of seconds within the specified interval.

Syntax:

secondsNumber = IntervalSeconds(aDeltaTime)

aDeltaTime – interval of time to calculate number of seconds.  This value needs to be formatted as an interval.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the input value is a valid interval.  If it is not a valid interval, LIMS returns the error: "Error in interval format:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS sets the output variable: secondsNumber to the number of seconds within the specified interval.

Example:

The following example returns the number of seconds in 1 day, 13 hours, 56 minutes and 7 seconds.

aDeltaTime = Interval("1 13:56:07")
secondsNumber = IntervalSeconds(aDeltaTime)

Returns:

The function returns the number of seconds if it completes successfully.  It returns the value ERROR if an error is triggered.

 

IsDate    

Purpose:

The IsDate function returns a Boolean indicating whether a string can be converted to a date.

Syntax:

status = IsDate(string)

Comments:

The string date format is mmm dd yyyy.  The month can be the three letter abbreviation or spelled out.  The year format is yy or yyyy.

Example:

status = IsDate("01/02/2000") 

Returns:

status = true if the string can be converted, false otherwise.

 

IsHoliday    

Purpose:

The IsHoliday function returns a Boolean indicating whether a date is a holiday.

Syntax:

status = IsHoliday(aDate)

aDate is a date data type or a string representation of a date.

Comments: 

aDate is checked against holidays setup in the Holiday Scheduler.

Example:

aDate = CvDate("01/01/2000" , "MDY")

status = IsHoliday(aDate) 

Returns:

status = true if the date is a holiday, false otherwise.

 

IsInterval    

Purpose:

The IsInterval function returns a Boolean indicating whether a string can be  converted to an interval.

Syntax:

status = IsInterval(string)

Comments:

The format of an interval is ddd hh:mm:ss.

Example:

status = IsInterval("0 06:00:00") 

Returns:

status = true if the string can be converted, false otherwise.

 

IsTime    

Purpose:

The IsTime function returns a Boolean indicating whether a string can be converted to a time data type.

Syntax:

status = IsTime(string)

Comments:

The time format is hh:mm:ss.

Example:

status = IsTime("00:30:00") 

Returns:

status = true if the string can be converted, false otherwise.

 

NextWorkDate    

Purpose:

The NextWorkDate function returns the next work date based on a starting date, number of days, non-work days and holidays. The function allows a different Holiday Schedule than the Holiday Schedule associated with the currently logged user to be specified.

Syntax:

nextDate = NextWorkDate(startDate, numDays, nonWorkDaysInWeekArray, workHolidaysFlag, holidaySchedule)

startDate – parameter must be a date.

numDays – integer defining the number of work days to look ahead.

nonWorkDaysInArray – One dimensional array defining the days of the week that are not workdays.  (Monday (1), Tuesday (2), Wednesday (3), Thursday (4), Friday (5), Saturday (6), and Sunday (7))

workHolidaysFlag – Boolean defining if holidays are considered workdays.  If set to false, the Holiday Scheduler is checked for holidays.

holidaySchedule – text entry defining the Holiday Schedule to use when calculating the next work date.  This parameter is optional, if null, the system uses the Holiday Schedule configured for the current User Account.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS evaluates the startDate.  If a date is not provided, the system sets the lastError with: "start date argument is not a valid datetime:" followed by the input.

•LIMS evaluates the workHolidaysFlag.  If not specified, the system defaults the parameter to false.

•LIMS calculates the next work date based on the parameters.

Example:

aDate = CvDate("12/31/1999", "MDY")
startDate = aDate
numDays = 7
nonWorkDaysInArray[1] = 7 ' Sunday
nonWorkDaysInArray[2] = 1 ' Monday
workHolidaysFlag = "T"

holidaySchedule = ""    (Defaults to User’s holiday schedule when null.)

nextDate = NextWorkDate(startDate, numDays, nonWorkDaysInArray, workHolidaysFlag)

Returns:

nextDate = 01/11/2000

 

 

Time    

Purpose:

The Time function returns the current system time.

Syntax:

now = Time()

Comments:

now is a time data type.

Example:

now = Time()   

Returns:

now = 08:05:00 AM

 

 

UTCDateTime   

Purpose:

The UTCDateTime returns the current date and time in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).  For LIMS purposes, UTC is the same as GMT.

Syntax:

now = UTCDateTime()

Comments:

LIMS obtains the current UTC date and time from the operating system that runs the LIMS application.

Example:

now = UTCDateTime()

Returns:

The current system time as a datetime value.

 

UTCOffset   

Purpose:

The UTCOffset function returns the difference between a given local date and time and the corresponding UTC (GMT) time.

Syntax:

seconds = UTCOffset(datetime)

datetime –local date and time to be evaluated

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

If a datetime is specified, and the value does not represent a valid datetime value, LIMS will trigger the error "Datetime argument is not a valid datetime.

If no datetime is specified, LIMS will use the current system date and time, expressed as local time.

LIMS determines the GMT offset of the current user.  If no GMT offset is defined, LIMS will trigger the error "Time offset problem: no GMT offset defined".

LIMS determines the daylight savings offset that was, or is, or will be, applicable on the specified datetime.  If no daylight savings offset is defined for the specified datetime, LIMS will use the value zero.

LIMS returns the sum of the GMT offset (converted to seconds) and the daylight savings offset.

Example:

The following example returns the current difference between local time and UTC time.

seconds = UTCOffset()

The following example returns the difference between local time and UTC time that was applicable when the sample in context was released.

releasedOn = SELECT sample.released_on

seconds = UTCOffset(releasedOn)

Returns:

The function returns the UTCOffset in seconds. It returns the value ERROR if an error is triggered.

 

WorkDaysBetweenDates    

Purpose:

The WorkDaysBetweenDates function returns the number of workdays there are between 2 dates.  The function allows a different Holiday Schedule than the Holiday Schedule associated with the currently logged user to be specified.

Syntax:

numDays = WorkDaysBetweenDates(startDate,endDate, nonWorkDaysInWeekArray, workHolidaysFlag, holidaySchedule)

startDate – date value defining the beginning of the evaluation period.

endDate – date value defining the end of the evaluation period.

nonWorkDaysInWeekArray – one dimensional array containing integer values that represent days of the week that are not workdays.  (1-Monday, 2-Tuesday, 3-Wednesday, 4-Thrusday, 5-Friday, 6-Saturday, 7-Sunday)

workHolidaysFlag – Boolean.  T, true, holidays are considered workdays.  F, false, holidays are not considers workdays and the Holiday Scheduler is considered.

holidaySchedule – text entry defining the Holiday Schedule to use when calculating the next work date.  This parameter is optional, if null, the system uses the Holiday Schedule configured for the User Account.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS evaluates the stateDate argument.  If a date is not provided, the system sets the lastError with: "start date argument is not a valid date:" followed by the input.

•LIMS evaluates the endDate.  If a date is not provided, the system sets the lastError with: "end date argument is not a valid date:" followed by the input.

•LIMS evaluates the workHolidaysFlag.  If not specified, the system defaults the parameter to false.

•LIMS calculates the number of work dates based on the parameters.

Example:

startDate = CvDate("12/31/1999", "MDY")
endDate = CvDate("01/07/2000", "MDY")
nonWorkDaysInWeekArray[1] = 7 'Sun
nonWorkDaysInWeekArray[2] =1 'Mon
workHolidaysFlag = "T"

holidaySchedule = "COUNTRY_A"   (If null, defaults to the holiday schedule configured on the User Account)

numDays = WorkDaysBetweenDates(startDate, endDate,  nonWorkDaysInWeekArray, workHolidaysFlag)    

Returns:

numDays = 5

 

 

28.3.10  DDE Functions
 

Function

DDEExecute

DDEPoke

DDERequest

 

DDEExecute    

Purpose:

The DDEExecute function opens a Dynamic Data Exchange link to an application and executes a command.  The link is then closed.

Syntax:

status = DDEExecute (application, topic, commandString, optionalTimeOutInSeconds)

Comments:

The application must be running. 

Example:

‘This example closes an open spreadsheet.

CommandString = "[Close(FALSE,FALSE)]"

status = DDEExecute("Excel", "System", commandString)

Returns:

status =  true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DDEPoke    

Purpose:

The DDEPoke function opens a Dynamic Data Exchange link to an

 

application and pokes an item.  The link is then closed.

Syntax:

status = DDEPoke (application, topic, item, value)

Comments:

The application must be running. 

Example:

‘This example inserts a value into an Excel spreadsheet.

Application = "Excel"

topic = "Sheet1"

item = "R1C1"

value = 50

status = DDEPoke(application, topic, item, value)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DDERequest    

Purpose:

The DDERequest function opens a Dynamic Data Exchange link to an  application and requests an item.  The link is then closed.

Syntax:

value = DDERequest (application, topic, item)

Comments:

The application must be running. 

Example:

‘This example requests a value from an Excel spreadsheet

application = "Excel"

topic = "Sheet1"

item = "R1C1" 

value = DDERequest(application, topic, item)

Returns:

value = 50

 

 

 

28.3.11  Debug Tools Functions
 

Function

Breakpoint

CodeSearch

CreateLIMSLog

CreateSchema

CreateSupportLog

DebuggingSet

DebugMessage

DebugOff

DebugOn

GetDiagnosticsMode

hasDebuggingPoints

LIMSLogClose

LoadDebuggingSet

RebuildNLS
