Purpose:

Unloads any currently loaded debugging points.

Syntax:

status = UnloadDebuggingSet()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks if there are any currently loaded debugging points.  If there are no points loaded, LIMS will trigger the error "There were no debugging points to unload"

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following statement unloads currently loaded debugging points.

status = UnloadDebuggingSet()

 

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.12  DLL Functions
 

Function

DLL

DLLCall

DLLClose

DLLCreateStructure

DLLDefineStructure

DLLOpen

DLLReadStructure

DLLReleaseStructure

DLLStructure

DLLWriteStructure

 

DLL    

Purpose:

The DLL function executes calls to external 32bit Dynamic Link Libraries (using the Pascal calling convention for argument passing).

Syntax:

x = DLL(DLLName, procedureName, argumentsArray, argumentTypesArray, returnType)

DLLName is the name of the DLL file (no path is required so long as the file is in one of the following directories: Windows or Windows\System or LIMS working directory)

procedureName is the name of the function being called from the DLL (the name is case-sensitive an must exactly match the name in the DLL)

argumentsArray is an array containing the arguments being passed to the function,

argumentTypesArray is an array containing the data types of the arguments that the function is expecting to receive.  The valid argument types are "short", "ushort", "long", "ulong", "double", "handle", "boolean", and "struct" (these are case-sensitive)

returnType is the data type of the value returned by the function. The return type can be "short", "ulongReturn", "boolean" or "none" (these are case-sensitive).

Comments:

The number of arguments and argument types must match.

The DLL is opened and closed with each call.

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Example:

‘This example returns the windows version number

dllName = "Kernel32"

proc = "GetVersion"

returns = "ulongReturn"

ver = DLL(dllName, proc, , , returns)

major = BitAnd(LowWord(ver), 255)

minor = BitShift(LowWord(ver), -8)

return (Str(major) + "." + Str(minor))

Returns:

ver = 5.0   (for Windows 2000)

 

DLLCall    

Purpose:

The DLLCall function will call the DLL defined in the LIMS Basic DLLOpen function (using the Pascal calling convention for argument passing).

Syntax:

status = DLLCall(DLLHandleName, procedureName, argumentsArray,  argumentTypesArray, returnType)

DLLHandleName is the DLL name defined in the DLLOpen function.

procedureName is the name of the function being called from the DLL (the name is case-sensitive an must exactly match the name in the DLL)

argumentsArray is an array containing the arguments being passed to the function,

argumentTypesArray is an array containing the data types of the arguments that the function is expecting to receive.  The valid argument types are "short", "ushort", "long", "ulong", "double", "handle", "boolean", and "struct" (these are case-sensitive)

returnType is the data type of the value returned by the function. The return type can be "short", "ulongReturn", "boolean" or "none" (these are case-sensitive).

Comments:

The number of arguments and argument types must match.

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DLLClose    

Purpose:

The DLLClose function closes the DLL opened in the LIMS Basic DLLOpen function.

Syntax:

status = DLLClose(DLLHandleName)

DLLHandleName is the DLL name defined in the DLLOpen function.

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DLLCreateStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLCreateStructure function creates a DLL structure using the structure definiton created by the LIMS Basic DLLDefineStructure function

Syntax:

status = DLLCreateStructure(structHandleName, structName)

structHandleName is the name being assigned to the structure so that it can be called by other LIMS Basic DLL functions

structName is the name of the structure defintion created in the DLLDefineStructure function

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DLLDefineStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLDefineStructure function creates a structure definition that can be used to create a DLL structure using the LIMS Basic DLLCreateStructure function.

Syntax:

status = DLLDefineStructure(structName, fieldNameArray, fieldTypeArray, offsetArray, fieldSizeArray)

structName is the name of the structure defintion being created by this DLLDefineStructure function

fieldNameArray is an array containing the names of the fields that will be contatined in this structure,

fieldTypeArray is an array containing the data types of the fields,

offsetArray is an array containing the memory offset for each field,

fieldSizeArray is an array containing the field length for each field.

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DLLOpen    

Purpose:

The DLLOpen function is used to open an external 32bit Dynamic Link Library(DLL).

Syntax:

status = DLLOpen(DLLHandleName, fileName)

DLLHandleName is the name being assigned to the DLL so that it can be called by other LIMS Basic DLL functions.

fileName is the file name of the Dynamic Link Library (no path is required so long as the file is in one of the following directories: Windows,  Windows\System or LIMS working directory)

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DLLReadStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLReadStructure function reads data from the specified field in the stucture defined in the LIMS Basic DLLCreateStructure function.

Syntax:

fieldValue = DLLReadStructure(structHandleName, fieldName)

structHandleName is the structure name defined in the DLLCreateStructure function

FieldName is the name of the field as defined in the DLLDefineStructure function

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

fieldValue = value of the field

 

DLLReleaseStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLReleaseStructure function releases the stucture defined in the LIMS Basic DLLCreateStructure function from memory.

Syntax:

status = DLLReleaseStructure(structHandleName)

structHandleName is the structure name defined in the DLLCreateStructure function

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise

 

DLLStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLStructure function returns the address of the DLL structure in memory.

Syntax:

structurepointer = DLLStructure(structHandleName)

structHandleName is the structure name defined in the DLLCreateStructure function

Comments:

NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

structurepointer = the memory address of the structure

 

DLLWriteStructure    

Purpose:

The DLLWriteStructure function writes data to the stucture defined in the LIMS Basic DLLCreateStructure function.

Syntax:

status = DLLWriteStructure(structHandleName, fieldname, fieldValue)

structHandleName is the structure name defined in the DLLCreateStructure function

FieldName is the name of the field as defined in the DLLDefineStructure function

Fieldvalue is the value of the field stored in the structure

Comments:

 NOTE:  Because of the complexity of the DLL LIMS Basic functions, it is recommended that they be used in consultation with LabWare.


Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

28.3.13  Dot Net Functions
 

Function

DnCreateObject

DnGetProperty

DnInvokeMethod

DnInvokeStaticMethod

DnLoadAssembly

DnLoadAssemblyFileName

DnLoadAssemblyFullName

DnSetProperty

 

DnCreateObject  

Purpose:

The DnCreateObject function is used to create and load the object name for subsequent commands.

Syntax:

dotNetObject = DnCreateObject(dotNetTypeName, argValue)

dotNetTypeName – the name of the dot net type that will be referenced

argValue – this can be empty, a single value, even an array of values with some of the array elements being arrays.  It is determined by the Dot Net Assembly being called so knowledge of the Dot Net structure is required.

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example creates and loads an object from the ECMAPI Assembly:

sConnection = DnCreateObject("Agilent.ECMAPI.ECMConnection")

Returns:

 

The function sets the dotNetObject value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnGetProperty

Purpose:

The DnGetProperty function is used to get the value for the specified property of the dot net object.

Syntax:

value = DnGetProperty(dotNetObject, propertyName, argValue)

dotNetObject – the object name retrieved by the DnCreateObject function

propertyName – the name of the property being retrieved from the Dot Net Assembly

argValue – this can be empty, a single value, even an array of values with some of the array elements being arrays.  It is determined by the Dot Net Assembly being called so knowledge of the Dot Net structure is required

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example checks that there is a connection.

value = DnGetProperty(sConnection, "Connected")

Returns:

 

The function returns the value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnInvokeMethod  

Purpose:

The DnInvokeMethod function is used to invoke the specified method specified from the Dot Net Object.

Syntax:

value = DnInvokeMethod(dotNetObject, methodName, argValue)

dotNetObject – the object name retrieved by the DnCreateObject function

methodName – the method name from the Dot Net Assembly

argValue –the parameters required by the dot net method being called.  It can be empty, a single value or a 1-D array.          

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example invokes a method that prompts the user for a folder:

value = DnInvokeMethod(ecmObject, "FolderPickerDialog", 3)

Returns:

 

The function may or may not return a value if it completes successfully, depending on the Dot Net method.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnInvokeStaticMethod

Purpose:

The DnInvokeStaticMethod function is used to invoke the static method specified by the given method name.

Syntax:

value = DnInvokeStaticMethod(dotNetTypeName, methodName, argValue)

dotNetTypeName – the Type from the Dot Net Assembly

methodName – the method name from the Dot Net Assembly

argValue –the parameters required by the dot net method being called.  It can be empty, a single value or a 1-D array.

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example

status = DnLoadAssembly("mscorlib")

value = DnInvokeStaticMethod("System.DateTime", "get_Now")

Returns:

 

The function may or may not return a value if it completes successfully, depending on the Dot Net method.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnLoadAssembly

Purpose:

The DnLoadAssembly function is used to load the Dot Assembly.

Syntax:

status = DnLoadAssembly(assemblyName)

assemblyName – the name of the Dot Net Assembly being called (usually this is the name of the dll or exe file without the file extension).

Comments:

This function requires that the assembly be in the global cache or the current directory.  No version information is required.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example loads the assembly ECMAPI:

status = DnLoadAssembly("ECMAPI")

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnLoadAssemblyFileName  

Purpose:

The DnLoadAssemblyFileName function is used to load the Dot Assembly from a file.

Syntax:

status = DnLoadAssemblyFileName(fileName)

fileName – the file and path name of the Dot Net Assembly being called.

Comments:

This function allows the use of the assemblies that are not in the global cache or the current directory.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example

filename =  "C:\Files\Barcode.dll"

status = DnLoadAssemblyFileName(fileName)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

DnLoadAssemblyFullName

Purpose:

The DnLoadAssemblyFullName function is used to load the Dot Assembly.

Syntax:

status = DnLoadAssemblyFullName(fullName)

fullName – the full name of the Dot Net Assembly being called (must include version, etc.)

Comments:

This function allows the use of the assemblies that are not in the global cache or the current directory.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example

fullName = "System.Speech, Version=3.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=31bf3856ad364e35"

status = DnLoadAssemblyFullName(fullName)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DnSetProperty  

Purpose:

The DnSetProperty function is used to set the value of the specified property of the specified dotNetObject to an argValue.

Syntax:

value = DnSetProperty(dotNetObject, propertyName, argValue)

dotNetObject – the object name retrieved by the DnCreateObject function

propertyName – the name of the property being set in the Dot Net Assembly

argValue – this can be empty, a single value, even an array of values with some of the array elements being arrays.  It is determined by the Dot Net Assembly being called so knowledge of the Dot Net structure is required

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

The following example sets the property ECMInstance:

Status = DnLoadAssembly("ECMAPI")

sConnection = DnCreateObject("Agilent.ECMAPI.ECMConnection")

sEcmObject = DnCreateObject("Agilent.ECMAPI.ECM", sConnection)

DnSetProperty(sConnection, "ECMInstance", sEcmObject)

Returns:

 

The function returns no value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.14  Excel Functions
 

Function

ExcelClose

ExcelGetArray

ExcelGetValue

ExcelQuit

ExcelSave

ExcelSetArray

ExcelSetValue

 

LIMS Basic includes these functions to implement a simplified OLE Automation interface specifically for Microsoft Excel. Their only purpose is to reduce the number of lines of LIMS Basic script that is required for common Excel interactions. Anything that can be done via these new functions can also be done via the OLE Automation functions.

 

28.3.14.1   Excel Automation Example 1
Below is an example LIMS Basic script that exercises the ExcelGetValue and ExcelSetValue functions.

 

' Example of Microsoft Excel Automation, based on OLE Automation

 

fileName = "C:\ExcelExample.xls"

 

' Read the value of cell B1 and display it

' Expected result is 2

value = ExcelGetValue(fileName, "Sheet1", "B1")

Msgbox(Str(value))

 

' Write the value into cell B5

status = ExcelSetValue(fileName, "Sheet1", "B5", value)

EndIf

 

Breakpoint("Verify the contents of the variables and the spreadsheet")

 

' Clean up

status = ExcelClose(fileName)

status = ExcelQuit()

Return

 

28.3.14.2   Excel Automation Example 2
Below is an example LIMS Basic script that exercises the ExcelGetArray and ExcelSetArray functions.

 

' Example of Microsoft Excel Automation, based on OLE Automation

 

fileName = "C:\ExcelExample.xls"

 

' Read the values of two rows and two columns starting at cell A1

status = ExcelGetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A1", "arrayA", , 2, 2)

 

' Read the values of two rows and all columns starting at cell A1

status = ExcelGetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A1", "arrayB", , 2, )

 

' Read the values of all rows and two columns starting at cell A1

' Transpose the array after reading

status = ExcelGetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A1", "arrayC", "T", , 2)

 

' Read the values of all rows and all columns starting at cell B2

' Transpose the array after reading

status = ExcelGetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "B2", "arrayD", "T", , )

 

' Write the values of arrayA starting at cell A5

status = ExcelSetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A5", arrayA, )

 

' Write the values of arrayB starting at cell A8

' Transpose the array before writing

status = ExcelSetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A8", arrayB, "T")

 

' Write the values of arrayC starting at cell A12

status = ExcelSetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A12", arrayC, )

 

' Write the values of arrayD starting at cell A15

' Transpose the array before writing

status = ExcelSetArray(fileName, "Sheet1", "A15", arrayD, "T")

 

Breakpoint("Verify the contents of the arrays and the spreadsheet")

 

' Clean up

status = ExcelClose(fileName)

status = ExcelQuit()

Return

 

ExcelClose  

Purpose:

The ExcelClose function closes a specified workbook or it closes all open workbooks.

CAUTION: The ExcelClose function will not work if the Excel workbook has been opened using an OLE method call such as status = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open","c:\timesheet.xls").


Syntax:

status = ExcelClose(fileName)

fileName – optional; the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If a file name is specified, LIMS will close the file that has the specified name.  If the file was not open, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook is not open".

•Otherwise, if no file name is specified, LIMS will close all workbooks that are open in the current instance of Microsoft Excel.

•LIMS returns the value true.

The current instance of the Excel application remains open.  To close it, use the ExcelQuit function.

Example:

The following example closes the open file named ExcelExample.xls.

status = ExcelClose("C:\ExcelExample.xls")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully. It returns the value false if an error is triggered.   In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

ExcelGetArray  

Purpose:

The ExcelGetArray function retrieves the values of a range of cells in a given spreadsheet and stores them in a LIMS Basic array

Syntax:

status = ExcelGetArray(fileName, sheet, cell, "arrayname", transpose, rows, columns)

fileName –the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder

sheet – the name of the sheet from which values are to be obtained (case-sensitive)

cell – the address of the first cell in the range whose values are to be obtained

arrayname – the name of the LIMS Basic array in which the values are to be stored

transpose – the value true (or "T") if the range is to be transposed before the values are written into the LIMS Basic array.  If not specified, LIMS will use the value false.

rows – the number of rows to be read.  If not specified, or if the argument is not a positive integer, LIMS will keep reading until a blank value is encountered in the first column.

columns – the number of columns to be read.  If not specified, or if the argument is not a positive integer, LIMS will keep reading until a blank value is encountered in the first row.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified file name is not an integer, LIMS will open the specified file.  If the file does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook not found".

•LIMS navigates to the specified worksheet.  If no sheet with the specified name exists in the workbook, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook has no sheet named".

•LIMS navigates to the specified range.

•LIMS obtains the values of the cells in the range.

•If the value of the transpose argument is true, LIMS will transpose the retrieved values, i.e., switch rows and columns.

•LIMS stores the values in a new LIMS Basic array with the specified array name.  If the values were not transposed, and if the number of retrieved columns is 1, the array will be a one-dimensional array.  If the values were transposed, and if the number of retrieved rows is 1, the array will be a one-dimensional array.  In all other cases, the array will be a two-dimensional array.

•LIMS returns the value true.

The spreadsheet and the Excel application remain open.  To close the spreadsheet, use the ExcelClose function.  To close the application, use the ExcelQuit function.

Example:

The following example reads two rows and two columns starting at cell A1 in sheet "Sheet1" in the spreadsheet with file name ExcelExample.xls.  The values are stored in the LIMS Basic array named arrayA.

status = ExcelGetArray("C:\ExcelExample.xls", "Sheet1", "A1", "arrayA", , 2, 2)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully. It returns the value false if an error is triggered.   In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

ExcelGetValue  

Purpose:

The ExcelGetValue function returns the value of a given cell in a given spreadsheet..

Syntax:

value = ExcelGetValue(fileName, sheet, cell)

fileName –the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder

sheet – the name of the sheet from which the value is to be obtained (case-sensitive)

cell – the address of the cell whose value is to be obtained

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified file name is not an integer, LIMS will open the specified file.  If the file does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook not found".

•LIMS navigates to the specified worksheet.  If no sheet with the specified name exists in the workbook, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook has no sheet named".

•LIMS navigates to the specified cell.

•LIMS obtains the value of the specified cell.

•LIMS returns the value.

The spreadsheet and the Excel application remain open.  To close the spreadsheet, use the ExcelClose function.  To close the application, use the ExcelQuit function.

Example:

The following example obtains the value of cell B1 in sheet "Sheet1" in the spreadsheet with file name ExcelExample.xls.

value = ExcelGetValue("C:\ExcelExample.xls", "Sheet1", "B1")

Returns:

The function returns the value of the specified cell if it completes successfully. It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.   In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value "ERROR" is returned.

 

ExcelQuit  

Purpose:

The ExcelQuit function closes the current instance of the Microsoft Excel application.  Other instances of Excel, i.e., those opened by the user outside of LIMS, are not affected.

CAUTION: The ExcelQuit function will not work if the Excel workbook has been opened using an OLE method call such as status = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open","c:\timesheet.xls").


Syntax:

status = ExcelQuit()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS closes the current instance of Microsoft Excel.

•LIMS returns the value true.

Example:

The following example closes Microsoft Excel.

status = ExcelQuit()

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

ExcelSave  

Purpose:

The ExcelSave function saves the specified spreadsheet. If a new file name is specified, it saves the spreadsheet with the new name.

CAUTION: The ExcelSave function will not work if the Excel workbook has been opened using an OLE method call such as status = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open","c:\timesheet.xls").


Syntax:

status = ExcelSave(fileName, newFileName)

fileName –the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder

newFileName – the new name of the spreadsheet in which the Excel workbook will be saved.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS trims any blanks off the file name of the specified Excel spreadsheet.

•LIMS checks if a new filename has been specified; if yes, it trims blanks off the same.

•LIMS saves the spreadsheet as a Microsoft Excel file; if a new file name has been specified, it saves the spreadsheet with the new file name.

 

The ExcelSave function does not work if the Excel spreadsheet has been opened using

Example:

The following example saves the Microsoft Excel file "ExcelExample.xls" with the new file name "ExcelExampleNew.xls".

status = ExcelSave("C:\ExcelExample.xls", "C:\ExcelExampleNew.xls")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

ExcelSetArray  

Purpose:

The ExcelSetArray function places the contents of a LIMS Basic array into a given range of cells in a given spreadsheet.

CAUTION: When setting values, the OLE interface converts values to Floating Point values.  As a result, some precision may be lost and the value set in Excel may not be the exact value passed to the function.  For example a value of 0.99999 may be set as 0.999989986419677. 

Also very large Integer values will trigger an error in Excel and will cause the function to fail.


Syntax:

status = ExcelSetArray(fileName, sheet, cell, array, transpose, skipEmpty)

fileName – one of the following: (a) the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder; or (b), an OLE Automation handle that refers to a Workbook object.

sheet – the name of the sheet into which values are to be written (case-sensitive)

cell – the address of the first cell in the range whose values are to be obtained

arrayname – the name of the LIMS Basic array in which the values are to be stored

transpose – the value true (or "T") if the LIMS Basic array is to be transposed before the values are written into the cells.  If not specified, LIMS will use the value false.

skipEmpty – the value true (or "T") if empty values and unassigned array indices should be skipped (i.e. they will not be pasted into the corresponding target cells).  If not specified, LIMS will use the value false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified file name is not an integer, LIMS will open the specified file.  If the file does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook not found".

•LIMS navigates to the specified worksheet.  If no sheet with the specified name exists in the workbook, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook has no sheet named".

•LIMS navigates to the specified range.

•LIMS validates each element in the array.

•If the element has more than two indices, LIMS will trigger the error "Array cannot have more than two dimensions".

•If the first index is not a positive integer, LIMS will trigger the error "First array index must be a positive integer".

•If the element has two indices, and if the second index is not a positive integer, LIMS will trigger the error "Second array index must be a positive integer".

•LIMS places the values of the array elements in the worksheet, starting with the specified cell address.

•The value of the array element [1] for a one-dimensional array and the array element [1,1] for a two-dimensional array is copied into the specified first cell.

•If the value true was specified for the transpose argument, the first array index identifies the column and the second array index identifies the row relative to the starting cell.  For example, if the first cell is C10, array element [1,2] will be copied to cell C11 and array element [2,1] will be copied to cell D10.

•Otherwise, if the value false was specified for the transpose argument, the first array index identifies the row and the second array index identifies the column relative to the starting cell.  For example, if the first cell is C10, array element [1,2] will be copied to cell D10 and array element [2,1] will be copied to cell C11.

•If the value of the skipEmpty argument is true, any element that is either missing or blank will be skipped.  In other words, the corresponding cell value in the worksheet will not be overwritten.

•Otherwise, if the value of the skipEmpty argument is false, any element that is either missing or blank will cause the corresponding cell value in the worksheet to be overwritten with "", the empty string.

•LIMS returns the value true.

The spreadsheet and the Excel application remain open.  Updates are not saved to the spreadsheet unless the ExcelSave function is used.  To close the spreadsheet, use the ExcelClose function.  To close the application, use the ExcelQuit function.

Example:

The following example writes the days of the week into the row that begins with cell A7.

weekDays = "Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday"

numElements = ArrayOfSubStrings(weekDays, ",","weekArray",)

status = ExcelSetArray(xlWorkbook, "Sheet1", "A7", weekArray, false)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully. It returns the value false if an error is triggered.   In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

ExcelSetValue  

Purpose:

The ExcelSetValue function sets the value of a given cell in a given spreadsheet..

CAUTION: When setting values, the OLE interface converts values to Floating Point values.  As a result, some precision may be lost and the value set in Excel may not be the exact value passed to the function.  For example a value of 0.99999 may be set as 0.999989986419677. 

Also very large Integer values will trigger an error in Excel and will cause the function to fail.


Syntax:

status = ExcelSetValue(fileName, sheet, cell, value)

fileName – one of the following: (a) the name of the Excel workbook file, including path information if the file is not located in the working folder; or (b), an OLE Automation handle that refers to a Workbook object.

sheet – the name of the sheet into which the value is to be placed (case-sensitive)

cell – the address of the cell whose value is to be updated

value – the value to be placed in the specified cell

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified file name is not an integer, LIMS will open the specified file.  If the file does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook not found".

•LIMS navigates to the specified worksheet.  If no sheet with the specified name exists in the workbook, LIMS will trigger the error "Workbook has no sheet named".

•LIMS navigates to the specified cell.

•LIMS sets the value of the specified cell.

•LIMS returns the value true.

The spreadsheet and the Excel application remain open.  Updates are not saved to the spreadsheet unless the ExcelSave function is used.  To close the spreadsheet, use the ExcelClose function.  To close the application, use the ExcelQuit function.

Example:

The following example set the value of cell B1 in sheet "Sheet1" in the spreadsheet with file name ExcelExample.xls to 123.

status = ExcelSetValue("C:\ExcelExample.xls", "Sheet1", "B5", 123)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully. It returns the value false if an error is triggered.   In the case of an OLE Automation error, the text of the associated error message is stored in the lastError variable, and the value false is returned.

 

 

28.3.15  Field Control Functions
 

Function

DisableEntryFields

EnableEntryFields

UpdateEntryFields

UpdateRunningNote

FieldDataType

MandatoryField

SetDBEntryFieldWhereClause

SetFieldColor

SetFocusOnWindow

SetListEntryFieldList

TableFields

UpdateEntryField

UpdateEntryFields

UpdateRunningNote

UpdateTableFlds

 

DisableEntryFields

Purpose:

This function is used to disable fields in a window.

Syntax:

status = DisableEntryFields(fieldsArray, suppressErrorFlag)

fieldNamesArray – A one dimensional array of the names of the fields, in uppercase, to be disabled

suppressErrorFlag – a Boolean indicating if missing fields will cause the function to fail.  If not specified, the value defaults to false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the suppressErrorFlag Boolean. If this Boolean is set to true, all fields from the fieldNamesArray that are present will be disabled and missing fields will be ignored. The function will return true with no last error. If this Boolean is set to false and a field in the fieldNamesArray is encountered that is not present in the current window, the function will stop disabling fields when the missing field is encountered and the function will return false with a last error: 'Window does not exist: field ' (where field is the name of the missing field).  The system will halt at the first missing field and no other fields will be checked.

NOTE: This function must be executed in an automation script environment. 


Example: 

fieldsArray[1] = "NUM_CONTAINERS"

fieldsArray[2] = "TEST_LIST"

suppressErrorFlag = "T"

status = DisableEntryFields(fieldsArray, suppressErrorFlag)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

EnableEntryFields

Purpose:

This function is used to disable fields in a window.

Syntax:

status = EnableEntryFields(fieldsArray, suppressErrorFlag)

fieldNamesArray – A one dimensional array of the names of the fields, in uppercase, to be enabled

suppressErrorFlag – a Boolean indicating if missing fields will cause the function to fail.  If not specified, the value defaults to false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The function checks the suppressErrorFlag Boolean. If this Boolean is set to true, all fields from the fieldNamesArray that are present will be enabled and missing fields will be ignored. The function will return true with no last error. If this Boolean is set to false and a field in the fieldNamesArray is encountered that is not present in the current window, the function will stop enabling fields when the missing field is encountered and the function will return false with a last error: 'Window does not exist: field ' (where field is the name of the missing field).  The system will halt at the first missing field and no other fields will be checked.

NOTE: This function must be executed in an automation script environment. 


Example:

 

 

fieldsArray[1] = "SAMPLE_VOLUME"

fieldsArray[2] = "SAMPLED_DATE"

suppressErrorFlag = "T"

status = DisableEntryFields(fieldsArray, suppressErrorFlag)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

FieldDataType     

Purpose:

The FieldDataType function returns the data type of the specified field.

Syntax:

limsType = FieldDataType(tableName, fieldName)

tableName – the name of the table that contains the field whose data type is to be returned

fieldName – the name of the field whose data type is to be returned

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no table name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "Table name must be specified".

•If no field name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "Field name must be specified".

•LIMS retrieves the data types for the specified table.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid table name specified".

•LIMS determines the data type of the specified field.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field name specified".

•LIMS returns the data type.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example returns the value "Integer".

limsType = FieldDataType("SAMPLE", "SAMPLE_NUMBER")

Returns:

The function returns a string that represents a data type if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

MandatoryField     

Purpose:

The MandatoryField function makes a table field mandatory or optional.

Syntax:

status = MandatoryField(tableName, fieldName, mandatoryFlag)

tableName – the name of the table, in uppercase, optional.. This argument is not used by the function to verify the field on the active window is actually from that table.

fieldName – the name of the field, in uppercase, to be made mandatory or optional

mandatoryFlag – an optional boolean flag.  If the value "T" (true) is specified, the field will be marked as mandatory.  Otherwise, the field will be marked as optional.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified field does not exist in the specified table, LIMS will return the value false without triggering an error.

•If the value of mandatoryFlag is true, LIMS will mark the specified field as mandatory.  Otherwise, LIMS will mark it as optional.

•If the function is being called from an automation script, and if the current window contains a field with the specified field name, LIMS will immediately mark that field as mandatory or optional.

•If the specified table is the SAMPLE table, and if a sample login window is open, and if the specified field is present on the current sample login template, LIMS will immediately mark that field as mandatory or optional.

•The specified setting will remain in effect in the currently running copy of the LIMS application until it is closed, or until it is modified by another call to the MandatoryField function, or until the database OBJ file is rebuilt using the currently running LIMS application.

NOTE:  Use of this function is no longer restricted to automation scripts.  However, this function only applies to template based fields.


Example:

The following example makes the alias name in the analysis table mandatory.

status = MandatoryField("ANALYSIS", "ALIAS_NAME", "T")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetDBEntryFieldWhereClause    

Purpose:

The SetDBEntryFieldWhereClause function sets the where clause for an entry field in a table.

Syntax:

status = SetDBEntryFieldWhereClause(fieldName, whereClause)

fieldName – the field on the active dialog to be restricted by the whereClause.

whereClause – the where clause to be imposed on the specified field.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS verifies that the field is defined on the active dialog.  If the field is not defined, the LIMS returns the last error:
"Field does not exist:" followed by the specified field.

•The LIMS verifies that the specified field is a database entry field.  If the field is not a database entry field, the LIMS returns the last error:
"Field is not a DB Entry field."

•The LIMS then restricts the browse of the field based on the specified where clause.

NOTE: This function can be used outside of an automation script environment.


Example:

The following example restricts the Common Name field on the Analysis table to records with the name starting with IMP.

‘Using the ANALYSIS table
field = "COMMON_NAME"
whereClause = "NAME LIKE ‘IMP%’"
status = SetDBEntryFieldWhereClause(field, whereClause)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetFieldColor    

Purpose:

The SetFieldColor function updates the color of the label for the specified field.

Syntax:

status = SetFieldColor(fieldName, color)

fieldName – name of the field to have the label color controlled.

color – integer representing the color the label is to be formatted to.

Valid color entries: 1-Black, 2-Blue, 3-Brown, 4-Cyan, 5-Dark Blue, 6-Dark Cyan, 7-Dark Gray,8-Dark Green, 9-Dark Pink, 10-Dark Red, 11-Gray, 12- Green, 13-Pale Gray, 14-Pink, 15-Red, 16-White, 17-Yellow

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the defined color input is valid.  If the value is not valid, LIMS returns the error: "Invalid Color".

•LIMS sets the label of the targeted field to the specified color if the field is displayed on the dialog. 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following code sets the label for the Alias Name field to green.

fieldName = "ALIAS_NAME"
color = 12
status = SetFieldColor(fieldName, color)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered or the specified field was not displayed.

 

SetFocusOnWindow    

Purpose:

The SetFocusOnWindow function positions the cursor in a pane in a window.

Syntax:

status = SetFocusOnWindow(paneName)

paneName is the Window pane name and is case sensitive.

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

The following example will move the cursor to the Product field (such as in a Sample Login Template).

‘ Sample login template

status = SetFocusOnWindow("PRODUCT")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

SetListEntryFieldList    

Purpose:

The SetListEntryFieldList function sets the list of choices for the specified List Entry field.

Syntax:

status = SetListEntryFieldList(fieldName, listArray)

fieldName – the name of the List Entry field

listArray – the array that contains the list of valid choices for the field

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS locates the field whose name is equal to the value of fieldName on the current window.  If no field is located, LIMS will trigger the error "Field does not exist".  If the field is not a List Entry field, LIMS will trigger the error "Field is not a List Entry field".

•LIMS sets the list of available values for the field equal to the contents of the listArray variable.

This function does not save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example sets the list of choices for the field named "SAMPLING_POINT" to "TOP" and "BOTTOM".

MyArray[1] = "TOP"
MyArray[2] = "BOTTOM"
status = SetListEntryFieldList("SAMPLING_POINT", MyArray)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

TableFields    

Purpose:

The TableFields function returns an array of field names on that table.

Syntax:

status = TableFields(tableName,"arrayName")

tableName – the name of the table to be queried.

arrayName – the name of the array to contain the list of field names.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that a tableName has been provided.  If no tableName has been provided, the LIMS returns the last error: "Table name must be specified."

•LIMS verifies that the provided tableName is valid for the system.  If the tableName is not valid, LIMS returns the error: "Invalid table specified:" followed by the specified tablename.

•LIMS verifies that an arrayName has been provided.  If no arrayName has been provided, the LIMS returns the last error: "Array name must be specified."

•LIMS populates the specified array with the list of fields in the order they appear within the database.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example retrieves the fields on the Test table and populates the array: TFArray.

tableName = "TEST"
status = TableFields(tableName, "TFArray")
status = TableFields(tableName,"arrayName")

Returns:

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  The function returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateEntryField    

Purpose:

The UpdateEntryField function writes a value to an entry field in a window.

Syntax:

status = UpdateEntryField(field, value)

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

‘ Sample login template

status = UpdateEntryField("LOCATION", "BLDG_1_A")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

UpdateEntryFields

Purpose:

This function is used to update fields in a window.

Syntax:

status = UpdateEntryFields(fieldNamesArray, fieldValuesArray, suppressErrorFlag)

fieldNamesArray – A one dimensional array of the names of the fields, in uppercase, to be updated

fieldValuesArray - A one dimensional array of the field values to be used for the update

suppressErrorFlag – a Boolean indicating missing fields will cause the function to fail.  If not specified, the value defaults to false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The function checks the suppressErrorFlag Boolean. If this Boolean is set to true, all fields from the fieldNamesArray that are present will be updated and missing fields will be ignored. The function will return true with no last error. If this Boolean is set to false and a field in the fieldNamesArray is encountered that is not present in the current window, the function will stop updating fields when the missing field is encountered and the function will return false with a last error: 'Window does not exist: field ' (where field is the name of the missing field).  The system will halt at the first missing field and no other fields will be checked.

NOTE: This function must be executed in an automation script environment.  This function does not check for valid field values.


Example:

 

fieldNamesArray[1] = "CUSTOMER"

fieldNamesArray[2] = "DESCRIPTION"

fieldValuesArray[1] = "AUTO1"

fieldValuesArray[2] = "Testing"

suppressErrorFlag = "T"

status = UpdateEntryFields(fieldNamesArray, fieldValuesArray, suppressErrorFlag)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

UpdateRunningNote

Purpose:

Opens a dialog to capture running notes for a record.  Notes will be appended in a single record.  Each added note will be stamped with user name and date/time.

Syntax:

updatedNoteString = UpdateRunningNote(noteString, table, keyFields, keyValues, field, incrementVersionFlag, commitFlag)

noteString – existing note prior to appending a new running note (this argument is ignored if  the table, keyFields, keyValues, and field arguments are specified)

table – table name to update

keyFields – Array of key fields name for the table

keyValues – Array of the key field values for the record

field – Field name for the running note

incrementVersionFlag – Boolean, if true, will increment the version if the table is versioned, if false, does not increment version

commitFlag – Boolean that determines if the information is posted to cache awaiting the next save or committed immediately.  The default value is false.

modal – a Boolean indicating if the dialog is to be opened as a modal dialog.  If the modal flag is True, the LIMS Basic code execution will halt until the dialog is closed.  If not specified the default is True.

NOTE:  The field used for the running note must be defined as a MEMO (Access), CLOB (Oracle), TEXT (Sql Server) and defined as a long field in Table Master.

Comments:

The function can be used in two different ways:

1. The table, keyFields, keyValues, and field arguments can be used and the contents of the specified field for the record will be brought into context and opened in the Running Notes Dialog. The record is updated if the user adds an entry in the lower pane of the Running Notes Dialog and clicks OK or Insert in the dialog. The modified entry is returned in the updatedNoteString variable.

2. The Running Notes Dialog can be opened using the "noteString". The contents of the Note string is displayed in the upper pane of the Running Notes Dialog. The string is updated if the user adds an entry in the lower pane of the Running Notes Dialog and clicks OK or Insert in the dialog.  The updated string will be returned in the updatedNoteString variable.

The function performs the following steps.

•The system checks if the keyFields and keyValues arrays are the same size. “Error: keys and keyValues arrays must be the same size”

•Version field should not be named a keyField. If Version is specified, the system will trigger the error “Error: can not specify version as a key field”.

•If the table name is not found, the system will trigger the error “Table is not defined: table”.

•The system checks if the parent table has one key field.  If not, the system will trigger the error “only works for single key parent tables”.

•The system checks the first value specified is the key for the parent table.  If not, the system will trigger the error “parent table key must be the first entry specified by user”.

•The system checks each record corresponding to the key value. If a record is found that is not valid, the system will trigger the error “The parent record is invalid: keyvalue” (where keyValue is the key corresponding to the invalid record).  If more than one record is found, the system will trigger the error “More than one parent record found for the specified keys”.

•The system checks the data type of the key value and compares to the data type for the table key. If they do not match, the system will trigger the error “The specified object does not exist”.

•The system checks that the specified record has not been approved.  If approved, the system will trigger the error “Error: record is approved”.

•The system will updated the running note field with the noteString.

 

Example:

 

 

The following is an example for a running note on a sample using an info rule.

sampleNumber = select SAMPLE.SAMPLE_NUMBER
tableName = "SAMPLE"
status = ClearArray("keyFieldsArr")
status = ClearArray("keyValuesArr")
keyFieldsArr[1] = "SAMPLE_NUMBER"
keyValuesArr[1] = sampleNumber
fieldName = "OBSERVATIONS"
incrementVersionFlag = "F"
commitFlag = "T"
modal = "F"

updatedNoteString = UpdateRunningNote(noteString, tableName, keyFieldsArr, keyValuesArr, fieldName, incrementVersionFlag, commitFlag, modal)



Returns:

 

Returns the updated Note string if successful, otherwise returns ERROR.

 

UpdateTableFlds    

Purpose:

The UpdateTableFlds function updates one or more fields in any LIMS table.

This function should be used with caution as almost any field in any table may be updated. (See Comments section for restrictions on use of this function)

Syntax:

status = UpdateTableFlds(tableName, keyFields, keyValues, fieldsArray, valuesArray, auditReason,  VersionFlag, commitflag, auditFlag )

tableName – the name of the table to be updated

keyFields – an array of the names of the key fields for the table

keyValues – an array of the key field values for the record to be updated

fieldsArray – an array of the names of the fields to update in the table

valuesArray – an array of the field values for the update

auditReason – an optional audit reason to be recorded with the update

incrementVersionFlag – a boolean flag that indicates whether the version number of the record should be incremented when the changes are saved.

commitFlag – a Boolean flag that if true will commit the data when the function completes. Otherwise, the data is committed during the next LIMS commit to the sample (the next commit may happen when a later LIMS Basic function performs a commit or when a Save operation is performed).  If not specified, the default is true.

auditFlag – a Boolean flag that indicates whether the transaction will create an audit record.  If not specified, the default value is true.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The system interprets the incrementVersionFlag as follows.

•If the value is blank or missing, the system will use the value False.

•Otherwise, if the value begins with the letter "T" or "Y", the system will use the value True.

•Otherwise, the system will use the value False.

•The system interprets the commitFlag as follows.

•If the value is blank or missing, the system will use the value True.

•Otherwise, if the value begins with the letter "T" or "Y", the system will use the value True.

•Otherwise, the system will use the value False.

•If no audit reason is specified, the system will use the reason that is currently in effect for the specified table.  If no reason is in effect, the system will use the translation for the NLS key "AuditorNoReasonSpecified", which defaults to "No reason specified".

•If "VERSION" is one of the specified key fields, the system will trigger the error "Can’t specify version as a key field".

•If the specified table cannot be found in the database, the system will trigger the error "Table is not defined".

•The system retrieves the top level parent record for the record to be updated.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error "More than one parent record found for the specified keys".  Note that this error is also triggered when no parent record is found at all.

•The system retrieves the record to be updated.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error "More than one record found for the specified keys".  Note that this error is also triggered when no record is found at all.

•If the record to be updated is marked as approved, the system will trigger the error "Record is approved".

•The system processes each of the specified fields as follows.

•If the specified field name does not exist in the table, the system will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified field is a link field, and if the specified value is not blank and does not exist in the corresponding link table, the system will trigger the error "Invalid value for record".

•The system interprets the specified value as follows.

•If the specified field is a date field, and if the specified value cannot be interpreted as a valid date, the system will apply a null value instead.

•If the specified field is a Boolean field, the system will apply the value True if and only if the specified value argument begins with the letter "T".  Otherwise, the system will apply the value False.

•If the specified field is an interval field, and if the specified value is blank, the system will apply the value 0 (zero) instead.

•Otherwise, the system will apply the value as specified.

•The system updates the record with all field values as interpreted above.

•If the version is to be incremented, the system will increment the version number of the top level parent record.

•If an audit reason was specified and auditFlag is true, the system will apply it to the pending transaction.

•If changes should be committed to the database, the system saves both the top level parent record and the record that was updated.

NOTE:  This function saves changes to the database only if requested.

NOTE:  If user does not have the correct group security to see the record that is being updated the UpdateTableFlds function will return an error "The parent record is invalid".

Comments:

NOTE:  This function is intended for use against tables with single Key Fields, or versioned tables with two keys where one of the keys is VERSION.

NOTE: Care must be taken when using this function against base LIMS tables, specifically in the following scenerios:

---When the field being updated is managed by the LIMS application.  Examples are STATUS fields, APPROVED, CHANGE_ON, etc.

---When LIMS in in the process of updating the table per normal operation.  Examples would be updates to the table while LIMS is in the process of completing a record approval.  Updating and commiting using this or other LIMS Basic functions may disrupt the normal LIMS commit process.

NOTE: This function should not be used for updating dynamic tables such as the SAMPLE< TEST, RESULT, PROJECT, etc.  There are separate LIMS Basic functions for updating these tables.

NOTE: When updating child tables, the Parent table must be defined within Table Master.  To update the Product table (and its child tables) use the UpdateProduct function instead.

NOTE:  If the table to be updated is a dependent table, the first key field must be the key field of the top level parent table.  For example, if this function is used for updating a result record, the first key field must be the sample number; if the function is used for updating a component record, the first key field must be the analysis name.

NOTE:  The tableName, keyFields, and field names must be capitalized.

NOTE: Key Fields cannot be updated.

NOTE: If a field has a name that is a reserved word, it cannot be updated.

Example:

tableName = "ANALYSIS"

keyFields[1] = "NAME"

keyValues[1] = "CARBS"

fieldsArray[1] = "REPORTED_NAME"

fieldsArray[2] = "INST_GROUP"

valuesArray[1] = "Carbs Reported"

valuesArray[2] = "INSTG"

auditReason = "Because I want to."

status = UpdateTableFlds(tableName, keyFields, keyValues, fieldsArray, valuesArray, auditReason, "F", "F")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.
