CreateDBDirectory

Purpose:

The CreateDBDirectory function creates an entry in the DB_DIRECTORY table.

Syntax:

dirNum = CreateDBDirectory(dbDirectoryPath)

dbDirectoryPath – (Required)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the dbDirectoryPath parameter.  If it is empty, the system will trigger the error: “Invalid directory specified: dbDirectoryPath”.  If the directory already exists, the system will trigger the error: “Specified database directory already exists: dbDirectoryPath”.

•Otherwise the system creates the new entry in the DB_DIRECTORY table and returns the new directory number.

 NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example  creates a new sub-directory ‘Seq123’ in the ‘Testing’ db directory:

dbDirectoryPath = "Testing\Seq123"

dirNum = CreateDBDirectory(dbDirectoryPath)

Returns:

 

The function returns the new directory number if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer ERROR and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

CurDir    

Purpose:

The CurDir function returns the current directory path. 

NOTE: In the Windows Client, the CurDir function returns the client directory path.  In the web the CurDir Function returns the client directory path from the related Windows client and the www folder for the unique session in the format of: <Client Directory>\www\<Client Name>_<Unique Session Code>.


Syntax:

pathname = CurDir()

Example:

pathname = CurDir()  

Returns:

Returns the name of the current directory, for example, pathname = "c:\lw-lims".

 

DirExists    

Purpose:

The DirExists function returns a Boolean indicating whether a directory exists.

Syntax:

status = DirExists(pathname)

pathname – directory to be checked.

Example:

status = DirExists("c:\lw-lims")

Returns:

status = true if the directory exists, false otherwise.

 

DirFiles    

Purpose:

The DirFiles function returns an array containing a list of files in a specified  directory.  The array also contains the file size, time/date, and attributes.

Syntax:

status = DirFiles(directory, filter, "returnArrayName", "returnSubDirectoriesArrayName")

directory – the location where the files reside.

filter – used to refine the list of files selected, for example filter may be "*.txt" or "lims*.*"

returnArrayName – the name of the array in which the file information will be retuned.
fileArray[1, 1] = filename
fileArray[1, 2] = file size in bytes
fileArray[1, 3] = file time/date
fileArray[1, 4] = file attributes
fileArray[1, 5] = internal system number used for sorting

returnSubDirectoriesArrayName – the name of the array in which the subdirectory names will be retuned.

Comments:

 

Example:

status = DirFiles("c:\lw-lims", "*.txt", "fileArray", "SubArray")

Returns:

 

fileArray[1, 1] = ‘!BIND.TXT’

fileArray[1, 2] = 246

fileArray[1, 3] = ‘07/15/1999 09:46:28 AM’

fileArray[1, 4] = ‘a’

fileArray[1, 5] = 310948788

subArray[1,1] = ‘Bitmaps’
subArray[1,2] = ‘Database’
subArray[1,3] = ‘Dynamic’
subArray[1,4] = ‘fckeditor’
subArray[1,5] = ‘Reports’
subArray[1,6] = ‘Static’

 

DirKill    

Purpose:

The DirKill function deletes a directory.

Syntax:

status = DirKill(pathName)

pathname – directory to be deleted.

Example:

status = DirKill("c:\lw-lims\test")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

DirNew    

Purpose:

The DirNew function creates a directory.

Syntax:

status = DirNew(pathName, createAllFlag)

pathname – the full path of the directory to be created.

createAllFlag – a Boolean parameter which if the function creates all directories up to the final named directory or just the final directory.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The system tries to create the specified directories.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error: “Error creating directory”.

 

Example:

The following example will create directories: Directory1 and Directory2 if they do not exist (Directory2 is a subdirectory of Directory1):

status = DirNew("c:\Directory1\ Directory2","T")

Returns:

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

DirZip  

Purpose:

The DirZip function zips the files in the specified directory using Windows built-in compression technology.

Syntax:

status = DirZip(dirName, zipName, filesFlag, subDirsFlag)

dirName- name of the directory containing the files to be zipped.

zipName- name with path of the zip file that will contain the files to be zipped

filesFlag- Boolean, if true, this function will add all files in the specified directory to the specified zip file. Default value is false.

subDirsFlag- Boolean, if true, this function will add all subdirectories in the specified directory to the specified zip file.  This includes the entire contents of those subdirectories as well as any subdirectories contained within them. Default value is false.

Comments:

If the specified zip file does not exist, it will be created.

If the specified zip file does exist, then the LIMS will trigger the error ‘Cannot zip.  Zip file exists’.

NOTE: if you wish to add one entire directory to a zip file, in such a way that the directory itself is the top-level object in the zip file, use the FileZip function instead.


 Example:

The following example zips all files in a directory called M0433Zip in the working directory:

pathName = CurDir()

dirName = pathName & "\M0433Zip"

zipName = pathName & "\M0433Out\M0433Zip2.zip"

filesFlag = "T"

subDirsFlag = "T"

status = DirZip(dirName,zipName,filesFlag,subDirsFlag)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

FileAtEnd    

Purpose:

The FileAtEnd function returns a Boolean indicating whether the file pointer is at the end of file.

Syntax:

status = FileAtEnd(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be open.

Example:

status = FileAtEnd("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the pointer is at end of file, false otherwise.

 

FileBottom    

Purpose:

The FileBottom function moves the file pointer to the bottom of a file.

Syntax:

status = FileBottom(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be open. 

Example:

status = FileBottom("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileClose    

Purpose:

The FileClose function closes a file.

Syntax:

status = FileClose(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be open.

Example:

status = FileClose("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileContents    

Purpose:

The FileContents function returns the contents of the specified file.

Syntax:

string = FileContents(fileName, treatAsText, encodingHint)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

treatAsText – Boolean indicating how file is to be evaluated; defaults to False if not specified.  If set to true, the file is considered to be a text file and is translated to Unicode based on the system default language.  If this is invoked from the web the output string is base64 encoded.

encodingHint – (optional) Used to specify a Unicode encoding for files which do not specify their own encoding. 'AUTO' is assumed if no value is defined.

Valid values include:

'AUTO' –The existing functionality is preserved.  Files with no BOM will be assumed to be ASCII files.
'UTF8' - Files with no BOM will use the UTF8 decoder
'UTF16' - Files with no BOM will use the UTF16 Little Endian decoder
'UTF16LE' - Files with no BOM will use the UTF16 Little Endian decoder
'UTF16BE' - Files with no BOM will use the UTF16 Big Endian decoder

NOTE: Files with BOM (Byte Order Marker) present will use the appropriate decoder and the encodingHint will be ignored.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the file indicated by fileName does not exist, the system will trigger the error "Specified file does not exist".

•LabWare checks the encoding. If it does not match “Invalid encoding. Should be AUTO, UTF8, UTF16, UTF16LE, or UTF16BE”

•The system retrieves and returns the contents of the file.

 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example displays the contents of the file name "c:\readme.txt" in a message box

encodingHint = "AUTO"

string = FileContents("c:\readme.txt",, encodingHint)
Msgbox(string)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value ERROR if an error is triggered.

 

FileContentsOfZip   

Purpose:

The FileContentsOfZip function reads the file and folder names of the specified zip file and places the results in arrays that have the specified names.

Syntax:

status = FileContentsOfZip(zipName, pattern, "filesArrayName", "sizesArrayName", "datesArrayName", "dirsArrayName", recursive, hierarchical)

zipName- name with path of the zip file that containing the zipped files

pattern- this argument should either be the exact name of a file that is present in the zip file, or "*.*" to get all files and directories

filesArrayName- an array name, if not specified, the following arguments will be ignored: pattern, sizesArrayName, datesArrayName

sizesArrayName- an array name, if specified, the sizes of all files will be placed in an array with this name.  The size at a given index is the size of the file whose name appears at the same index in the files array.

datesArrayName- an array name, if specified, the modification dates of all files will be placed in an array with this name.  The date at a given index is the date of the file whose name appears at the same index in the files array

dirsArrayName- an array name, if specified, the names of all directories will be placed in an array with this name

 recursive- Boolean, if true, all directories in the zip file will be read (as well as all directories contained within those directories, and so on), and all file names will be added to the files array and all directory names will be added to the directories array.  Default value is false.

hierarchical- Boolean, if both recursive and hierarchical are true, all elements in the files array and the dirs array will be prefixed with the names of the parent folders.  Default value is false.

Comments:

The specified zip file must already exist.

 Example:

The following example prompts for the user to select a Zip file then lists any XLS files found in the zip file.

zipName = PromptForFile("Pick a zip file")

pattern = "*.xls"

recursive = "T"

hierarchical = "T"

status = FileContentsOfZip(zipName, pattern, "filesArrayName", "sizesArrayName", "datesArrayName", "dirsArrayName", recursive, hierarchical)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

FileCopy    

Purpose:

The FileCopy function copies a file from a source to a destination.

Syntax:

status = FileCopy(source, destination)

source – the directory and name of the target file

destination – the location and name of the file to be created.

Comments:

Wildcards are not allowed. 

Example:

status = FileCopy("d:\pclims\test.txt", "a:\new_file.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileDate    

Purpose:

The FileDate function returns the date stamp of a file.

Syntax:

fdate = FileDate(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

fdate is a date data type.

Example:

fdate = FileDate("c:\pclims\junk.txt")   

Returns:

fdate = 06/17/94

 

FileExists    

Purpose:

The FileExists function returns a Boolean indicating whether a file exists.

Syntax:

status = FileExists(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Example:

status = FileExists("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the file exists, false otherwise.

 

FileFindReplace    

Purpose:

The FileFindReplace function finds strings within a file and replaces them with  other strings.

Syntax:

status = FileFindReplace(filename, findStringArray, replaceStringArray)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

findStringArray – One dimensional array of strings to be replaced.

replaceStringArray – One dimensional array of strings to be inserted in place of the corresponding value in the findStringArray.

Comments:

The file must be closed.

Example:

 

findStringArray[1] = "Lima"

replaceStringArray[1] = "LIMS"

status = FileFindReplace("c:\pclims\junk.txt", findStringArray,   replaceStringArray)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileIsOpen    

Purpose:

The FileIsOpen function returns a Boolean indicating whether a file is open.

Syntax:

status = FileIsOpen(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Example:

status = FileIsOpen("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the file is open, false otherwise.

 

FileKill    

Purpose:

The FileKill function deletes a file.

Syntax:

status = FileKill(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file.

timeOut – Time out interval in seconds.  If not specified a timeout of 0 is defaulted.

Comments:

If the file cannot be deleted within the specified timeout the function returns false.

NOTE: Wildcards are not allowed.


Example:

status = FileKill("d:\pclims\test.txt", 60)

Returns:

status = true if the file was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileNew    

Purpose:

The FileNew function creates and opens a new file.

Syntax:

status = FileNew(fileName, unicodeFlag)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

unicodeFlag – Boolean; determines if the generated file is written in Unicode.  The default value is false.

Comments:

The file cannot already exist.

Example:

status = FileNew("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileNormal    

Purpose:

The FileNormal function sets the attributes of a file to normal.

Syntax:

status = FileNormal(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

This function is used to change file attributes from read only to normal.

Example:

status = FileNormal("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileOpen    

Purpose:

The FileOpen function opens a file for reading or writing. 

Syntax:

status = FileOpen(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be closed. If the specified fileName does not exist, the system will create the file.  The created file will be ASCII by default.

NOTE: To create a Unicode file, use the FileNew command using the Unicode parameter.

Example:

status = FileOpen("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileRead    

Purpose:

The FileRead function reads the current line from a file.

Syntax:

line = FileRead(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be open. After the read, the file pointer will be positioned to the next line in the file.

Example:

line = FileRead("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

line ="line of data from file"

 

FileReadOnly    

Purpose:

The FileReadOnly function sets the attributes of a file to read only.

Syntax:

status = FileReadOnly(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

Setting the attribrutes of a file to read only leaves the file open until the system is rebooted.

Example:

status = FileReadOnly("c:\pclims\junk.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileRename    

Purpose:

The FileRename function renames a file.

Syntax:

status = FileRename(oldName, newName)

oldName – the directory and original name of the file

newName – the directory and new name of the file

Comments:

A file cannot be renamed across devices.

Example:

status = FileRename("c:\pclims\test.txt", "c:\pclims\new.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileSend    

Purpose:

The FileSend function will send a file from the LIMS server to the Web Browser.  The behavior of the File Download depends on the browser.

Syntax:

status = FileSend(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file to be sent

Comments:

The file is sent to the browser and is downloaded using the browser’s file download function.  Some browsers may block this kind of download and the user may have to specifically allow the download.

Example:

fileName = "c:\Output\Workbook_091211_1.xls"

status = FileSend(fileName)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileSize    

Purpose:

The FileSize function returns the size of the file.

Syntax:

integer = FileSize(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The size is returned in bytes.

Example:

integer = FileSize("c:\pclims\test.txt")   

Returns:

integer = 9863

 

FileTime    

Purpose:

The FileTime function returns the time stamp of a file.

Syntax:

ftime = FileTime(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

ftime is a time data type. 

Example:

ftime = FileTime("c:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

ftime = 12:02:12  PM

 

FileTop    

Purpose:

The FileTop function positions the file pointer to the top of a file.

Syntax:

status = FileTop(fileName)

fileName – the directory and name of the file

Comments:

The file must be open.

Example:

status = FileTop("d:\pclims\test.txt")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileUnzip  

Purpose:

The FileUnzip function extracts all files that match the specified pattern from the specified zip file and places them in the specified directory using Windows built-in compression technology..

Syntax:

status = FileUnzip(pattern, zipName, dirName)

pattern- this argument should either be the exact name of a file that is present in the zip file, or "*.*" to get all files and directories.

zipName- name with path of the zip file that will be unzipped

dirName- name of the directory to which the files will be unzipped.

Comments:

This function extracts all files that match the specified pattern from the specified zip file and places them in the specified directory.

The specified zip file must already exist.

The specified directory must already exist.

 

Other patterns may be possible, but have not been explicitly tested by LabWare.

 Example:

The following example extracts all pdf files from the specified zip file to the current working directory:

zipName = PromptForFile("Pick a zip file")

pattern = "*.pdf"

dirName = CurDir()

status = FileUnzip(pattern, zipName, dirName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

FileWrite    

Purpose:

The FileWrite function writes a line to a file.

Syntax:

status = FileWrite(fileName, line, newLineFlag)

fileName – the directory and name of the file to which the text will be written.

line – text to be inserted.

newLineFlag – determines whether a "CR,LF" is added at the end of the new line.  If this flag = F then the next line written using this command will be appended to the existing line.  This argument defaults toT if not specified.

Comments:

The file must be open.

Example:

line = "This is a test."

status = FileWrite("d:\pclims\test.txt", line, "T")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileWriteContents    

Purpose:

The FileWriteContents function writes the specified contents to a file with the specified name.

Syntax:

status = FileWriteContents(fileName, contents, uniCode)

fileName – the name of the file to be written

contents – the contents to be written to the file

unicodeFlag – Boolean; determines if the generated file is written in Unicode.  The default value is false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS creates a file with the specified name.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger an error that is equal to the corresponding operating system error message.

•LIMS writes the specified contents to the file.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example creates a file named "c:\test.log" with two lines: "abc" on the first line and "def" on the second line.

contents = "abc" + Chr(13) + Chr(10) + "def"
status = FileWriteContents("c:\test.log", contents)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FileZip   

Purpose:

The FileZip function adds the specified file or directory to the specified zip file using Windows built-in compression technology.

Syntax:

status = FileZip(fileName, zipName)

fileName – string, usually, the name of the file that is to be zipped. If a directory name, without a file name, is specified, the entire directory including all its files and subdirectories, will be added to the zip file.  The directory itself will be the top-level object in the zip file.

zipName- name with path of the zip file that will contain the files to be zipped. 

Comments:

If the specified zip file does not exist, it will be created.

If the specified zip file does exist, then ideally it should be a zip file that was created by one of the LIMS Basic functions that are available for that purpose.  LabWare does not guarantee that the function will work with zip files that were created by a third-party software package.

 Example:

The following example zips the specified file in the specified zip file to the current working directory:

fileName = PromptForFile("Pick a file")

pathName = CurDir()

zipName = pathName & "\M0433Zip2.zip"

status = FileZip(fileName, zipName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

GetDBFile    

Purpose:

The GetDBFile function is used to download a LIMS Database File into the computer drive.

Syntax:

status = GetDBFile(dbFileName, filePathName)

dbFileName - the name of the file in the database

filePathName - the name of the file to be created in the file system.  The full file path and the file name must be specified.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS will check the filePathName  If the filePathName already exists , LIMS will trigger the error "Specified file already exists: ' , filePathName."

•The LIMS will try to extract the db file record.  If a file is not created from the query, (for example if the file does not exist in the database) LIMS will trigger the error "Error getting file from the database'.". 

Example:

The following example downloads a Database File "0000000007.ppt" into computer drive "C:\DBFiles\".  The created file is named "M0190.ppt".

dbFileName = "0000000007.ppt"

filePathName = "C:\DBFiles\M0190.ppt"

status = GetDBFile(dbFileName, filePathName)

Returns:

 

If successful returns True else returns False.

 

PrintFile    

Purpose:

The PrintFile function prints a text file on a local or network printer.

Syntax:

status = PrintFile(printer, jobName, fileName)

printer: the Windows© printer name. If printer is set to "", the default printer is used. 

jobName:s a user defined string to identify the print job. 

fileName: the path and file name of the text file to be printed.

Comments:

NOTE:  The PrintFile function cannot be used with PDF Files.  Instead the PrintPDF function should be used.  If used with a PDF file it will return false with a last error..


Example:

status = PrintFile("HP DeskJet 855C", "LIMS Job",  "c:\lims\trace.log")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

PrintFileRaw    

Purpose:

The PrintFileRaw function prints the contents of a file to a Windows printer in raw format..

Syntax:

status = PrintFileRaw(printer, jobname, fileName)

printer – the name of the Windows© printer on which the data is to be printed

jobname – the title to be shown as the job name in the print spooler

fileName – the path and file name of the file to be printed.

NOTE:  The file to be printed must include the printer codes since the data is being sent direct to the printer.  Normally this function is used to send to data to barcode printers.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If a printer name is specified, and it has not been defined in the Windows operating system as a printer, LIMS will trigger the error "Printer does not exist".

•If the specified file does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "File does not exist".

•LIMS opens a job with specified job name on the Windows print spooler for the specified printer, and sends it the contents of the file to be printed in raw format.  (This step is performed using Windows API calls.  If any API call fails, LIMS will return the value False without displaying a LIMS error message in addition to any message that Windows may already have displayed.)

Example:

The following example prints the contents of the file named "c:\lims\results.txt" on the printer named "HP Deskjet 650" with job name "My Print Job".

status = PrintFileRaw("HP Deskjet 650", "My Print Job", "c:\lims\results.txt")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PutDBFile    

Purpose:

The PutDBFile function creates a LIMS Database file.

Syntax:

dbFileName = PutDBFile(filePath, dbDirectoryPath, fieldsArray, valuesArray, createDirFlag)

filePath - the file path name of the file in the file system

dbDirectoryPath - the directory path to the folder in the database to contain the file (folder will be created if it does not already exist)

fieldsArray - the name of the property fields to have values set

valuesArray - the values for the property fields

createDirFlag - a Boolean value indicating if the dbDirectoryPath should be created if it doesn’t exist

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS will check the fieldsArray and valuesArray..  If the fieldsArray and valuesArray are not the same size , LIMS will trigger the error "The number of arguments and values are not the same."

•The LIMS will check the filePath.  If the filePath does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified file does not exist: ' , filePath."

•The LIMS will check the dbDirectoryPath.  If the dbDirectoryPath does not exist, LIMS will create the directory unless createDirFlag is false then LIMS will trigger the error "Specified database directory does not exist".

•The LIMS will try to copy the file to the db file record.  If a file is not created, LIMS will trigger the error "Error copying file to the database.". 

NOTE: This function does perform a database commit.

Example:

The following example uploads a file in the database folder  "LW Documentation"

file = PromptForFile("Select a File", "C:\")

dbDirectoryPath = "LW Documentation"

fieldsArray[1] = "DESCRIPTION"

valuesArray[1] = "M0190 - Documentation"

dbFileName = PutDBFile(file,dbDirectoryPath,fieldsArray,valuesArray, "T")

Returns:

If successful returns dbFileName otherwise ERROR.

 

ShellCommand    

Purpose:

The ShellCommand function executes a Windows operating system command.

NOTE:  This command is not recommended for use with a web client since the command will execute on the server not the client machine.


Syntax:

status = ShellCommand(fileName, operation, waitFlag, parameters)

fileName – the name (and path) of the file on which the operation is to be performed.

operation – the operation to be performed on the file.  Valid values include "open", "print", or any other action defined in the Windows Registry for the file’s file type.

waitFlag – the value True if LIMS should wait for the operation to complete before continuing, and the value False otherwise.  The default value is False.

parameters – The operation variable defines the %1 input for the DDE command line.  The parameters input provides a mechanism to define %2 input (and additional inputs) based on the definition for the file type in the Windows Registry.  To define multiple inputs using the parameter variable, enter the values separated by a space (e.g., parameter = "value1 value2" sets %2 = value1 and %3 = value2 for the DDE command line).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the file does not represent a web URL, i.e., does not begin with the substring "http://", LIMS will check if the file exists.  If the file is not found, LIMS will trigger the error "File does not exist".

•LIMS asks the operating system to perform the specified operation on the specified file.

•If the value of waitFlag begins with "T" or "Y", LIMS will interpret the value as True and wait until the operation completes.  Otherwise, LIMS will continue as soon as the operating system allows it to do so.

•If an error condition is returned by the operating system, LIMS will trigger the error "Shell execute failed".

Example:

The following example prints the file named nlstmp.txt to the specified printer (Adobe PDF, without waiting for the printing to complete.

Parameter = chr(34) + "Adobe PDF" + chr(34)

status = ShellCommand("c:\lw-lims\nlstmp.txt", "printto", ,parameter)

NOTE:  The defined parameters must literally include double quotes when passed to Windows, chr(34) imbeds the double quotes into the parameter.


Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

NOTE: If the waitFlag is set to false, the command is passed to the operating systems and LIMS does not monitor if the operation is completed successfully


 

WaitForFile   

Purpose:

The WaitForFile  function waits until the file exists and is not locked.   If the file is not found before the timeOut elapses, it will return false.

Syntax:

status = WaitForFile(fileName, timeOut, size)

filename – the name and path of the file to be found (if no path is included the system will check the LabWare working directory).

timeOut – time interval in seconds.  If the file is not found after checking for this amount of time the function will stop checking.

size – (optional) minimum size of file, in bytes. If specified, the file must be larger than size before the function returns true.  (The size should not include commas e.g. use 10000 not 10,000).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks that filename is a string and is not empty. If not, the system will trigger the error “Invalid filename: filename”.

•The system checks that timeOut is an integer. If not, the system will trigger the error “Invalid timeOut: timeOut”.

•If specified, the system checks that size is an integer. If not, the system will trigger the error “Invalid size: size”.

The system checks for filename.  If not found in the specified time interval or the detected file is too small, the function will return false. If found and the size is greater than size (if specified), the function returns true.

 Example:

The following example checks for the file named " resultsin.csv” that is at least 500 bytes.

fileName = "c:\import\resultsin.csv"

timeOut = 15

size = 500

status = WaitForFile(fileName, timeOut,size)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered or the file is not found.

 

 

28.3.17  Folder Functions
 

Function

AddObjectToObjectGroup

CreateAdHocFolder

CreateFolder

CreateObjectGroup

DeleteFolder

FolderObjects

FolderResults

 

AddObjectToObjectGroup    

Purpose:

This function adds an object to an active Object Group.

Syntax:

Status = AddObjectToObjectGroup(tableName, ObjectId)

tableName – string defining the name of the table the object belongs to.

ObjectId – the key value of the object to be added.

Comments:

NOTE:  This function requires that an Object Group is the active window.

This function does not perform a commit to the database.

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS evaluates the input tableName.  If empty, LIMS returns the last error "No table name specified".

•LIMS evaluates the input objectId.  If empty, LIMS returns the last error: "No object id specified".

•LIMS attempts to identify the active Object Group.  If there is no active window, LIMS returns the error: "There is no active window".  If the active window is not an Object Group, LIMS returns the error: "The active window is not object group manager.

•LIMS assigns the object to the active object group.

 

Example:

Status = AddObjectToObjectGroup("PROJECT", "DEMO-0123")

Returns:

Function returns status = true if successful, false if an error is triggered.

 

CreateAdHocFolder    

Purpose:

The CreateAdHocFolder function creates a new ad hoc folder.

Syntax:

status = CreateAdHocFolder(objectClass, objectIdArray, templateName, temporaryFlag)

objectClass – the type of object to be stored in the folder.  Must be one of "SAMPLE", "TEST", or "RESULT".

objectIdArray – the primary key values of the objects to be stored in the folder.  In other words, an array of sample numbers, test numbers, or result numbers, depending on the value of objectClass.

templateName – the name of the folder template from which the folder is to be created. temporaryFlag– a Boolean value indicating if the folder is temporary and therefore not to be  saved.  If false a Save Changes prompt will be displayed when exiting Folder Manager or opening another folder.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the folder template whose name is equal to the value of templateName.  If no such template exists, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid folder template name".

•If the value of objectClass is not one of "SAMPLE", "TEST", or "RESULT", LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid object class".

•LIMS creates the new folder.  It sets the group name of the folder equal to the group name of the current user; it sets the owner equal to the current user; and it sets the template equal to the name of the retrieved folder template.

•LIMS retrieves the specified objects.  Objects that exist are added to the folder.  Objects that cannot be retrieved are skipped without triggering an error.

•LIMS opens the new folder in Folder Manager.

 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example creates a new folder from the template named "TEMPL1" and stores the tests 451, 784, and 419 in it.

myArray[1] = 451

myArray[2] = 784

myArray[3] = 419

status = CreateAdHocFolder("TEST", myArray, "TEMPL1","T")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

CreateFolder    

Purpose:

The CreateFolder function creates a new folder.

Syntax:

status = CreateFolder(folderName, templateName, owner, description, adHocFlag, objectClass, objectIdArray)

folderName – the name of the folder to be created

templateName – the name of the template from which the folder is to be created

owner – the username of the LIMS user who should own the folder.  (The default value is the current user.)

description – a description for the folder

adHocFlag – the value true if LIMS should create an ad hoc folder.  (The default value is false.)

objectClass – the type of object to be stored in the folder.  Must be one of the following: "SAMPLE", "TEST", or "RESULT".

objectIdArray – an array that contains the primary key values of the objects to be stored in the folder.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no value is specified for folderName, LIMS will trigger the error "Folder name must be provided".

•If a folder with the specified name already exists, LIMS will trigger the error "Folder already exists".

•If an owner is specified, but no LIMS user exists with that name, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified owner is not a valid user name".

•The following steps apply when the value of adHocFlag is true.

•If a value is specified for templateName, LIMS will trigger the error "Can not specify a template if the ad hoc flag is set".

•If no value is specified for objectClass, LIMS will trigger the error "Object class must be specified for an ad hoc folder".

•If the value of objectClass is not equal to "SAMPLE", "TEST", or "RESULT", LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid object class".

•LIMS creates a new folder; assigns it the specified name, description, and owner; and assigns it the group name of the current user.

•LIMS associates the primary key values in objectIdArray with the folder.

•LIMS saves the folder in the database.

•The following steps apply when the value of adHocFlag is false or not specified.

•If no value is specified for templateName, LIMS will trigger the error "Template must be specified if the ad hoc flag is false".

•If no folder template exists for the specified name, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid template name".

•LIMS creates a folder from the specified template; assigns it the specified name, description, and owner; and assigns it the group name of the owner.

•LIMS saves the folder in the database.

NOTE: LIMS does not validate the values in the objectIdArray.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example creates a normal folder named "FOLDER2" from the template named "TEMPL1".  The folder will have description "new".

status = CreateFolder("FOLDER2", "TEMPL1", "", "new", false, "", "")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

CreateObjectGroup   

Purpose:

The CreateObjectGroup function creates an object group based on the specified template and opens Object Group Manager.

Syntax:

status = CreateObjectGroup(objectGroupName, templateName)

objectGroupName – the name for the new object group. The argument is optional if the template has an Id Config defined.  However, if specified, this argument will override the Id Config value.

templateName – the name of the Object Group template from which the object group is to be created

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the objectGroupName against existing names.  If the name already template exists, LIMS will trigger the error "Object Group already exists".

•LIMS checks the templateName size.  If null, LIMS will trigger the error "Template must be specified"

•LIMS retrieves the template corresponding to the value of templateName.  If no such template exists, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid template name".

•LIMS creates the new Object Group and opens Object Group Manager.  It sets the group name of the folder equal to the group name of the current user; it sets the owner equal to the current user; and it sets the template equal to the name of the retrieved folder template. If an error is encountered, LIMS will trigger the error "Error creating Object Group"

 

NOTE: This function does save changes to the database.


 Example:

The following example creates an object group named "TESTING_123":

objectGroupName = "TESTING_123"

templateName = "TESTING"

status = CreateObjectGroup(objectGroupName, templateName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

DeleteFolder    

Purpose:

The DeleteFolder function deletes the specified folder.

Syntax:

status = DeleteFolder(folderName)

folderName – the name of the folder to be deleted

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no folder name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "Folder name must be provided".

•If a folder with the specified name does not exist in the database, LIMS will trigger the error "Folder does not exist".

•LIMS deletes the folder from the database and saves the changes.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example deletes the folder named "FOLDER1"

status = DeleteFolder("FOLDER1")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

FolderObjects    

Purpose:

The FolderObjects function returns an array containing information on objects in a folder. 

Syntax:

status = FolderObjects(folderName, "arrayName")

folderName – Name of the folder to be evaluated.

arrayName – Name of the one dimensional array containing the key values for the objects.

Comments:

If the folder contains samples, sample numbers are returned.  If the folder  contains tests, test numbers are returned.  If the folder contains results, result numbers are returned.

Example:

status = FolderObjects("MY_FOLDER", "myArray")

Returns:

myArray = (28 22 23 23 67)

 

FolderResults    

Purpose:

The FolderResults function returns a multi-dimensional array containing  information on objects in a folder

Syntax:

status = FolderResults(folderName, "arrayName")

folderName – name of the folder to be evaluated.

arrayName – name of the return array.

Comments:

The following columns are returned in the array:

1 - Sample Number

2 - Test Number

3 - Analysis

4 - Result Number

5 - Result Name

6 - Result Status

7 - Formatted Entry

8 - In_spec

9 - In_cal

Example:

status = FolderResults("MY_FOLDER", "myArray")

Returns:

myArray = (27 35 ‘FAT’ 225 ‘Before’ ‘E’ ‘100’ ‘T’ ‘F’)

CellText    

Purpose:

The CellText function returns the contents of a cell in a grid.

Syntax:

string = CellText(x, y)

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

string = CellText(1, 1)

Returns:

string = "CET Interval"

 

CreateFence    

Purpose:

The CreateFence function creates a fence around cells in a grid.  The fenced cells are white in color.

Syntax:

status = CreateFence(topLeftX, topLeftY, bottomRightX, bottomRightY)

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

status = CreateFence(1, 3, 3, 3)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

FindResultCell    

Purpose:

The FindResultCell function searches a result entry grid for a cell matching specified search criteria and returns an array containing the position of the cell matching the criteria.  If a cell is not found matching the criteria, the array is empty.

Syntax:

status = FindResultCell(fieldsArray, valuesArray,  "positionArrayName")

fieldsArray – One dimensional array of field names to be used as search criteria.

valuesArray – One dimensional array of values that correspond to the fields in the fields array.

positionArrayName – Array of cell positions that match the defined search criteria.

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

fieldsArray[1] = "RESULT.ENTRY"
fieldsArray[2] = "RESULT.SAMPLE_NUMBER"
valuesArray[1] = "75"
valuesArray[2] = 42
positionArrayName = "positionArray"

status = FindResultCell(fieldsArray, valuesArray, positionArrayName)

Returns:

positionArray = (1 3)

 

GoToCell    

Purpose:

The GoToCell function makes a cell in a grid the active cell.

Syntax:

status = GoToCell (x, y)

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

string = GoToCell(3, 2)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

IsCellLocked    

Purpose:

The IsCellLocked function returns a Boolean indicating whether a cell in a grid  is locked (not an input field).

Syntax:

locked = IsCellLocked (x, y)

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment.

Example:

locked = IsCellLocked(1, 1)

Returns:

status = true if the cell is locked, false otherwise

 

SetCellColor  

Purpose:

The SetCellColor function changes the background color of a cell in a grid.

Syntax:

status = SetCellColor(x, y, colorNumber)

x – an integer defining the column of the cell to be updated.

y – an integer defining the row of the cell to be updated.

colorNumber – integer value that defines what the color is set to.

Comments:

This function must be executed in an automation script environment. 

Valid colors:

1-Black, 2-Blue, 3-Brown, 4-Cyan, 5-Dark Blue, 6-Dark Cyan, 7-Dark Gray,

8-Dark Green, 9-Dark Pink, 10-Dark Red, 11-Gray, 12-Green, 13-Pale Gray,

14-Pink, 15-Red, 16-White, 17-Yellow

Example:

The following example sets the background color of the 3rd cell in the  2nd row of a grid to Dark Gray:

status = SetCellColor(3, 2, 7)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

SetCellValue  

Purpose:

The SetCellValue function updates the displayed grid by inserting the specified value in the specified cell.

Syntax:

status = SetCellValue(x,y,cellValue)

x – an integer defining the column of the cell to be updated.

y – an integer defining the row of the cell to be updated.

cellValues – value that defines what the field is to be set to.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies a valid window is in focus.  If the focus in to set to a valid window, LIMS returns the error: "Window is not valid".

•LIMS verifies that the window has a grid.  If the window does not have a valid grid, LIMS returns the error: "A valid grid could not be found in this window."

•LIMS verifies that the row specified is available on the grid.  If the row is greater than the last row on the grid, the LIMS returns the last error:
"Specified point is outside the grid rows."

•LIMS verifies that the column specified is available on the grid.  If the column is greater than the last column on the grid, the LIMS returns the last error:
"Specified point is outside the grid columns."

•LIMS updates the cell on the grid without regard to format restriction.

NOTE: This function will not work with the Result Entry grids.

Also, this function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following code sets the first field on the grid to 47.

X = 2
y = 1
cellValue = "47"
status = SetCellValue(x, y, cellValue)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetGridCellColors  

Purpose:

The SetGridCellColors function sets the background cell colors in a user grid.

Syntax:

status = SetGridCellColors(inputArray)

inputArray – array of values specifying cell colors.  Allowed values are 1-Black, 2-Blue, 3-Brown, 4-Cyan, 5-Dark Blue, 6-Dark Cyan, 7-Dark Gray, 8-Dark Green, 9-Dark Pink, 10-Dark Red, 11-Gray 12- Green, 13-Pale Gray, 14-Pink, 15-Red, 16-White, 17-Yellow.

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example sets the cell colors for the first 5 columns in the first 3 rows:

inputarray1[1] = 16

inputarray1[2] = 2

inputarray1[3] = 16

inputarray1[4] = 11

inputarray1[5] = 2

    For i = 1 to 3

        For j = 1 to 5

            inputarray[j,i] = inputarray1[j]

        next j

    Next i

status = SetGridCellColors(inputArray)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetGridColumnHeaders  

Purpose:

The SetGridColumnHeaders function sets the column headers in a user grid.

Syntax:

status = SetGridColumnHeaders(inputArray)

inputArray – array of values specifying Column Headers.

Comments:

Example:

The following example sets the column headers for the first 5 columns in a user grid:

inputarray[1] = "Name"

inputarray[2] = "Project Link"

inputarray[3] = "Investigation Link"

inputarray[4] = "Batch Link"

inputarray[5] = "General Field"

statusGCH = SetGridColumnHeaders(inputArray)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetGridFonts  

Purpose:

The SetGridFonts function sets the fonts in a user grid.

Syntax:

status = SetGridFonts(inputArray)

inputArray – array of values specifying that values to be entered in a user grid.   Valid values are: Bold, Italic, Underscore, Strikeout, Normal.

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example sets the cell font to bold for the first 4 columns in the first 2 rows of the user grid:

For i = 1 to 2

    For j = 1 to 4

        inputarrayF[ i ,j ] = "Bold"

    next j

Next i

statusGF = SetGridFonts(inputArrayF)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

status = SetGridRowLabels(inputArray)

inputarray2[1] = "Row 1"

SetGridTextColors  

Purpose:

The SetGridTextColors function sets the font colors for cells in a user grid.

Syntax:

status = SetGridTextColors(inputArray)

inputArray – array of values specifying cell font colors.  1-Black, 2-Blue, 3-Brown, 4-Cyan, 5-Dark Blue, 6-Dark Cyan, 7-Dark Gray, 8-Dark Green, 9-Dark Pink, 10-Dark Red, 11-Gray 12- Green, 13-Pale Gray, 14-Pink, 15-Red, 16-White, 17-Yellow

Comments:

This function does NOT save changes to the database.
