•Checks the lotNumber.  If not valid, the system triggers the error: “Lot number not valid: lotNumber”. If the lot does not exit, the system triggers the error: “Lot does not exist: lotNumber”.

•They system refreshes the lot.

NOTE:  A database commit is not part of this function.


Example:

lotNumber = 2

status = RefreshLot(lotNumber, "T")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

RemoveLotSource    

Purpose:

The RemoveLotSource function removes a source lot from a lot.

Syntax:

status = RemoveLotSource(lotNumber, samplingPoint, sourceLot)

lotNumber – the lot number from which a source lot is to be removed

samplingPoint – the sampling point from which a source it to be removed

sourceLot – the lot number of the source lot to be removed

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the lot with the specified lotNumber.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified lot does not exist".

•If a value is specified for samplingPoint, LIMS will retrieve it.  If no such sampling point is found for the lot’s product grade, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified grade sampling point does not exist on the lot".

•If no value is specified for samplingPoint, LIMS will use the current sampling point for the lot.

•If the specified sourceLot is not already a source for the lot, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified source does not exist".

•LIMS marks the source lot association for deletion.  (The source lot itself is not marked for deletion, only its association as a source for the specified lotNumber.)

•If an audit reason is specified for the lot table, LIMS will associate it with the pending deletion.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example removes lot 412 as a source for the current sampling point of lot 501.

status = RemoveLotSource(501, "", 412)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SplitLot    

Purpose:

The SplitLot function splits a lot.

Syntax:

splitLotNumber = SplitLot(lotNumber,templateName,fieldsArray,valuesArray)

lotNumber – the lot number of the lot to be split

templateName – the name of the template to be used for creating the new lot

fieldsArray – an array of field names whose values need to be set for the new lot

valuesArray – an array of values to be assigned to the corresponding fields

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If LIMS is unable to find a lot template with the specified templateName, it will trigger the error "Lot template does not exist".

•If the value of lotNumber is missing, zero, or cannot be converted to a positive number, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid lot number specified".

•If LIMS is unable to find a lot with the specified lotNumber, it will trigger the error "Lot does not exist".

•LIMS splits the lot and assigns the values in the valuesArray to the corresponding fields in the fieldsArray of the new lot.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Could not split the lot".

•LIMS saves the new lot to the database using audit type "CreateLot" and using the audit reason for the lot table, if one is specified.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error committing lot record".

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example splits lot 127 using template "TPL1" and sets the customer equal to "ACME".

myFields[1] = "CUSTOMER"
myValues[1] = "ACME"
splitLotNumber = SplitLot(127, "TPL1", myFields, myValues)

Returns:

The function returns the lot number of the new lot if it completes successfully.  It returns the value zero if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateLotFld    

Purpose:

The UpdateLotFld function updates a field of a lot.

Syntax:

status = UpdateLotFld(lotNumber,field,value)

lotNumber – the lot number of the lot to be updated

field – the name of the field to be updated

value – the value to be assigned to the specified field

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If LIMS is unable to find a lot with the specified lotNumber, it will trigger the error "Lot does not exist".

•If the lot table does not include a field with the specified field name, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified field is a link field (foreign key), LIMS will validate the specified value against the target table of the link.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid value for lot record".

•If the specified field is a date field and if the specified value cannot be converted to a date, LIMS will use a null value instead.

•If the specified field is a boolean field and if the specified value is missing or does not represent the value true, LIMS will use the value false instead.

•If the specified field is an interval field and if the specified value is missing or blank, LIMS will use the value zero instead.

•LIMS assigns the specified value to the specified field.  If an audit reason is specified for the lot table, LIMS will assign it to the pending transaction.  LIMS also assigns the audit type "UpdateLot" to the pending transaction.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example sets the customer of lot 733 to "ACME".

status = UpdateLotFld(733, "CUSTOMER", "ACME")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateLotSamplingPointFld    

Purpose:

The UpdateLotSamplingPointFld function updates the specified field of the specified lot sampling point with the specified value.

Syntax:

status = UpdateLotSamplingPointFld(lotNumber,samplingPoint,grade,field,value)

lotNumber – the lot number of the sampling point to be updated

samplingPoint – the sampling point name of the sampling point to be updated

grade – the grade of the sampling point to be updated

field – the name of the field to be updated

value – the value to be stored in the specified field

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no lot number is specified, or if no lot exists with the specified lot number, LIMS will trigger the error "Lot does not exist".

•If no sampling point is found for the specified combination of lot number, sampling point, and grade, LIMS will trigger the error "Lot Sampling Point does not exist".

•If the specified field name does not exist in the lot sampling point table, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified field is a link field, and if the specified value is not blank and does not exist in the corresponding link table, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid value for lot sampling point record".

•LIMS updates the specified lot sampling point field as follows:

•If the specified field is a date field, and if the specified value cannot be interpreted as a valid date, LIMS will apply a null value instead.

•If the specified field is a boolean field, LIMS will apply the value True if and only if the specified value argument begins with the letter "T".  Otherwise, LIMS will apply the value False.

•If the specified field is an interval field, and if the specified value is blank, LIMS will apply the value 0 (zero) instead.

•Otherwise, LIMS will apply the value as specified.

•If an audit reason is specified for the lot sample type table, LIMS will apply it to the pending transaction.

•LIMS applies the audit type "UpdateLot" to the pending transaction.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example updates the disposition of the sampling point "TOP" of lot 1493 and grade "NONE" to True.

status = UpdateLotSamplingPointFld(1493, "TOP", "NONE", "DISPOSITION", "T")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

 

28.3.27  Mail Functions
 

Function

CloseSMTPConnection

MailReport

SendMail

SendSMTPMailMessage

 

 

CloseSMTPConnection  

Purpose:

The CloseSMTPConnection function closes the SMTP link immediately after sending messages

Syntax:

status = CloseSMTPConnection()

Comments:

 

Example:

status = CloseSMTPConnection ()

Returns:

status = true if the function is successful, false otherwise.

 

MailReport    

Purpose:

The MailReport function runs a report and e-mails the report to a list of users.  This function uses Crystal Reports to mail the report using the export command in Crystal.

Syntax:

status = MailReport(reportName, argArray, valArray, toArray, ccArray, subject, message, languagePrefix, locale)

reportName – a string representing the name of the report query tag to be executed.

The argArray and valArray arrays are used to specify the filter criteria for record selection for the report.

argArray – one dimensional array used to specify the table_name.field_name for the filter criteria.

valArray – one dimensional array used to specify the filter values.

toArray – one dimensional array used to specify the email addresses of the recipients.

ccArray – one dimensional array used to specify the email address of carbon copy recipients (optional).

subject – string input to be used as the subject line of the email.

message – string input to be used as the main body of the email.

languagePrefix – the language prefix for the NLS translation to be displayed for Text Objects in the Crystal Report (optional).

locale – the Preferred Viewing Local to be passed from LabWare to Crystal Reports.  The Crystal Reports functions “ContentLocale” and “SelectionLocale” will be set to the specified value.

Comments:

The function performs the following:

1.  Compares the size of the argArray and valArray.  If not the same size, the lastError variable is set to “Error:  Arguments and value are not the same size”.

2.  If a valid Query Tag does not exist for the specified reportName, the lastError is set to “Query tag does not exist:  <reportName>”.

3.  Evaluates languagePrefix.  If the language prefix is not specified for the function, the language prefix for the logged in User will be applied.

Example:

The following example will email the Sample Summary report for Sample 49 to “USER_A”.  The subject line in the email will display “Sample Summary Report – Sample 49” and the body of the email will display “The Sample Summary Report for Sample 49 is attached”.  A carbon copy of the e-mail with attached report will be sent to “USER_B”.  The report will display the German NLS translation for Text Objects in the report.  The Crystal Reports functions “ContentLocale” and “SelectionLocale” will be set to “de_DE” respectively.

reportName = “SAMPLE_SUMMARY”

argArray[1] = “SAMPLE.SAMPLE_NUMBER”

valArray[1] = 49

toArray[1] = “USER_A”

ccArray[1] = “USER_B”

subject = “Sample Summary Report – Sample 49”

message = “The Sample Summary Report for Sample 49 is attached”

languagePrefix = “DE_”

locale = “ceLocaleGerman”

status = MailReport(reportName, argArray, valArray, toArray, ccArray, subject, message, languagePrefix, locale)

Returns:

The status variable is set to true if the function completes successfully, else status is set to false.

 

SendMail    

Purpose:

The SendMail function sends an electronic mail message via SMTP.

This function is provided for backward compatibility in situations where a customer replaces a prior mail interface (MAPI, VIM, or Notes) with the SMTP interface.  For new LIMS Basic scripts, LabWare recommends use of the SendSMTPMailMessage function.

Syntax:

status = SendMail(recipientsArray, subject, noteText, fileListArray, ccArray)

recipientsArray – an array that contains the internet email addresses to which the message will be sent

subject – the subject of the message

noteText – the body of the message

fileListArray – an array that contains the full paths of the files to be attached to the message

ccArray – an array that contains the list of recipients to populate the cc field of the sent email (optional)

Comments:

The following function call:

status = SendMail(recipientsArray, subject, noteText, fileListArray)

is functionally equivalent to the following function call:

status = SendSMTPMailMessage(subject, noteText, recipientsArray, , , , , fileListArray)

(Refer to the section below on the SendSMTPMailMessage function for a detailed description of this function).

Example:

The following example prompts for a subject and then sends a two-line email to John Perry.

cr =str(chr(10))

subject =InputBox("Enter the subject","Subject",)

noteText="This is the first line of the message" + cr
noteText = noteText + "This is the second line"

recipientsArray[1] = "John Perry <perry@labware.com>"

fileListArray[1] = "C:\LabWare.jpg"

status = SendMail(recipientsArray, subject, noteText, fileListArray)

if(status)then
   MsgBox("Success!")
else
   MsgBox(lastError)
end if

Returns:

 

status = true if the function is successful, false otherwise.

 

SendSMTPMailMessage    

Purpose:

The SendSMTPMailMessage function sends an electronic mail message via SMTP.

Syntax:

status = SendSMTPMailMessage(subject, messageText, toList, ccList, fromAddress, mailServerName, fromServerName, attachmentList, closeConnection)

subject – the subject of the message

messageText – the body of the message

toList – an array that contains internet email addresses to which the message will be sent

ccList – an array that contains internet email addresses to which a carbon copy of the message will be sent

fromAddress – the internet email address, without additional formatting such as angle brackets, that will be shown as the sender of the message

mailServerName – the SMTP relay server through which the message will be sent.  This argument is optional.  If it is not specified, the SMTPMailServerName value set in the Server Configuration dialog will be used.  (Note: the server name can be specified in the form of an IP address.)

fromServerName – the server name that is supplied during the HELO or EHLO greeting.  This argument is optional.  If it is not specified, the SMTPFromServerName value set in the server configuration will be used.

attachmentList – an array that contains the full paths of the files to be attached to the message

closeConnection - This is "T" or "F" and specifies whether or not the SMTP connection should be closed after sending the email.  If it is not specified, the value set in the server configuration will be used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the value for fromAddress is blank or missing, LIMS will use the email address stored in the user record of the current user.  If the current user has no email address, LIMS will trigger the error "The user does not have an email address."

•If the value of mailServerName is blank or missing, and if no value for SMTP Mail Server was specified in the Server Configuration, LIMS will trigger the error "No SMTP Mail Server specified in the Server Configuration."

•If the value of fromServerName is blank or missing, and if no value for SMTP From Server was specified in the Server Configuration, LIMS will trigger the error "No SMTP From Server specified in the Server Configuration."

•If the toList array is missing or has no entries, LIMS will trigger the error "No toList specified."

•If an attachmentList is specified, and if any of the elements in the attachmentList is not the path of a file that is accessible, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified file attachment does not exist."

•If an SMTP connection is already open, LIMS will use it.  Otherwise, LIMS will open a new connection as follows.

•If both an SMTP Server Username and an SMTP Server Password were specified in the Server Configuration, LIMS will open an EHLO connection.  If the connection fails, LIMS will trigger one of the following errors.

Socket timed out waiting for response to EHLO

Unexpected response to EHLO

Server did not respond to the EHLO request

Did not receive expected 334 response for username

Unexpected response to AUTH LOGIN username

Did not receive expected 334 response for password

Unexpected response to AUTH LOGIN password

Login rejected

Otherwise, LIMS will open a HELO connection.  If the connection fails, LIMS will trigger one of the following errors.

Socket timed out waiting for response to HELO

Unexpected response to HELO

LIMS sends the message.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger one of the following errors.

Socket timed out waiting for response to MAIL FROM

Unexpected response to MAIL FROM

Socket timed out waiting for response to RCPT TO

Unexpected response to RCPT TO to toList

 

 

Unexpected response to RCPT TO ccList

Socket timed out waiting for response to DATA

Unexpected response to DATA

Error sending from: information

Error sending to: information

Error sending cc: information

Error sending subject

Error sending date

Error sending MINE info

Error sending multi-part content type

Error sending starting boundary

Error sending content type

Error sending message text

Error sending end body boundary

Error sending attachment content type

Error sending attachment content encoding

Error sending attachment content disposition

Error sending ending attachment boundary

Socket timed out waiting for response to "."

Unexpected response to "."

NOTE: The error messages shown in steps 6 and 7 above cannot be triggered on demand.  In other words, constructing test cases that will trigger these errors is difficult if not impossible.  The messages are provided here for two reasons.

One, if any error is encountered, the lists in steps 6 and 7 will help confirm whether the error originated in the SendSMTPMailMessage function.  The most likely cause of such an error is an interruption in the connection between LIMS and the relay server while the message was being sent.  The recommended course of action is to try re-sending the message.

Two, if the same error occurs consistently, i.e., can be reproduced reliably, there may be an incompatibility between LabWare’s implementation of SMTP and the implementation of SMTP on the relay server.  This should be brought to LabWare’s attention via the usual support mechanisms.

 

 

 

Example:

The following example prompts for a subject and then sends a two-line email to John Perry with a copy to Jon Raiford.

cr = str(chr(10))

subject = InputBox("Enter the subject","Subject",)

messageText = "This is the first line of the message" + cr
messageText = messageText + "This is the second line"

toList[1] = "John Perry <perry@labware.com>"
ccList[1] = "Jon Raiford <raiford@labware.com>"
fromAddress = "padin@labware.com"
mailServerName = "mail.labware.com"
fromServerName = "lwlims.labware.com"
attachmentList[1] = "C:\LabWare.jpg"

status = SendSMTPMailMessage(subject, messageText, toList, ccList, fromAddress, mailServerName, fromServerName, attachmentList)

if (status) then
   MsgBox("Success!")
else
   MsgBox(lastError)
end if

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

 

28.3.28  Miscellaneous Functions
 

Function

IpCloseLink

IpListenerAck

IpListenerStart

IpListenerStop

IpSend

NullValue

 

IpCloseLink    

Purpose:

The IpCloseLink function closes a Windows Socket.

Syntax:

status = IpCloseLink(ipAddress, portNumber)

ipAddress – the IP Address associated with the socket.

portNumber – the port number associated with the socket.

Comments:

This function is used to close the socket opened by the IpSend function.

If the socket is not open, LIMS returns the error:

"Specified socket is not open."

Example:

The following example closes the socket opened by the IpSend function shown:

 

ipAddress = "192.168.1.44"

portNumber = 9090

message = "test message<eom>"

Terminator = "<term>"

status = IpSend(ipAddress, portNumber, message,, terminator)

status = IpCloseLink(ipAddress, portNumber)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

IpListenerAck    

Purpose:

The IpListenerAck function sends an acknowledge message.

Syntax:

status = IpListenerAck(ackMessage)

ackMessage – the acknowledge message.

Comments:

This function is used to send an acknowledgement message in response to a received message.

LIMS verifies that an IP connection has received a message.  If not, LIMS returns the error: "No IP connection to Ack."

LIMS sends the acknowledge message.  If not successful, LIMS returns one of the following errors:
"No socket defined."
"Socket is closed."
"Ack failed."

Example:

The following example returns the message "listener got your message<term>" to a message received on port 9090, following the IpListenerStart function shown:

 

portNum = 9090

status = IpListenerStart(portNum, 1, "<eom>", "IP-RECV", basicVariable)

 

Then, in the subroutine IP-RECV:

ackMessage = "listener got your message<term>"

status = IpListenerAck(ackMessage )

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

IpListenerStart    

Purpose:

The IpListenerStart function starts a listener on a port, and calls a LIMS Basic routine whenever a message is received on that port.

Syntax:

status = IpListenerStart(portNum, msgTimeOut, msgTerminator, basicRoutine, basicVariable)

portNum – the port number on which to open a listener.

msgTimeOut – the timeout (in seconds) for received messages.  This is the time allowed for a message to complete, once it has started being received.

msgTerminator – the terminator for received messages (optional).

basicRoutine – LIMS Basic subroutine to be called when a message is received.

basicVariable – LIMS Basic variable set to received message on call to basicRoutine.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that portNum is an integer between 0 and 65535. If not, LIMS returns the error:  "Invalid port number"

•LIMS checks that there is not already a listener open on the specified port.  If there is, LIMS returns the error:  "Another listener already opened on specified port."

•LIMS opens the port.  If not successful, LIMS returns one of the following errors:
"Failed to obtain socket."
"Specified port would not open and listen."

•Then, for any message received on portNum, one of the following occurs:

•If no value is specified for msgTerminator, then after msgTimeOut seconds, the subroutine basicRoutine is called, with basicVariable containing the received message.

•If a value is specified for msgTerminator and the message contains msgTerminator, the subroutine basicRoutine is called, with basicVariable containing the received message, up to and including msgTerminator.

NOTE: If the message contains more than one instance of msgTerminator, the subroutine basicRoutine will be called once for each instance.

If a value is specified for msgTerminator and the message does not contain msgTerminator, then after msgTimeOut seconds, the message buffer is flushed and no further action is taken.


Example:

The following example opens a listener on port 9090, listening for messages terminated by "<eom>", with a 1 second timeout on incoming messages.  When a message is successfully received, the subroutine IP-RECV will be called with the variable receivedMessage containing the message.

 

portNum = 9090

msgTimeOut = 1

msgTerminator = "<eom>"

basicRoutine = "IP-RECV"

basicVariable = "receivedMessage"

status = IpListenerStart(portNum, msgTimeOut, msgTerminator, basicRoutine, basicVariable)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

IpListenerStop    

Purpose:

The IpListenerStop function stops the listener on a port.

Syntax:

status = IpListenerStop(portNum)

portNum – the port number on which to stop the listener

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that a listener is open on the specified port.  If not, LIMS returns the error:
"No IP Listener open on specified port number"

•LIMS closes the listener.  If not successful, LIMS returns one of the following errors:
"failed to close listener … not open."
"failed to close listener."

Example:

The following example closes the listener on port 9090:

portNum = 9090

status = IpListenerStop(portNum)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

IpSend    

Purpose:

The IpSend function sends a message to a server and waits for a returned message in reply.

Syntax:

status = IpSend(ipAddress, portNumber, message, timeOut, terminator, basicVariable, closeFlag)

ipAddress – the IP Address of the server.

portNumber – the port number on the server.

message – the message to be sent to the server.

timeOut – the timeout (in seconds) for receiving a reply (optional, defaults to 0).  If 0, no timeout is set.

terminator – the terminator for the reply (optional).

basicVariable – variable containing the returned message. If not specified, defaults to "ipSendReply".

closeFlag – Boolean setting defining if the socket used to connect to the server is to be closed on receipt of a reply.  This value defaults to false if unspecified.  Values starting with "T" or "Y" are interpreted as true.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks if a socket connected to portNumber on ipAddress is already open.  If not, LIMS opens a socket connected to portNumber on ipAddress.  If unsuccessful, LIMS returns the error:
"ERROR – Socket could not be opened"

•LIMS sends "message" and waits for a reply.

•When a reply is received or timeout occurs, if closeFlag is true, LIMS closes the socket.  If no reply is received by timeOut seconds, LIMS returns the error:
"ERROR – No reply received"

•LIMS returns the reply message in basicVariable.

•If no value is specified for terminator, the reply is returned after timeOut seconds.

•If a value is specified for terminator and the message contains the terminator, the reply up to and including the terminator is returned.

•If a value is specified for terminator and the message does not contain the terminator, LIMS returns the error:
"ERROR – Timed out. Specified terminator not found."

Example:

The following example sends the message "test message<eom>" to the server on port 9090 at IP address 192.168.1.44, and waits 10 seconds for a reply which is terminated by "<term>".  The reply is saved in the variable replyMessage.

ipAddress = "192.168.1.44"

portNumber = 9090

message = "test message<eom>"

timeOut = 10

Terminator = "<term>"

basicVariable = "replyMessage"

closeFlag = "F"

status = IpSend(ipAddress, portNumber, message, timeOut, terminator, basicVariable, closeFlag)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

NullValue

Purpose:

The NullValue function sets the value of a variable to a Null value.

Syntax:

value = NullValue()

value – is the variable that will be set to null

Comments:

 

Example:

The following example sets the variable myVar to null then check using the isNull function.

myVar = NullValue()

status = IsNull(myVar)

MsgBox(status)

Returns:

 

The function sets the variable to a null value.

 

 

 

28.3.29  ODBC Functions
 

Function

IsODBCOpen

ODBCClose

ODBCColumns

ODBCOpenWithConnectString

ODBCQuery

ODBCSourceNames

IsODBCOpen    

Purpose:

The IsODBCOpen function returns a Boolean indicating whether an ODBC link is open.

Syntax:

status = IsODBCOpen(linkName)

Comments:

linkName is the name given to the ODBC link in the OdbcOpen function.

Example:

status = IsODBCOpen("myOdbcLink")

Returns:

status = true if the ODBC link is open, false otherwise.

 

ODBCClose    

Purpose:

The ODBCClose function closes an ODBC link.

Syntax:

status = ODBCClose(linkName)

linkName is the name given to the ODBC link in the OdbcOpen function. 

Comments:

The link will close automatically when the LIMS application is closed.

Example:

status = ODBCClose("myOdbcLink")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

ODBCColumns    

Purpose:

The ODBCColumns function returns an array containing the names of the columns generated from an ODBCQuery function.

Syntax:

status = ODBCColumns(linkName, "arrayName")

linkName is the name given to the ODBC link in the OdbcOpen function.

arrayName is a one-dimensional array that contains the names of the column headers. 

Comments:

This function must be executed after an ODBCQuery function and before an ODBCClose function.

Example:

status = ODBCColumns("myOdbcLink","arrayColumns")

Returns:

arrayColumns[1] = "NAME"

 

ODBCDateTimeStamp    

Purpose:

The ODBCDateTimeStamp function converts a datetime data type to a format compatible with the current ODBC driver.

Syntax:

timeStamp = OdbcDateTimeStamp(aDateTime)

Comments:

This function is useful for constructing SQL statements. 

Example:

now = datetime()

timeStamp = OdbcDateTimeStamp(now)

Returns:

timeStamp = {ts ‘1998-04-15 20:21:18’}

 

ODBCGetFile    

Purpose:

The ODBCGetFile function is used to download a LIMS Database File using ODBC into the computer drive.

Syntax:

status = ODBCGetFile(linkname,query, fileName)

linkname - the link_name defined in the ODBCOpen function call (refer to the LIMS Basic reference manual for more on the ODBCOpen function)

query – a SQL query to identify the DB_FILE record to be downloaded

fileName - the name of the file to be created in the file system.  The file name must be specified.  If the path is not specified, the current directory will be used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS will check the link_name .  If the link_name does not specify an open ODBC link, LIMS will trigger the error "Link is not open."

•The LIMS will check the fileName.  If the fileName already exists , LIMS will trigger the error "File name already exists: ' , fileName."

•The LIMS will execute the query to extract the db file record.  If a file is not created from the query, LIMS will trigger the error "Query failed.".  If a file is created but has file size 0, the file will be deleted and, LIMS will trigger the error "Query return nothing."

NOTE:  The ODBCOpen LIMS Basic function must be used prior to this function to open the ODBC connection.

Example:

The following example downloads a Database File "0000000007.ppt" into computer drive "C:\DBFiles\".  The created file is named "M0190.ppt".

query =  "SELECT DB_FILES.FILE_NAME FROM DB_FILES WHERE DB_FILES.FILE_NAME='0000000007.ppt’"

FileName = "DBFiles\M0190.ppt"

status = ODBCGetFile(linkName, query, filePathName)

Returns:

 

If successful returns True else returns False.

 

ODBCName    

Purpose:

The ODBCName function returns the ODBC Database Server Name as entered in the Server Configuration Manager dialog.  This is the dialog that is accessed via the menu item Configure / System / Server.

Syntax:

name = ODBCName()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

LIMS returns the ODBC name as entered in the Server Configuration Manager dialog.

Example:

The following example returns the ODBC name.

name = ODBCName()

Returns:

The function returns the ODBC name.

 

ODBCOpen    

Purpose:

The ODBCOpen function opens an ODBC data source or Access® mdb database.

Syntax:

status = ODBCOpen(linkName, odbcSourceName,userName,password,passwordEncrypted)

linkName – a user defined string that names the link so it can be referenced later (e.g., in ODBCQuery)

odbcSourceName – the ODBC Data Source Name as specified in the ODBC Setup dialog in the Windows Control Panel.  This parameter can also be the name of an mdb file with path (only works with Access® databases).

userName – the username for logging on to the database.  This argument is optional.  It must be specified if the database requires authentication and if the username is not already specified in the Control Panel.

password – the username for logging on to the database.  This argument is optional.  It must be specified if the database requires authentication and if the password is not already specified in the Control Panel.

passwordEncrypted – a boolean value that specifies whether the password is encrypted.  This argument is optional.  If missing, the value "F" (false) will be used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the value of passwordEncrypted is true, LIMS will perform the following steps.

•If the specified password value is not an encrypted string, LIMS will trigger the error "password is not encrypted".

•LIMS decrypts the password.

•LIMS opens the specified data source.

The ODBC link will remain open until it is closed using the ODBCClose function or the LIMS application is terminated.

Example:

The following example opens the ORALIMS data source.

status = ODBCOpen("myOdbcLink", "ORALIMS", "pclims",  "mypw")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ODBCOpenWithConnectString

Purpose:

The ODBCOpenWithConnectString function opens an ODBC link using a user specified connect string.

Syntax:

status = ODBCOpenWithConnectString(linkName, connectString, encryptedValue)

linkName – a user defined string given to the ODBC link so that so it can be referenced by other functions

connectString– user specified connect string for logging on to the database.  

encryptedValue – the encrypted value is provided to allow substitution in the connect string with an encrypted value (typically a password).  All occurrences of %1 in the connect string will be replaced by the unencrypted value.

NOTE: The encrypted_value must be encrypted using the Encrypt  LIMS Basic function with no pass phrase (i.e. encryptedString = Encrypt(unencryptedString,))  It will not work if the EncryptPassword function is used to encrypt the string.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If encrypted_value has a value, LIMS will check that it is an encrypted string, If not properly encrypted, LIMS will trigger the error "password is not encrypted".

•LIMS decrypts the encryptedValue and inserts the values in the connect_string.

•LIMS opens the link using the connectString.

The ODBC link will remain open until it is closed using the ODBCClose function or the LIMS application is terminated.

 Example:

The following example demonstrates the function:

connectString =  InputBox("Please enter the ODBC Connect string", "LIMS Basic", ";DSN=")

status = ODBCOpenWithConnectString(linkName,connectString,)

if (status) then

    msgbox("Connection Established")

else

    msgbox("Connection Failed")

endif

status = ODBCclose(linkName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ODBCQuery    

Purpose:

The ODBCQuery function executes a query to an ODBC database.

Syntax:

status = ODBCQuery(linkName, query, "arrayName")

linkName – the name given to the ODBC link in the OdbcOpen function.

arrayName – the name of a multi-dimensional array where the results of the query are returned.

Comments: 

 

Example:

query = "select name, description from analysis"

status = ODBCQuery("myOdbcLink", query, "resultArray")

Returns:

resultArray[1,1] = "LOD"

resultArray[1,2] = "Loss on Drying"

 

ODBCSourceNames    

Purpose:

The ODBCSourceNames function

Syntax:

status = ODBCSourceNames("arrayName")

arrayName – the name of the array to be filled with data source names.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

If no array name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No array name specified".

LIMS creates a LIMS Basic array with the specified name and fills it with all ODBC datasource names that are defined in the Microsoft Windows operating system environment in which LIMS is currently executing.

LIMS returns the value true.

Example:

The following example populates the array mySources with all available ODBC datasource names.

status = ODBCSourceNames("mySources")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ODBCWriteFile   

Purpose:

The ODBCWriteFile function stores the contents of a file into an external database.

Syntax:

status = ODBCWriteFile(linkName, fileName, table, fileField, keyField, keyValue)

linkName - the link_name defined in the ODBCOpen function call (refer to the LIMS Basic reference manual for more on the ODBCOpen function)

fileName - the name of the file to be copied in the file system.  The file name must be specified.  If the path is not specified, the current directory will be used

table –the name of the database table in which the file contents will be stored

fileField– the field in the specified table in which the file contents will be stored.  Ideally, this field should be a BLOB field (or equivalent for the relevant database type) otherwise the file information may not be copied correctly.

keyField– the name of the key field for the specified table (multiple keys not supported at this time)

keyValue– the value of the key field corresponding to the new record entry where the file contents will be placed (a new record is created with this key value and the file contents are copied into the fileField)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the linkName, If not valid, LIMS will trigger the error "Link is not open".

•LIMS checks the fileName, If not found, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified file does not exist: fileName".

•LIMS attempts to write the file contents to the external database.  If not successful, LIMS will trigger the error "Query failed".

CAUTION: This function will create a new entry with the specified name and will copy the file information to the specified field.  However, no other field information is passed to the new record.  It will not update existing records.


 Example:

The following example copies the file A_PDF.PDF to The EXT_CONTENTS field in "MY_TABLE":

linkName = "MY_ODBC"

fileName = curdir() + "\M0433\A_PDF.PDF"

table = "MY_TABLE"

fileField = "EXT_CONTENTS"

keyField = "NAME"

keyValue = "ABC"

statusO = ODBCWriteFile(linkName, fileName, table, fileField, keyField, keyValue)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

28.3.30  OLE Automation Functions
Function

OleCreate

OleGetProperty

OleInvokeMethod

OleRelease

OleReleaseAll

OleSetProperty

 

OLE Automation is a generic technology that allows one application to control another application. Many applications offer an application programming interface (API) that allows for control by another application, but most of these APIs are proprietary. OLE Automation is neither the first nor the last technology that offers a generic interface. LabWare LIMS supports the following.

 

SendKeys

Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE)

OLE Automation

Web Services

 

The primary reason why support for OLE Automation has been added to LabWare LIMS is because it offers a means of controlling Microsoft Excel in a manner that cannot be interrupted or influenced by the end user. Both SendKeys and DDE work in the foreground, i.e., they communicate with an application (such as Excel) in more or less the same manner as the user’s keyboard and mouse. Suppose the user runs a LIMS Basic subroutine that uses SendKeys or DDE to manipulate a spreadsheet. The user can potentially influence the behavior of this subroutine by entering keystrokes in Excel while the LIMS Basic subroutine is running.

OLE Automation provides the ability to communicate with another application without regard to what the user is doing in the foreground process of that other application. In the case of Excel, it even provides the ability to keep Excel invisible while a LIMS Basic subroutine is executing.  For further information on OLE Automation, you may wish to start with its entry in Wikipedia, at:

 http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OLE_Automation

OLE Automation is based on Microsoft’s Component Object Model (COM). An object model describes one or more objects. Each object has properties and methods. LIMS Basic can be used to obtain the value of a property, to change the value of a property, and to invoke a method. It is the responsibility of each application to describe its own object model. As an example, the object model for Microsoft Excel 2000 is described in a file named VBAXL9.CHM. This file can usually be found in the following location.

"C:\Program Files\Microsoft Office\Office\1033\VBAXL9.CHM"

Interacting with another application via OLE Automation involves the use of pointers to objects. LIMS Basic does not support the concept of pointers. LIMS keeps all OLE Automation pointers in memory, and provides handles that LIMS Basic subroutines can use to refer to those pointers. The handles themselves are simply integers.

Each pointer uses external memory, i.e., non-LIMS memory. When a LIMS Basic script finishes, LIMS automatically releases all LIMS memory that was used by the script. LIMS does not automatically release non-LIMS memory, and therefore, LIMS Basic scripts that use OLE Automation must use either the OleRelease function or the OleReleaseAll function to release memory.

Note that releasing memory does not cause the OLE Automation server application to close. For example, each time LIMS initiates a connection to Microsoft Excel, a new instance of the Microsoft Excel application is created. If this instance is not explicitly closed by invoking its quit method, it will remain open even after memory is released.

28.3.30.1   OLE Automation Example 1
Below is an example LIMS Basic script that exercises the various OLE Automation functions. This example uses a spreadsheet that is used by LabWare staff as a timesheet. Not counting comment lines, this example includes 20 LIMS Basic statements. When error handling is added, the full length of the script, including comment lines, expands to over 100 lines. (Note that much of the error handling can be condensed by using the new signalLastError argument in the Subroutine function.)

 

' Example of OLE Automation using Microsoft Excel

 

' Start the Microsoft Excel application

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

 

' Make the application visible so we can see it

status = OleSetProperty(xlApplication,"Visible",true)

 

' Tell the application to not show alerts

' This is needed to be able to close workbooks without saving

status = OleSetProperty(xlApplication,"DisplayAlerts",false)

 

' Ask the application for its Workbooks object

xlWorkbooks = OleGetProperty(xlApplication,"Workbooks")

 

' Ask the Workbooks object to open the timesheet workbook

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open","c:\timesheet.xls")

 

' Ask the Workbooks object for its first workbook

xlWorkbook = OleGetProperty(xlWorkbooks,"Item",1)

 

' Ask the Workbook object for its Sheets object

xlSheets = OleGetProperty(xlWorkbook,"Sheets")

 

' Ask the Sheets object for its first Worksheet object

xlWorksheet = OleGetProperty(xlSheets,"Item",1)

 

' Enter my name in cell C2

xlRange = OleGetProperty(xlWorksheet,"Range","C2")

status = OleSetProperty(xlRange,"Value","Erick de Vries")

 

' I worked 3 hours on the first Monday

xlRange = OleGetProperty(xlWorksheet,"Range","E10")

status = OleSetProperty(xlRange,"Value",3)

 

' I worked 4 hours on the second Tuesday

xlRange = OleGetProperty(xlWorksheet,"Range","E19")

status = OleSetProperty(xlRange,"Value",4)

 

' Determine the total number of hours

xlRange = OleGetProperty(xlWorksheet,"Range","E49")

totalHours = OleGetProperty(xlRange,"Value")

 

' Close the application

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Quit")

 

' Release all memory associated with OLE automation

status = OleReleaseAll()

 

' Display the total number of hours

MsgBox("Total Hours: " & totalHours)

Return

28.3.30.2   OLE Automation Example 2
Below is an example LIMS Basic script that executes two macros in Microsoft Excel. The first macro requires no arguments. The second macro requires two arguments.

 

' Example of OLE Automation using Microsoft Excel

 

' This example uses a spreadsheet named ExcelMacroExample.xls

' which contains macros named Example1 and Example2

 

fileName = "C:\ExcelMacroExample.xls"

 

' Start the Microsoft Excel application

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

 

' Make the application visible so we can see it

status = OleSetProperty(xlApplication,"Visible",true)

 

' Tell the application to not show alerts

' This is needed to be able to close workbooks without saving

status = OleSetProperty(xlApplication,"DisplayAlerts",false)

 

' Ask the application for its Workbooks object

xlWorkbooks = OleGetProperty(xlApplication,"Workbooks")

 

' Ask the Workbooks object to open the workbook that contains the example macros

xlWorkbook = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open",fileName)

 

' Run the first example macro

value1 = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Run","Example1")

 

' Run the second example macro

array2[1] = "Example2"

array2[2] = 10

array2[3] = 20

value2 = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Run",array2)

 

Breakpoint("Verify the contents of the variables and the spreadsheet")

 

' Close the application

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Quit")

 

' Release all memory associated with OLE automation

status = OleReleaseAll()

Return

 

OleCreate   

Purpose:

The OleCreate function creates and returns a pointer to an OLE automation server.

Syntax:

handle = OleCreate(versionIndependentProgID, executionContext)

versionIndependentProgID – the version-independent Program ID of the automation server to which a connection is to be established

executionContext – the context in which the automation server runs.  This is usually left blank-see note below.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no program ID is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No version independent program ID specified".

•LIMS retrieves application information for the specified program ID.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Application not found".  This error indicates that either the program ID is invalid or the program is not installed on the computer where the current LabWare LIMS session is running.

•LIMS connects to the application.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Unable to create controller".

•LIMS returns a value that can be used by the calling LIMS Basic code as a handle for subsequent OLE function calls.

NOTE: LabWare recommends that you do not specify a value for the executionContext argument.  The allowable values can be found in Microsoft’s documentation of the Component Object Model (COM).  Microsoft’s documentation can be found, for example, by visiting the website http://msdn.microsoft.com  and searching that website for "CLSCTX Enumeration".  By default, the OleCreate function uses the value 5, which is the combination of CLSCTX_INPROC_SERVER (1) and  CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER (4).

NOTE: OLE automation provides a generic interface to another application.  Each OLE automation server provides its own interface.  The interface may be version-specific.  The interface is established at runtime and depends on what is installed on the computer on which LIMS is running.  For example, a LIMS Basic subroutine with OLE automation calls that was developed and tested with Excel 2003 may not work on a computer on which the available Excel version is Excel 2000.


Example:

The following example connects to Microsoft Excel.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

Returns:

The function returns an integer value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

OleGetProperty   

Purpose:

The OleGetProperty function gets the property of an OLE automation object.

Syntax:

valueOrHandle = OleGetProperty(handle, propertyName, argValue)

handle – a handle (pointer) to an OLE automation object.

propertyName – the name of the property whose value is to be obtained.  Dot notation may be used to get the property of an object that is the property of another object.

argValue – optional; an argument value to be passed in.  The value may be a scalar value or an array.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no property name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No property name specified".

•If the specified handle does not point to a valid OLE automation object, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid handle".

•LIMS obtains the value of the specified property.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger an error and set the lastError variable equal to text of the corresponding OLE automation error.

•LIMS returns the value that the OLE automation server returns.  This may be a scalar value or it may be a handle of the same or a different OLEautomation object.

NOTE: To determine the properties that can be obtained, whether or not an argument is required,  and to determine what value is returned when getting a property value, refer to the object model documentation for the OLE application server.  For example, the object model documentation for Microsoft Excel 2000 can be found in a file named VBAXL9.CHM that is supplied with the application.


Example:

The following example opens Microsoft Excel and obtains a handle on its collection of open workbooks.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

xlWorkbooks = OleGetProperty(xlApplication,"Workbooks")

Returns:

See note above.  The function returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

OleInvokeMethod   

Purpose:

The OleInvokeMethod function invokes a method of an OLE automation object.

Syntax:

value = OleInvokeMethod(handle, methodName, argValue, isHandle, "arrayName")

handle – a handle (pointer) to an OLE automation object.

propertyName – the name of the method to be invoked.  Dot notation may be used to invoke a method of an object that is the property of another object.

argValue – optional; an argument value to be passed in.  The value may be a scalar value or an array.

isHandle – a Boolean value. If set to True, LIMS interprets the argument as the handle to an OLE object instead of as an integer.

arrayName –if the method returns an array and an arrayName is specified, then LIMS would create a LIMS Basic array with the specified name populated with the contents of the returned array.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no method name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No method name specified".

•If the specified handle does not point to a valid OLE automation object, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid handle".

•LIMS invokes the specified method.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger an error and set the lastError variable equal to text of the corresponding OLE automation error.

•LIMS evaluates if the method returns an array and populates the specified arrayName with the values returned in the array. If no arrayName is specified, LIMS generates an error and populates the lastError variable with "Method returns an array. No array name specified."

•LIMS returns the value that the OLE automation server returns.  This may be a scalar value or it may be a handle of the same or a different OLE automation object.

NOTE: To determine the methods that can be invoked, whether or not an argument is required,  and to determine what value is returned when invoking a method, refer to the object model documentation for the OLE application server.  For example, the object model documentation for Microsoft Excel 2000 can be found in a file named VBAXL9.CHM that is supplied with the application.


Example:

The following example opens Microsoft Excel, obtains a handle on its collection of open workbooks, and invokes the Open method to open a workbook.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

xlWorkbooks = OleGetProperty(xlApplication,"Workbooks")

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlWorkbooks,"Open","c:\MyExcelFile.xls")

Returns:

See note above.  The function returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

OleRelease   

Purpose:

The OleRelease function releases memory associated with an OLE automation object.  Failure to release memory may cause a memory leak in LIMS.

Syntax:

status = OleRelease(handle)

handle – a handle (pointer) to an OLE automation object.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no handle is specified, LIMS will do nothing.

•If the specified handle does not point to a valid OLE automation object, LIMS will do nothing.

•LIMS releases all memory associated with the specified object.

•LIMS returns the value true.

Example:

The following example opens Microsoft Excel, closes it, and then releases memory.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Quit")

status = OleRelease(xlApplication)

Returns:

The function returns the value true.

 

OleReleaseAll   

Purpose:

The OleReleaseAll function releases all memory associated with all OLE automation objects.  Failure to release memory may cause a memory leak in LIMS.

Syntax:

status = OleReleaseAll(exceptions)

exceptions –is a 1D array listing the OLE automation objects that will not have the memory released.  This argument is optional.  If not specified releases all memory associated with all OLE automation objects will be released.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS releases all memory associated with all OLE automation objects not listed in the exceptions array.  If no array is passed then all objects are released.

•LIMS returns the value true.

Example:

The following example opens Microsoft Excel, closes it, and then releases memory.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

status = OleInvokeMethod(xlApplication,"Quit")

status = OleReleaseAll()

Returns:

The function returns the value true.

 

OleSetProperty   

Purpose:

The OleSetProperty function sets the property of an OLE automation object.

Syntax:

status = OleSetProperty(handle, propertyName, value)

handle – a handle (pointer) to an OLE automation object.

propertyName – the name of the property to be set.  Dot notation may be used to set the property of an object that is the property of another object.

value – the value to be assigned to the specified property.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no property name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No property name specified".

•If the specified handle does not point to a valid OLE automation object, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid handle".

•LIMS sets the value of the specified property.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger an error and set the lastError variable equal to text of the corresponding OLE automation error.

•LIMS returns the value that the OLE automation server returns.  This may be a scalar value or it may be a handle of the same or a different OLEautomation object.

NOTE: To determine the properties that can be set, and to determine what value is returned when setting a property, refer to the object model documentation for the OLE application server.  For example, the object model documentation for Microsoft Excel 2000 can be found in a file named VBAXL9.CHM that is supplied with the application.


Example:

The following example opens Microsoft Excel and makes the application visible.

xlApplication = OleCreate("Excel.Application")

status = OleSetProperty(xlApplication,"Visible",true)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

28.3.31  Order Functions
 

Function

AddOrderItem

AddSampleToOrder

CloseOrder

CreateOrder

LockOrder

RemoveOrderItem

RemoveSampleFromOrder

UnlockOrder

UpdateOrderFld

UpdateOrderFlds

UpdateOrderItemFld

 

AddOrderItem  

Purpose:

The AddOrderItem function adds an order item to the specified order.

Syntax:

status = AddOrderItem(orderNum, itemTable, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

orderNum – the name of the order to which the item will be added

itemTable- the name of the item table to which the entry is being added

fieldsArray – an array containing field names

valuesArray – an array containing values corresponding to the fields specified in the fieldsArray

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:  .

•LIMS checks the sizes of the fieldsArray and valuesArray.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Field and value array size are not the same".

•LIMS retrieves the order whose name is equal to the value of orderNum.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified order does not exist: orderNum".

•LIMS checks if the order is closed.  If closed, LIMS will trigger the error "Order closed: orderNum".

•LIMS checks the itemTable.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified item table is not valid for the order".

•LIMS adds the item table entry with the specified field values.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

orderNum = "M11-00104"

itemTable = "X_ORD_PAT"

fieldsArray[1] = "FIRST_NAME"

fieldsArray[2] = "LAST_NAME"

fieldsArray[3] = "GENDER"

valuesArray[1] = "PEDRO"

valuesArray[2] = "MARTIN"

valuesArray[3] = "M"

status = AddOrderItem(orderNum, itemTable, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

AddSampleToOrder    

Purpose:

The AddSampleToOrder function adds a sample to the specified order.

Syntax:

status = AddSampleToOrder(orderNumber, sampleNumber)

orderNum – the name of the order to which the sample will be added

sampleNumber - the sample number to add to the order

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the order whose name is equal to the value of orderNum.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified order does not exist: orderNum".

•LIMS checks the sampleNumber.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified sample does not exist: sampleNumber".

•LIMS adds the sample to the order.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

orderNumber = "M12-00093"

sampleNumber = 5550

status = AddSampleToOrder(orderNumber, sampleNumber)

save()

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

CloseOrder    

Purpose:

The CloseOrder function closes an order.   

Syntax:

status = CloseOrder(orderNum)

orderNum – the number of the order to be closed.

Comments:

A database commit is part of this function.

Example:

The following example closes order R05-00011

status = CloseOrder("R05-00011")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

CreateOrder    

Purpose:

The CreateOrder function creates an order.   

Syntax:

orderNumber = CreateOrder(template, fieldsArray, valuesArray, aboutEntity, forEntity, profileArray, testsArray, createSamplesFlag)

template – the order template to be used.

fieldsArray – an array of the order fields in which values are to be inserted.

valuesArray – an array of the values to insert in to the fields specified in fieldsArray.

about – the about entity for this order.

for – the for entity for this order.

profileArray – an array of profiles to assign for this order.  This paramer is only meaningful when M0234 LIS is bound.

testsArray – an array of tests to assign for this order.

createSamplesFlag – a Boolean parameter that determines if the samples are to be created when the order is created.  This value defaults to True.

Comments:

This function does not check for valid entries in the fieldsArray, ValuesArray, testsArray, aboutEntity or forEntity.  The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS evaluates the input: createSamplesFlag.  If the input is not specified, the system interprets the value as True.

•LIMS evaluates the input: template.  If the Order Template is not located, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: : "Order template does not exist:" followed by the input.

•LIMS attempts to create the Order (in cache) using the specified template.  If the order is not create, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Order could not be created".

•LIMS sets the ABOUT_ENTITY field to input: about and the FOR_ENTITY to input: for.

•If the createSampesFlag was interpreted as True, the system creates the required samples.

•The system attempts to save the created Order.  If the save fails, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Error creating order record".

NOTE: A database commit is part of this function.


Example:

The following example creates an order using the template "INPATIENT", for patient number "01-595-42-8466" and practitioner "ALLEN", to do the test "PSA", with no fields modified from the defaults.

template = "INPATIENT"

about = "01-595-42-8466"

forent = "ALLEN"

testsArray[1] = "PSA"

 

orderNumber = CreateOrder(template,,, about, forent,, testsArray)

Returns: 

The function returns the number of the created order.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

LockOrder    

Purpose:

The LockOrder function locks an order.   

Syntax:

status = LockOrder(orderNumber)

orderNumber – the number of the order to be locked.

Comments:

This function does not save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example locks order R05-00012

status = LockOrder("R05-00012")

Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

RemoveOrderItem  

Purpose:

The RemoveOrderItem function removes the specified entry in an item table from the order.

Syntax:

status = RemoveOrderItem(orderNum, itemTable, seqNum, commitFlag)

orderNum – the name of the order from which the item will be removed

itemTable- the name of the item table from which the entry is being removed

seqNum– the value of the SeqNum field for the record that is to be deleted

commitFlag  – a Boolean indicating if a database commit is to be performed after the function executes.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the order whose name is equal to the value of orderNum.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified order does not exist: orderNum".

•LIMS checks if the order is closed.  If closed, LIMS will trigger the error "Order closed: orderNum".

•LIMS checks the itemTable entries for the specified order.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified item table has no entries for the order".

•LIMS checks the itemTable.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified item table is not valid for the order".

•LIMS removes the item table entry with the specified seqNum. If the commitFlag  is true then LIMS commits the change.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error committing order".

This function does save changes to the database if commitFlag  = T.

Example:

orderNum = "M11-00104"

itemTable = "X_ORD_PAT"

seqNum = 20

commitFlag = "T"

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

RemoveSampleFromOrder  

Purpose:

The RemoveSampleFromOrder function removes a sample from the specified order.

Syntax:

status =  RemoveSampleFromOrder(orderNumber, sampleNumber)

orderNum – the name of the order from which the sample will be removed

sampleNumber - the sample number to remove from the order

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the order whose name is equal to the value of orderNum.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified order does not exist: orderNum".

•LIMS checks the sampleNumber.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified sample does not exist: sampleNumber".

•LIMS adds the sample to the order.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

orderNumber = "M12-00155"

sampleNumber = 5737

status = RemoveSampleFromOrder(orderNumber, sampleNumber)

save()

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

UnLockOrder    

Purpose:

The UnLockOrder function unlocks an order.    

Syntax:

status = UnLockOrder(orderNumber)

orderNumber – the number of the order to be unlocked.

Comments:

This function does Not save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example unlocks order R05-00012

status = CloseOrder("R05-00012")

Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateOrderFld    

Purpose:

The UpdateOrderFld function updates a field in an order. 

Syntax:

status = UpdateOrderFld(orderNumber, field, value, commitFlag)

orderNumber – the number of the order to be updated

field – the name of the field to be updated.

value – the value to be assigned to the specified field.

commitFlag - Boolean that determines if the information is posted to cache awaiting the next save or committed immediately.  The default value is false.

Comments:

CAUTION:  This function should be used with caution as any field in the ORDERS table may be updated.

 

The function performs the following steps.

•If the system is unable to find an order with the specified orderNumber, it will trigger the error "Order does not exist".

•If the Orders table does not include a field with the specified field name, the system will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified field is a link field (foreign key), the system will validate the specified value against the target table of the link.  If this fails, the system will trigger the error "Invalid value for order record".

•If the specified field is a date field and if the specified value cannot be converted to a date, the system will use a null value instead.

•If the specified field is a Boolean field and if the specified value is missing or does not represent the value true, the system will use the value false instead.

•The system assigns the specified value to the specified field.  If an audit reason is specified for the order table, the system will assign it to the pending transaction.  The system also assigns the audit type "CalculationUpdate" to the pending transaction.

NOTE: This function can save changes to the database depending on the commitFlag.


Example:

The following example sets the Lab Group of order "R05-00045" to "CLINIC_1"

orderNumber = "R05-00045"

field = "LAB_GROUP"

value = "CLINIC_1"

commitFlag = “T”

status = UpdateOrderFld(orderNumber, field, value, commitFlag)

 

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateOrderFlds    

Purpose:

The UpdateOrderFld function updates specified fields in an order.

Syntax:

status = UpdateOrderFld(orderNumber, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

orderNumber – string defining the order number of the order to be updated

fieldsArray – one dimensional aray of the field names to be updated.

valuesArray – one dimensional array of values for the corresponding fields.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If LIMS is unable to find an order with the specified orderNumber, it will trigger the error "Order does not exist".

•If the orders table does not include a field with the specified field name, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified field is a link field (foreign key), LIMS will validate the specified value against the target table of the link.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid value for order record".

•If the specified field is a date field and if the specified value cannot be converted to a date, LIMS will use a null value instead.

•If the specified field is a Boolean field and if the specified value is missing or does not represent the value true, LIMS will use the value false instead.

•LIMS assigns the specified value to the specified field.  If an audit reason is specified for the order table, LIMS will assign it to the pending transaction.  LIMS also assigns the audit type "CalculationUpdate" to the pending transaction.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example sets the Lab Group of order "R05-00045" to "CLINIC_1"

orderNumber = "R05-00045"

fieldsArray[1] = "LAB_GROUP"

valuesAray[1] = "CLINIC_1"

 

status = UpdateOrderFlds(orderNumber, fieldsArray, valuesAray)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateOrderItemFld  

Purpose:

The UpdateOrderItemFld function updates an existing order item for the specified order.

Syntax:

status = UpdateOrderItemFld(orderNum, itemTable, seqNum, field, value, commitFlag)

orderNum – the name of the order to which the item belongs

itemTable- the name of the item table in which the entry is being modified

seqNum– the value of the SeqNum field for the record that is to be updated

field– the name of field to be updated

value – the new value for  the field specified in the field argument

commitFlag  – a Boolean indicating if a database commit is to be performed after the function executes.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the order whose name is equal to the value of orderNum.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified order does not exist: orderNum".

•LIMS checks if the order is closed.  If closed, LIMS will trigger the error "Order closed: orderNum".

•LIMS checks the itemTable entries for the specified order.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified item table has no entries for the order".

•LIMS checks the itemTable.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified item table is not valid for the order".

•LIMS checks the value.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid value for item record: value".

•LIMS updates the item table entry with the specified field value. If the commitFlag  is true then LIMS commits the change.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error committing order".

This function does save changes to the database if commitFlag  = T.

Example:

orderNum = "M11-00104"

itemTable = "X_ORD_PAT"

seqNum = 20

field = "FIRST_NAME"

value = "PEDRO"

commitFlag = "T"

status = UpdateOrderItemFld(orderNum, itemTable, seqNum, field, value, commitFlag)

Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.
