ParserSkipWords

 

OpenParserInterface      

Purpose:

The OpenParserInterface function opens the Parser Interface window.  This is a LabStation window.

Syntax:

status = OpenParserInterface()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the LabStation module is not loaded, LIMS will trigger an error stating the M0042 is required for this function.

•LIMS opens the Parser Interface window.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example opens the Parser Interface

status = OpenParserInterface()

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserCopyChars    

Purpose:

The ParserCopyChars function returns the specified number of characters from the current position of the specified parser.

Syntax:

string = ParserCopyChars(parserName, numChars)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

numChars – the number of characters to be copied

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•If the number of characters remaining in the data string starting from the current position is greater than or equal to the number of characters requested, LIMS will return the substring contained in the requested number of characters and move the position to the end of that substring.

•Otherwise, LIMS will return the remaining characters and position the parser at the end of the entire data string.

Example:

The following example returns the next five characters from the data string of the parser named "PARSER1".

string = ParserCopyChars("PARSER1", 5)

Returns:

The function returns a string with characters copied from the data string if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" otherwise.

 

ParserCopyLines    

Purpose:

The ParserCopyLines function returns the specified number of lines from the current position of the specified parser.

Syntax:

string = ParserCopyLines(parserName, numLines)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

numLines – the number of lines to be copied

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS starts at the current position and finds numLines occurrences of either a Line Feed (ascii 10) or a combination of Carriage Return (ascii 13) and Line Feed.

•If all numLines occurrences are found, the parser will be positioned just after the last Line Feed character that was found.

•Otherwise, the parser will be positioned at the end of the entire data string.

•LIMS returns a string that contains the found substrings separated by a Line Feed character.

Example:

The following example returns the next three lines from the data string of the parser named "PARSER1"

string = ParserCopyLines("PARSER1", 3)

Returns:

The function returns a string with characters copied from the data string if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" otherwise.

 

ParserCopyTo    

Purpose:

The ParserCopyTo function returns the characters from the current position of the specified parser up to the next occurrence of the specified search string.

Syntax:

string = ParserCopyTo(parserName, pattern)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

pattern – the pattern to be found; use an asterisk as a wildcard character

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parserName is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If no pattern is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No pattern was specified".

•If the specified parserName has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS starts searching from the current position until it encounters the first substring that matches the search pattern.

•If it finds such a substring, the parser is positioned at the end of that substring and LIMS will return the characters from the previous position of the specified parser up to the new position  Otherwise, the parser is positioned at the end of the entire data string and LIMS will return all characters from the previous position up to the end..

 Example:

The following example returns from the current position up to the first occurrence of a string where the 2nd character is G (because of the wildcard character the first character can be any character):

parserName = "ParserTest"

pattern = "*G"

string = ParserCopyTo(parserName, pattern)

Returns:

The function returns the string. It returns ERROR if an error is triggered.

 

ParserCopyWords    

Purpose:

The ParserCopyWords function returns the specified number of words from the current position of the specified parser.

Syntax:

string = ParserCopyWords(parserName, numWords)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

numWords – the number of words to be copied

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS starts at the current position and finds numWords occurrences of a separator character.  Separator characters include the following: space, tab, carriage return, line feed, form feed, and double-byte space.  Separator characters that immediately follow another separator character are ignored.

•If all numWords occurrences are found, the parser will be positioned just after the last substring that was found, i.e., the next character to be read will be a separator character.

•Otherwise, the parser will be positioned at the end of the entire data string.

•LIMS returns a string that contains the found substrings separated by a space character.

Example:

The following example returns the next two words from the data string of the parser named "PARSER1".

string = ParserCopyWords("PARSER1", 2)

Returns:

The function returns a string with characters copied from the data string if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" otherwise.

 

ParserCreate    

Purpose:

The ParserCreate function creates a new parser.

Syntax:

status = ParserCreate(parserName, dataString)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be created

dataString – the data string to be parsed

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If no data string is specified, or if the data string is an empty string, LIMS will trigger the error "No data specified".

•LIMS creates a new parser with the specified name and positions it at the beginning of the specified data string.

Example:

The following example creates a parser named "PARSER1" for the contents of the file named "default.htm".

mytext = FileContents("default.htm")
status = ParserCreate("PARSER1", mytext)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserFind    

Purpose:

The ParserFind function positions the specified parser at the next occurrence of the specified search string.

Syntax:

status = ParserFind(parserName, pattern)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be searched

pattern – the pattern to be found; use an asterisk as a wildcard character

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•If no pattern is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No pattern was specified".

•LIMS starts searching from the current position until it encounters the first substring that matches the search pattern.

•If it finds such a substring, the parser is positioned at the end of that substring and LIMS will return the value True.

•Otherwise, the parser is positioned at the end of the entire data string and LIMS will return the value False.

Example:

The following example finds the substring "SAMPLE" in the data string of the parser named "PARSER1".

status = ParserFind("PARSER1", "SAMPLE")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it finds the substring.  It returns the value false otherwise.

 

ParserGetDataFields    

Purpose:

The ParserGetDataFields function parses the specified substrings and returns them as an array of values.

Syntax:

status = ParserGetDataFields(parserName, sizeArray, typeArray, "dataFieldArrayName")

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be parsed

sizeArray – an array in which each element specifies the number of characters to be parsed

typeArray – an array in which each element specifies the data type to which the parsed string should be converted

dataFieldArrayName – the name of the array in which the parsed values should be stored. 

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•If the sizeArray contains no elements, LIMS will trigger the error "No sizes specified".

•If the typeArray contains no elements, LIMS will trigger the error "No types specified".

•If the number of elements in the sizeArray is not equal to the number of elements in the typeArray, LIMS will trigger the error "Sizes and types arrays do not have the same number of arguments".

•LIMS processes each element in the two arrays as follows:

•LIMS copies the next n characters from the current position of the data string into a variable, where n is the value of the current sizeArray element.

•If the value of the current typeArray element is "text", LIMS will interpret the variable as a text string and trim blanks from both ends.

•Otherwise, if the value of the current typeArray element is "date", LIMS will convert the variable to a date value.

•Otherwise, if the value of the current typeArray element is "datetime", LIMS will convert the variable to a datetime value.

•Otherwise, if the value of the current typeArray element is "integer", LIMS will convert the variable to an integer value.

•Otherwise, if the value of the current typeArray element is "number", LIMS will convert the variable to a numeric value.

•Otherwise, if the value of the current typeArray element matches the format "fm.n", where the letters m and n each represent a number, LIMS will interpret the variable as a floating point number with m digits of which n digits appear after the decimal point.  (The original data string should not contain a decimal point.)

•Otherwise, LIMS will leave the variable unchanged.  This is equivalent to type "text", except that blanks will not be trimmed.

•LIMS adds the converted value to the data field array.

Example:

The following example retrieves an integer with four digits and a text string of six characters from the data string for the parser named "PARSER1".  The two values are displayed in a message box.

mysizes[1] = 4
mysizes[2] = 6
mytypes[1] = "integer"
mytypes[2] = "text"
status = ParserGetDataFields("PARSER1", mysizes, mytypes, "mydata")
IF (status) THEN
    msgbox(mydata[1])
    msgbox(mydata[2])
ELSE
    msgbox(lasterror)
ENDIF

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserGotoMarker    

Purpose:

The ParserGotoMarker function returns to the position that was marked by the most recent call to the ParserSetMarker for the specified parser.

Syntax:

status = ParserGotoMarker(parserName)

parserName – the name of the parser whose position is to be changed to the marked position

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS sets the position of the parser equal to the position that was marked by the ParserSetMarker function.

Example:

The following example sets the position of the parser named "PARSER1" back to the marked position.

status = ParserGotoMarker("PARSER1")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserReset    

Purpose:

The ParserReset function returns the specified parser to the beginning of its data string.

Syntax:

status = ParserReset(parserName)

parserName – the name of the parser whose position is to be reset to its beginning

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS returns the parser to the beginning of the data string.

Example:

The following example sets the parser named "PARSER1" to the beginning of its data string.

status = ParserReset("PARSER1")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserSetMarker    

Purpose:

The ParserSetMarker function marks the current position of the specified parser so that it can return to that position at a later time via the ParserGotoMarker function.

Syntax:

status = ParserSetMarker(parserName)

parserName – the name of the parser whose current position is to be marked

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS saves the current position of the parser.

Example:

The following example marks the current location of the parser named "PARSER1".

status = ParserSetMarker("PARSER1")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserSkipChars    

Purpose:

The ParserSkipChars function advances the parser position by the specified number of characters from the current position of the specified parser.

Syntax:

status = ParserSkipChars(parserName, numChars)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

numChars – the number of characters to be skipped

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•If the number of characters remaining in the data string starting from the current position is greater than or equal to the number of characters requested, LIMS will move the position to the end of that substring.  Otherwise, LIMS will position the parser at the end of the entire data string.

 Example:

The following example advances the parser 3 characters from the current position:

parserName = "ParserTest"

numChars = 3

string = ParserSkipChars(parserName, numChars)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ParserSkipWords    

Purpose:

The ParserSkipWords function advances the parser position by the specified number of words from the current position of the specified parser.

Syntax:

status = ParserSkipWords(parserName, numWords)

parserName – the name of the parser whose data is to be copied

numWords – number of words to be skipped

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no parser name is specified, LIMS will trigger the error "No name specified".

•If the specified parser name has not yet been created using the ParserCreate function, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified parser does not exist".

•LIMS starts at the current position and finds numWords occurrences of a separator character.  Separator characters include the following: space, tab, carriage return, line feed, form feed, and double-byte space. Separator characters that immediately follow another separator character are ignored.  If all numWords occurrences are found, the parser will be positioned just after the last substring that was found, i.e., the next character to be read will be a separator character.  Otherwise, the parser will be positioned at the end of the entire data string.

 Example:

The following example advances the parser 2 words from the current position:

parserName = "ParserTest"

numWords = 2

string = ParserSkipWords(parserName, numWords)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.33  PDF Functions
 

Function

AddPdfTextStamp

ConvertToPDF

EncryptPDF

ReplacePDFTags

AddPdfTextStamp    

Purpose:

This function stamps .pdf files with text.  This function can manage position, alignment and font characteristics.

Syntax:

Status = AddPdfTextStamp(filename, position, text, color, alignment, angle, fontcode, fontsize)

fileName – string defining the path and file name of the target file.

position – integer defining the position.  Valid enties are 1 through 9.

1 - Top-Left

4 - Right-Center

7 - Left-Bottom

2 - Top-Center

5 - Right-Bottom

8 - Left-Center

3 - Top-Right

6 - Bottom-Center

9 - Center

text – string defining the text to be stamped into the file.

color –  integer;  color is a hex number based on Red, Green, and Blue: 0xRRGGBB.

Black = 0x000000 = 0

Blue = 0xFF0000 = 16711680

White = 0xFFFFFF = 16777215

Green = 0x00FF00 = 65280

 

Red = 0x0000FF = 255

alignment – integer defining the alignment of the stamp.

16 - left alignment

18 - right alignment

17 - center alignment

 

angle –  integer defining the angle of the stamp on the pdf page.

0 - horizontal

-45 - Diagonal like \

45 - Diagonal like /

90 - vertical

fontcode – integer defining the font for the stamp.

100 - Times-Roman

202 - Courier-Oblique

101 - Times-Bold

203 - Courier-Bold-Oblique

102 - Times-Italic

300 - Helvetica

103 - Times-Bold-Italic

301 - Helvetica-Bold

200 - Courier

302 - Helvetica-Oblique

201 - Courier-Bold

303 - Helvetica-Bold-Oblique

 

400 - Symbol

fontsize – integer defining the font size.

16 - 16pt Font

24 - 24pt Font

 

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS evaluates the file name.  If the file does not have a .pdf extension, LIMS returns the last error: Must be a PDF file.

•LIMS checks to see if the file exists.  If the file does not exist, LIMS returns the last error: Invalid file specified.

•LIMS evaluates the position input.  If the value is not an integer from 1 through 9, the LIMS returns the last error: Position must be an integer between 1 and 9.

•LIMS evaluates the text input.  If there is no text input, the LIMS returns the last error: No text specified.

•LIMS evaluates that color.  If the input is not a valid color it defaults to black.

•LIMS evaluates the alignment.  If the input is not an integer, the LIMS returns the last error: Invalid angle specified.

•LIMS evaluates the fontcode.  If the input is not an integer, the LIMS returns the last error: Invalid fontcode specified.

•LIMS evaluates the fontsize.  If the input is not an integer, the LIMS returns the last error: Invalid fontsize specified.

•LIMS accesses the file.  If this fails, the LIMS returns the last error: There is an error in the pdf file.

•LIMS stamps the text into the file.

Example:

Status = AddPdfTextStamp("c:\lw-lims \target.pdf", 5, "Confidential", 16711680, 17, 0, 301, 24)

Returns:

Status = true if function is successful, otherwise false.

 

ConvertToPDF   

Purpose:

The ConvertToPDF function converts the source file to the PDF file specified in the destination. 

Syntax:

status = ConvertToPDF(source, destination, timeout)

source- The source file name with path

destination- File name with path with PDF extension for the file produced by this function.

timeout- numeric value, specified in seconds.  This timeout is used in case the conversion process fails and does not return immediately.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks the source.  If not found, LIMS will trigger the error "The specified source file does not exist: source".

•LIMS checks the destination.  If not a PDF file, LIMS will trigger the error "The specified destination is not a PDF file!!".  If a file of the same name exists, LIMS will trigger the error "The specified destination file exists!!".  Otherwise LIMS converts the file to PDF.

NOTE: The PDF Camp printer must be present on the client PC for the ConvertToPDF LIMS Basic function to work.

NOTE: 64-bit machines not supported with this LIMS Basic function. For 64-bit support please refer to M0524 (PDFEnhancementsForV6).


 Example:

The following example vconverts the Word document to a pdf file.

sourcefile = curdir() + "\M0433_Word.doc"

destination = curdir() + "\M0433_outpdf.pdf"

timeout = 5

status = ConvertToPDF(sourcefile, destination, timeout)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

EncryptPDF   

Purpose:

The EncryptPDF function encrypts a PDF file and allows setting editing, printing, and/or owner/user passwords

Syntax:

status = EncryptPDF(fileName, allowEditing, allowPrinting, ownerPassword, userPassword)

filename- File name with path of the PDF to be secured

allowEditing-  A Boolean indicating the security to be defined for the file related to Editing.  If T, then editing of the encrypted file is allowed. If F, then no editing of the encrypted file is allowed.  The default is T.

allowPrinting- A Boolean indicating the security to be defined for the file related to Printing.  If T, then printing of the encrypted file is allowed. If F, then no printing of the encrypted file is allowed.  The default is T.

ownerPassword   a string defining the password to prevent changes to the security settings (by users opening the file in Adobe Acrobat)

userPassword-  a string defining a password that will be required to view the file

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks the filename.  If not a PDF file, LIMS will trigger the error "Must be a PDF file".  If not found, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid file specified".

•LIMS encrypts the PDF file according to the specified parameters.

 Example:

The following example  prevents printing of the specified file and password protects the file:

fileName = curdir() + "\M0433_pdf_SEC.pdf"

allowEditing = "T"

allowPrinting = "F"

ownerPassword = "ABC"

userPassword = "DEF"

status = EncryptPDF(fileName, allowEditing, allowPrinting, ownerPassword, userPassword)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

GetPdfPageCount

Purpose:

The GetPdfPageCount function returns the page count for a specified PDF file.

Syntax:

numPages = GetPdfPageCount(fileName)

fileName – the file name with path of the PDF file.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the fileName.  If the file does not exist, the system triggers the error: “File does not exist: fileName”. If the file does not have the .pdf file extension, the system triggers the error: “File is not a PDF file: fileName”.

•The system gets the page count for the specified file”.

Example:

 

fileName = "C:\LabWare\PDFS\myPdf.pdf"

numPages = GetPdfPageCount(fileName)

Returns: 

The function returns the number of pages if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer ERROR and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

GetPDFTags  

Purpose:

The GetPDFTags function gets tag names that use the specified tag delimiters and outputs the list in an array.

Syntax:

status = GetPDFTags(tagChars, fileName, "tagArrayName")

tagChars – the four characters used a delimiters for the tags in the PDF file. The first 2 characters are used at the beginning of the tag and the last 2 characters mark the end of the tag.

fileName – the name of the source PDF file, with path.

tagArrayName – the name of the array that will contain the list of tags found in the PDF file

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS evaluates the tagChars parameter.  If empty, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘No tag chars specified’.

•LIMS checks the size of the tagChars parameter.  If not equal to 4, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Tag chars must be of size 4: tagChars’.

•LIMS checks the fileName parameter.  If not found, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Specified input file does not exist: fileName’.

•LIMS checks the tagArrayName parameter.  If the argument is empty, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Error: no array name specified’.

•The LIMS retrieves the tags and outputs the list in the output array.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example checks the PDF file named source_1.pdf and returns the list of tags that are delimited by the << and >> characters in the array named tagArrayName:

tagChars = "<<>>"

fileName = "C:\ source_1.pdf"

 

returnstatus = GetPDFTags(tagChars, fileName, "tagArrayName")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

MergePdfFiles     

Purpose:

The MergePdfFiles function reads the contents of two specified PDF files and writes them into one single PDF file as an output, i.e., it combines the contents of 2 specified PDF files into a single PDF file.

Syntax:

status = MergePdfFiles(firstPdf, secondPdf, outputPdf)

firstPdf – the name and path of the first Pdf file to be merged

secondPdf – the name and path of the second Pdf file to be merged

outputPdf – the name and path of the output Pdf file to be generated

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the specified first and second Pdf files exist. If either file does not exist, then LIMS will trigger an error and set the lastError variable equal to "First/Second PDF file does not exist" (depending upon which file is non-existent).

•LIMS verifies that the specified files do not have any securities defined. If either file has any securities defined, LIMS will trigger an error and set the lastError variable equal to "First/Second PDF has security defined" (depending upon which file has securities defined).

•LIMS checks if the Second PDF is same as the output PDF file. If they are the same, LIMS generates an error and sets the lastError variable equal to "Second PDF must not match the output PDF".

•LIMS copies the contents of the first PDF file and writes the contents to the output file. If an error is generated, then LIMS populates the lastError variable with "Error creating output pdf".

•LIMS copies the contents of the second PDF file and appends the contents to the output file. If an error is generated, the LIMS populates the lastError variable with "Error merging the pdf files".

Example:

The following example copies the contents of the 2 PDF files "Source1.pdf" and "Source2.pdf" and merges them into one single pdf file "Target.pdf".

Status = MergePdfFiles("c:\Source1.pdf", "c:\Source2.pdf", "c:\Target.pdf")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

MergePdfs   

Purpose:

The MergePdfs function merges the specified files in a new PDF file.

Syntax:

status = MergePdfs(pdfFileArray, outputFileName)

pdfFileArray – an array containing the file names with path for the source PDF files;

outputFileName – a string containing the file name with path for the merged file.

NOTE: File path names that contain non-ansi characters will not work with this function.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks that the pdfFileArray is an array.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "PDF file array is not an array".

•LIMS checks that the pdfFileArray has at least 2 array elements.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "PDF file array must contain at least 2 PDF file names".

•LIMS checks the files in the pdfFileArray. If the file is not found, LIMS will trigger the error "File does not exist:".  If the file is not a PDF, LIMS will trigger the error "File is not a PDF file:".

•LIMS checks that the outputFileName has a PDF file extension.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Output file must be a PDF file".

•If the outputFileName already exists then LIMS tries to delete the existing file before creating the new file.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error ensuring output file is deleted before merge".

•LIMS tries to create the merged file.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error merging PDF files into: outputFileName".

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

 

The following example merges 2 PDF files then places the new file in the current working directory:

file1 = PromptForFile("Select the first PDF file", "")

file2 = PromptForFile("Select the next PDF file", "")

pdfFileArray[1] = file1

pdfFileArray[2] = file2

cdir = curdir()

outputFileName = cdir + "\PDF_ouput1.pdf"

status = MergePdfs(pdfFileArray, outputFileName)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PrintPDF    

Purpose:

The PrintPDF function prints the specified PDF file on the specified printer.

Syntax:

status = PrintPDF(aPDFFileName, aPrinterName)

aPDFFileName – the name and path of the PDF file to be printed.

aPrinterName – the name of the printer to be used to print the file. If set to "DEFAULT" then Windows default printer is used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the printer name is valid.  If this fails, LIMS will return the error: "Printer does not exist.".

•LIMS verifies the file name.  If this fails, LIMS will return the error:
"PDF file does not exist."

•LIMS attempts to print file.

•If unsuccessful, LIMS attempts to open a PDF application.  IF the attempt to open a PDF application fails, LIMS will return:
"Failed to opn DDE session to PDF server."

•If LIMS fails to print the file within 60 seconds, the system returns the error:
"Failed to print PDF file.".

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example prints the file: Target.pdf on the HP LaserJet 2300.

aPDFFileName = "c:\target.pdf"
aPrinterName = "HP LaserJet 2300"
status = PrintPDF(aPDFFileName, aPrinterName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ReplacePDFTags  

Purpose:

The ReplacePDFTags function replaces the tags identified in the fieldsArray with the information contained in the valuesArray.

Syntax:

status = ReplacePDFTags(inFileName, outFileName, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

inFileName – the name of the source PDF file, with path.

outFileName – the name of the processed PDF file, with path.

fieldsArray – the 1-dimensional array containing the tags that will be updated

valuesArray- the 1-dimensional array containing the text values that will be used to replace the tags in the processed PDF file.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS checks the inFileName parameter.  If not found, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Specified input file does not exist: inFileName.

•LIMS checks the size of the fieldsArray and  valuesArray.  If the size is different, LIMS will trigger the error "Fields and values arrays must be the same size".

•LIMS updates the PDF with the new data and generates the new file.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error: ‘Replacement of tags failed’.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.

When specifying the fieldsArray, all tags found in the document should be included.  Otherwise the tag is not replaced and will be displayed in output PDF file.  If not used, the valuesArray can set the value to "" so that the tag is removed.


Example:

The following example replaces the tags found in the source_2.pdf file and outputs the processed file:

 

inFileName = "C:\source_2.pdf"

increment = GetIncrement("pdfout", 1, "F")

filename = "PDF_Out" & increment & ".pdf"

outFileName = "C:\Output\" & filename

fieldsArray[1] = "<<text1>>"

fieldsArray[2] = "<<text2>>"

fieldsArray[3] = "<<text3>>"

fieldsArray[4] = "<<text4>>"

fieldsArray[5] = "<<text5>>"

valuesArray[1] = "LabWare "

valuesArray[2] = "3 Mill Rd,"

valuesArray[3] = "Wilmington"

valuesArray[4] = "Delaware"

valuesArray[5] = "19806"

status = ReplacePDFTags(inFileName, outFileName, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

SplitPdf  

Purpose:

The SplitPdf function splits the specified PDF page into a new PDF file.

Syntax:

status = SplitPdf (inputFileName, pageRange, outputFileName)

inputFileName – a string containing the file name with path for the source PDF file

pageRange – a string indicating the range of pages for splitting into the new PDF file.  This may contain any number of individual pages or page ranges, however a single page should only be included once.

            Examples:

                "1" = Split out page 1

                "1,2" = Split out pages 1 and 2

                "3,5,10" = Split out pages 3, 5, and 10  (any combination is allowed)

                "4-" = Split out page 4 through the last page

                "-4" = Split out page 1 through page 4

                "3-5" = Split out page 3 through page 5

                "5,-3" = Split out page 5 followed by pages 1 through 3

 

outputFileName – a string containing the file name with path for the new PDF file

NOTE:  File path names that contain non-ansi characters will not work with this function.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks that the inputFileName has a PDF file extension.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "PDF file array is not an array".

•LIMS checks the inputFileName.  If the file is not found, LIMS will trigger the error "File does not exist:". 

•LIMS checks that the outputFileName has a PDF file extension.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Output file must be a PDF file".

•If the outputFileName already exists then LIMS tries to delete the existing file before creating the new file.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error ensuring output file is deleted before split".

•LIMS tries to create the split the file.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Error splitting pages from PDF file: outputFileName".

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

 

 

The following example

cdir = curdir()

inputFileName  = PromptForFile("Select the PDF to split, "")

pageRange = "1-3"

outputFileName = cdir + "\PDF_output1.pdf"

status = SplitPdf(inputFileName, pageRange, outputFileName)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.34  Plate Window Functions
 

Function

PlateWindowAboveLabels

PlateWindowBelowLabels

PlateWindowCircleRadius

PlateWindowColors

PlateWindowHighlights

PlateWindowShowGridLines

PlateWindowTextTop

PlateWindowAboveLabels    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowAboveLabels function determined the labels displayed above the plate objects.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

labelsArray – a 2-dimensional array containing object labels.  The first dimension references the row in the Plate Window.  The second dimension references the column.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS evaluates the input LabelsArray.  If the array is not two dimensional, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Label Array must be two dimensional".

•LIMS updates the labels immediately above the displayed wells in the Plate Window.  If a label is not specified in the array for a well, the system posts a null string.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

 

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"

windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")

status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)



Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowBelowLabels    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowBelowLabelsfunction determined the labels displayed below the plate objects.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowBelowLabels(windowName, belowlabelsArray)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

belowlabelsArray – a 2-dimensional array containing object labels.  The first dimension references the row in the Plate Window.  The second dimension references the column.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS evaluates the input LabelsArray.  If the array is not two dimensional, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Label Array must be two dimensional".

•LIMS updates the labels immediately below the displayed wells in the Plate Window.  If a label is not specified in the array for a well, the system posts a null string.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
BelowlabelsArray[1,1] = "1"
BelowlabelsArray[1,2] = "2"
BelowlabelsArray[2,2] = "3"
windowName = "PLATE"
status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")

status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)

status = PlateWindowBelowLabels(windowName, BelowlabelsArray)



Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowCircleRadius    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowCircleRadius function determined the circle size representing the wells on the Plate Window.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowCircleRadius(windowName, radius)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

radius – an integer representing the circle radius.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the input value for the radius is an integer.  If the value is not an integer, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Radius is not an integer".

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS updates the radius of the wells displayed on the Plate Window.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"

labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")

status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)

status = PlateWindowCircleRadius(windowName, 12)



Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowColors    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowColors function determined the colors for plate objects.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowColors(windowName, colorsArray) 

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

colorsArray – a 2-dimensional array of the colors for plate objects. 
The valid colors are: 1-Black, 2-Blue, 3-Brown, 4-Cyan, 5-Dark Blue, 6-Dark Cyan, 7-Dark Gray, 8-Dark Green, 9-Dark Pink, 10-Dark Red, 11-Gray 12-Green, 13-Pale Gray, 14-Pink, 15-Red, 16-White, 17-Yellow

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS evaluates the input LabelsArray.  If the array is not two dimensional, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Label Array must be two dimensional".

•LIMS sets the Plate Window wells to the specified colors.  If a color is not specified for a specific well, then the color defaults to 16, white.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
colorsArray[1,1] = "1"
colorsArray[1,2] = "2"
colorsArray[2,1] = "15"
colorsArray[2,2] = "17"
windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")
status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)
status = PlateWindowColors(windowName, colorsArray) 



Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowHighlights    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowHighlights function determined which plate objects will be highlighted by a second circle.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowHighlights(windowName, highlightsArray)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

highlightsArray – a 2-dimensional array containing True or False, indicating if the object is to be highlighted.  The first dimension references the row in the Plate Window.  The second dimension references the column.  If a value is not specified for a position on the Plate Window, the value defaults to False.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS evaluates the input LabelsArray.  If the array is not two dimensional, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Label Array must be two dimensional".

•LIMS highlights all the well positions on the specified Plate Window that has a value of True in the input array.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below (only the objects in the second row have circles around them)

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
highlightsArray[1,1] = "F"
highlightsArray[1,2] = "F"
highlightsArray[2,1] = "T"
highlightsArray[2,2] = "T"

windowName = "PLATE"
status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")
status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)
status = PlateWindowHighlights(windowName, highlightsArray)

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowHorizontalDividerIncrement    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowHorizontalDividerIncrement function that adds dividers to the Plate Window

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowHorizontalDividerIncrement(windowName, increment)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

increment – an integer indicating the number of rows between dividers.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS evaluates the input Increment.  If the input is not an integer, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Increment is not an integer"

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS inserts horizontal lines on the Plate Window after increments number of rows.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")
status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)

increment = 1
status = PlateWindowHorizontalDividerIncrement(windowName, increment)



Returns: 

The function returns true if it completes successfully.  It returns false if an error is triggered.

 

PlateWindowLineThickness    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowLineThickness function enable the setting of the weight of the border and highlighs displayed within the Plate Window.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowLineThickness(windowName, thickness)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

thickness – integer defining the weight of the border and highlights within the Plate Window.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS evaluates the input Thickness.  If the input is not an integer, the system triggers an error and the Last Error is set to: "Thickness is not an integer"

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS modifies the thickness of the border and the well highlights to match the specified thickness.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
windowName = "PLATE"
status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")

status = PlateWindowAboveLabels(windowName, labelsArray)

highlights[1,1] = "T"
highlights[2,2] = "T"
status = PlateWindowHighlights(windowName, highlights)

thickness = 10
status = PlateWindowLineThickness(windowName, thickness)



Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

PlateWindowShowGridLines    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowShowGridLines function that adds gridlines to the Plate Window.

status = PlateWindowShowGridLines(windowName, showFlag)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

showFlag – a Boolean value indicating if grid lines are to be displayed in the plate window.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS displays the grid lines or removes the grid lines from the Plate Window based on the input: showFlag.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

labelsArray[1,1] = "A"
labelsArray[1,2] = "B"
labelsArray[2,2] = "D"
windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

status = PlateWindowTextLeft(windowName, text)

text – The text that is to be displayed at the left side border of the Plate Window.

Comments:

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS inserts the input text value in the left hand border of the Plate Window.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")
status = PlateWindowTextLeft(windowName, "LEFT")



Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

28.3.35  Project Functions
 

Function

AddAdHocProjectResult

AddProjectItem
