ASC    

Purpose:

The Asc function converts a character value to its equivalent ASCII character  code.

Syntax:

x = ASC(char)

Char –

Comments:

char is any character.  Refer to the ASCII character code table for values.

Example:

x = ASC("T") 

Returns:

x = 84

 

AsciiSum    

Purpose:

The AsciiSum function returns the sum of the ASCII values of the characters in a string.

Syntax:

num = AsciiSum(string)

string – the string whose ASCII values are to be summed

Comments:

•The function performs the following steps.

•If the length of the string is zero, LIMS will return the value zero.

•LIMS computes the ASCII value of each character in the string and returns the sum of these values.

Example:

The following example returns the value 198.

num = AsciiSum("ABC")

Returns:

The function returns a number that represents the sum of the ASCII values.

 

Base64Decode    

Purpose:

The Base64Decode function converts a Base 64 string to plain text.

Syntax:

string = Base64Decode(base64string)

base64string – the string to be decoded from Base 64

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the Base 64 string to plain text and returns the result.

Example:

The following example encodes the string "Hello World" to Base 64 and then decodes it back to plain text.

plainText = "Hello World"
base64string = Base64Encode(plainText)
plainText = Base64Decode(base64string)

Returns:

The function returns a plain text value.

 

Base64Encode    

Purpose:

The Base64Encode function converts plain text to a Base 64 encoded string.

Syntax:

base64string = Base64Encode(string)

string – the string to be encoded to Base 64

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the Base 64 string to plain text and returns the result.

Example:

The following example encodes the string "Hello World" to Base 64 and then decodes it back to plain text.

plaintext = "Hello World"
base64string = Base64Encode(plaintext)
plaintext = Base64Decode(base64string)

Returns:

The function returns a Base 64 text value.

 

Chr    

Purpose:

The Chr function converts an ASCII character code to its equivalent character  value.

Syntax:

x = Chr(integer)

Comments:

integer is an integer value in the range of 0 to 255.  Refer to the ASCII character code table for values.

Example:

x = Chr(84)  

Returns:

x = T

 

ContextTranslate    

Purpose:

The ContextTranslate function uses the ContextSubstitutionEngine (CSE).  Like the ContextTranslateSQL function, this function will process the input string to substitute data from context.  The ContextTranslate function will always replace { } tags.  If it cannot evaluate the tag, the tag will be removed.  Refer to section 6.6.6 Context Substitution for more on using context substitution.

Syntax:

outString = ContextTranslate(inString)

inString – the string with tags to be processed.

Comments:

Below are some examples of tags that can be included in the Input string.

Tag Examples

Returns

{variable}

Any LIMS Basic variable

{TABLE.FIELD}

The field value for an object in the LIMS Basic selects group.

Eg.  {SAMPLE.TEXT_ID}

{TABLE. TABLE.KEYFIELD.FIELD}

The first value is the table name from the record in context. The second value is used to determine the linked table from table master. The second, third, fourth, etc.. values up to but not including the last field value are the fields on the linked table that match the key fields on the record table.  The last value is the field on the linked table that is to be returned.

Eg. {SAMPLE.PRODUCT.PRODUCT_VERSION.DESCRIPTION}

{SUBROUTINE:SUB_NAME}

Run the subroutine SUB_NAME and substitute the subroutine return value into the string.

{CLIENT_DIR}

The full path of the LabWare client directory

{DATE}

The current date using the Windows settings format

{SERVER_DIR}

The full path of the LabWare server directory

{START_IN_DIR}

The LabWare start in directory

{TIME}

The current time using the Windows settings format

{GROUP_CLAUSE}

Returns an SQL clause for the user’s group security. Eg. "GROUP_NAME in (‘GROUP1’, ‘GROUP2’)"

{NLS:NLS_KEY}

Returns the value for the NLS Key


Example:

The following example retrieves sample information from context..

sampleNumber = Select.SAMPLE.SAMPLE_NUMBER

inString = "This is the login date {SAMPLE.LOGIN_DATE} for sample {sampleNumber}."

 

outString = ContextTranslate(inString)

Returns:

The function returns a string value.

 

ContextTranslateSQL   

Purpose:

The ContextTranslateSQL function uses the ContextSubstitutionEngine (CSE).  Like the ContextTranslate function this function will process the input string to substitute data from context.  The ContextTranslateSQL function will leave any tags that cannot be evaluated.  This allows ODBC constructs like {ts ... } and dates to be included in the string without fear of the tags being removed.  Also, any tags that evaluate to date objects will be translated into {ts ... } values. Refer to section 6.6.6 Context Substitution for more on using context substitution.

Syntax:

outString = ContextTranslateSQL(inString)

inString – the string with tags to be processed.

Comments:

Refer to the ContextTranslate function for examples of tags that can be included in the Input string.

Example:

The following example retrieves sample information from context..

inString = "Date is {DATE}. Time is {TIME}."

 

outString = ContextTranslateSQL(inString)

Returns:

The function returns a string value.

 

ConvertHTMLToText   

Purpose:

This function strips the HTML formatting and converts an HTML input into plain text.

Syntax:

Text = ConvertHTMLToText(HTML)

HTML – input HTML string to be stripped of HTML formatting and converted to plain text.

Comments:

When this function is triggered LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS checks the input HTML string for HTML tags character by character.

•If any HTML formatting is found, LIMS strips them off and writes the unformatted output as a plain text.

Example:

Text = ConvertHTMLToText(HTML)

Returns:

If successful, returns the input HTML as a plain text, stripped off the formatting.

 

ConvertRTFToText    

Purpose:

The ConvertRTFToText function converts a Rich Text Format string to plain text.

Syntax:

text = ConvertRTFToText(rtfText)

rtfText – the rich text to be converted to plain text

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the rich text to plain text and returns the result.

Example:

The following example converts the contents of an RTF file to plain text.

rtfText = FileContents("mydoc.rtf")
text = ConvertRTFToText(rtfText)

Returns:

The function returns a text value

 

ConvertTextToRTF    

Purpose:

The ConvertTextToRTF function converts plain text to Rich Text Format.

Syntax:

rtf = ConvertTextToRTF(text)

text – the plain text to be converted to Rich Text Format

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the plain text to rich text and returns the result.

Example:

The following example converts the contents of a text file to rich text.

text = FileContents("mydoc.txt")
rtf = ConvertTextToRTF(text)

Returns:

The function returns a text value (in rich text format).

Decrypt    

Purpose:

The Decrypt function decrypts an encrypted string.

Syntax:

unencryptedString = Decrypt(encryptedString, passPhrase)

encryptedString – the string to be decrypted

passPhrase – string defining the encryption key.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no encrypted string, or an empty string, is specified, LIMS will return an empty string.

•Otherwise, LIMS decrypts the string and returns the result.

Example:

The following example decrypts the value stored in the variable named SecureResult.

unencryptedString = Decrypt(SecureResult, "key phrase")

Returns:

The function returns a string value.

 

Encrypt    

Purpose:

The Encrypt function encrypts a string.

Syntax:

encryptedString = Encrypt(unencryptedString, passPhrase)

unencryptedString – the string to be encrypted

passPhrase – string defining the encryption key.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no unencrypted string, or an empty string, is specified, LIMS will return an empty string.

•Otherwise, LIMS encrypts the string and returns the result.

Example:

The following example encrypts the string "Hello World".

encryptedString = Encrypt("Hello World", "key phrase")

Returns:

The function returns an encrypted string.

 

ExtractNumber    

Purpose:

The ExtractNumber function extracts a number from a string.

Syntax:

num = ExtractNumber(stringWithNumber, returnZeroFlag)

stringWithNumber – input string.  If the input string contains multiple numbers, the leftmost number will be extracted. The returned value is numeric.

returnZeroFlag – Boolean used to define the return num value if no numbers are located.  If set to True (T), num will be set to 0.  If set to False (F) num will be null.  If not specified, the setting defaults to true.

Comments:

NOTE:  When extracting a number using the extract number method, the following rules apply:

If the string contains a period use the period as the decimal separator

If the string does not contain either a period or comma, use the period as the decimal separator (arbitrary)

If the string contains a comma and (nls db decimal separator is a comma) then use comma as the decimal separator.

   Otherwise, use a period as the decimal separator.


Example:

num = ExtractNumber("<2.2")   

Returns:

num = 2.2

 

ExtractNumbers    

Purpose:

The ExtractNumbers function extracts numbers from a string and returns the numbers in an array along with the number found.

Syntax:

numFound = ExtractNumbers(input, "outputArrayName")

input – string to be evaluated for numbers.

outputArrayName – name of the return array containing located numbers.

Comments:

The returned values are numeric.

NOTE:  When extracting an array of numbers from a string using the extract numbers method, the following rules apply:

If the string contains a period use the period as the decimal separator

If the string does not contain either a period or comma, use the period as the decimal separator (arbitrary)

If the strring contains a comma and (nls db decimal separator is a comma) then use comma as the decimal separator.

   Otherwise, use a period as the decimal separator


Example:

 

input = "-12.3def0.93jk4"

outputArrayName = "numberArray"

numFound = ExtractNumbers(input, outputArrayName)   

Returns:

numFound = 3

numberArray[1] = -12.3

numberArray[2] = 0.93

numberArray[3] = 4.0

 

InStr    

Purpose:

The InStr function returns the first occurrence of a substring within a

string and returns the position where the string starts.

Syntax:

index = InStr(string, substring)

Comments:

If substring is not found within string, index = 0.

Example:

index = InStr("The brown cow", "cow") 

Returns:

index = 11

 

LCase    

Purpose:

The LCase function converts a string to lowercase.

Syntax:

x = LCase(string)

Comments:

Only uppercase letters are converted to lowercase.  All lower case letters and non letter characters remain unchanged.

Example:

x = LCase("HeLLo") 

Returns:

x = "hello"

 

Left    

Purpose:

The Left function returns the leftmost number of characters in a string.

Syntax:

x = Left(string, num)

Comments:

If num is greater than the length of the string, the entire string is returned.  If num is 0, an empty string is returned.

Example:

x = Left("LabWare", 3)    

Returns:

x = "Lab"

 

Len    

Purpose:

The Len function returns the number of characters in a string.

Syntax:

x = Len(string)

Comments:

Non-printing characters and blanks are counted.

Example:

x = Len("Hi there.")   

Returns:

x = 9

 

Mid    

Purpose:

The Mid function returns a string of characters from a specified position within a string

Syntax:

x = Mid(string, start, num)

start indicates the character position in string to start returning characters.

num indicates the number of characters to return.

If start is greater than the number of characters in the string, a zero length string is returned.

Comments:

 

Example:

x = Mid("Now is the time", 8, 8) 

Returns:

x = "the time"

 

PrettyString    

Purpose:

The PrettyString function reformats a string so that (for example) it can be used as a display title or field label.

Syntax:

pretty = PrettyString(inputString)

inputString – the string to be formatted

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the entire input string to lowercase.

•LIMS converts the first character of the input string to uppercase.

•LIMS converts each character that follows an underscore character to uppercase.

•LIMS replaces each sequence of one or more underscore characters with a single space character.

•LIMS removes all leading and trailing blanks from the string.

•LIMS returns the formatted string.

NOTE:  This function is not intended to be used with input strings that contain non-printable characters.


Example:

The following example returns the string "Sample Number".

pretty = PrettyString("SAMPLE_NUMBER")

Returns:

The function returns a string.

 

Replace    

Purpose:

The Replace function replaces all occurrences of a pattern in an input string with a replacement string.

Syntax:

newString = Replace(inputString, pattern, replacementString)

inputString – the string in which values are to be replaced

pattern – the sub string to be replaced

replacementString – the value that is to replace each occurrence of the pattern

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the value of inputString is blank or missing, LIMS will trigger the error "Input string not defined".

•If the value of pattern is blank or missing, LIMS will trigger the error "Pattern not defined".

•If no value is specified for replacementString, an empty string will be used instead.

•LIMS replaces all occurrences of pattern in inputString with replacementString and returns the result.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example replaces all occurrences of the string "low" with the string "high" in the file name "c:\myfile.txt".

mycontent = FileContents("c:\myfile.txt")
newcontent = Replace(mycontent, "low", "high")
status = FileWriteContents("c:\myfile.txt",newcontent)

Returns:

The function returns the modified string if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

Right    

Purpose:

The Right function returns the rightmost number of characters in a string.

Syntax:

x = Right(string, num)

Comments:

If num is equal to or greater than the length of the string, the entire string is returned.  If num is 0, the return value is a zero-length string

Example:

x = Right("Now is the time", 8)  

Returns:

x = "the time"

 

Space    

Purpose:

The Space function returns a string of spaces.

Syntax:

x = Space(number)

Comments:

number is the number of spaces in the string.

Example:

x = Space(5)    

Returns:

x = "     "

 

Str    

Purpose:

The Str function returns a string representation of a number.

Syntax:

string = Str(Number)

Number – integer to be converted to text.

Comments:

 

Example:

nmbr = 11

string = Str(nmbr)  

Returns:

string = "11"

 

StringReplace    

Purpose:

This function evaluates an input string and replaces a pattern of characters with a replacement string.

Syntax:

newString = StringReplace(inputString, stringToReplace, replacementString, caseSensitive)

inputString – (text, Required) target string to be evaluated

stringToReplace – (text, Required) string of characters to be replaced.

replacementString – (text, Optional) string of characters to be inserted in place.  Default is null.

caseSensitive – (Boolean, Optional) If set to false, the function will replace any occurrences where the text matches the characters in the stringToReplace parameter.  If set to true, only occurrences that match the exact case used in the stringToReplace will be replaced. If not specified, this defaults to True.

Comments:

LIMS evaluates the stringToReplace parameter.  If empty, LIMS returns the last error: Pattern must be specified."

Example:

inputString = "Colour test."

stringToReplace = "Colour"

replacementString = "Color"

caseSensitive = "F"

newString  = StringReplace(inputString, stringToReplace, replacementString, caseSensitive)

 

The output of the function is:

newString = "Color test"

Returns:

The function returns the modified string.  If unsuccessful, returns ERROR and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

StripChars    

Purpose:

The StripChars function removes all occurrences of one or more characters from a specified text string.

Syntax:

newString = StripChars(inputString, charsToStrip)

inputString – the text from which characters are to be removed.

charsToStrip – a text string that consists of all the characters that are to be removed from the input string

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no charsToStrip are specified, LIMS will return the input string.

•LIMS makes a copy of the input string.

•For each character in charsToString, LIMS removes all occurrences of that character from the copied value.

•LIMS returns the result.

Example:

The following example removes all parentheses, hyphens, and spaces from a telephone number and returns just the digits.

formattedPhone = "(302) 658 – 8444"
justDigits = StripChars(formattedPhone, "( –)" )
’ value of justDigits is "3026588444"

Returns:

The function returns a text value.

 

StrNoExp    

Purpose:

The StrNoExp function converts a exponential numeric expression into a number.

Syntax:

string = StrNoExp(aNumber)  

Comments:

 

Example:

string = Str(4e2)  

Returns:

string = "400.0"

 

Trim    

Purpose:

The Trim function returns a copy of a string with the leading and trailing spaces removed.

Syntax:

newString = Trim(string)

Comments:

 

Example:

newString = Trim("   Now is the time     ")

Returns:

newString = "Now is the time"

 

UCase    

Purpose:

The UCase function converts a string to uppercase.

Syntax:

x = UCase(string)

Comments:

Only lowercase letters are converted to uppercase, all uppercase letters and non letter characters remain unchanged.

Example:

x = UCase("Hi There!")

Returns:

x = "HI THERE!"

 

UnicodeToAscii    

Purpose:

The UnicodeToAscii function converts a Unicode encoded string to ASCII.

Syntax:

ascii = UnicodeToAscii(unicodeString)

unicodeString – the unicode text to be converted to ASCII

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS converts the Unicode text to plain text and returns the result.

Example:

The following example converts the contents of a Unicode file to ASCII text.

unicodeString = FileContents("mydoc.txt")
ascii = UnicodeToAscii(unicodeString)

Returns:

The function returns a plain text value.

AnalysisApproval    

Purpose:

The AnalysisApproval function approves a given user for a given analysis and optionally updates one or more fields in the analysis approval record.

Syntax:

status = AnalysisApproval(userName, analysis, approver, approvalDate, approvalInterval, logComment, fields, values)

userName – the user name of the user to be approved

Analysis – the name of the analysis for which the user is to be approved

approver – a boolean flag that indicates whether the user is authorized to approve others for the same analysis.  If this value is blank, LIMS will assume the value false.

approvalDate – the date on which the approval was granted

approvalInterval – the number of days for which the approval remains valid.  This value is ignored in the case of a recertification.

logComment – in the case of recertification, a comment to be included in the log.  This value is ignored in the case of an initial certification.

fields – an array that contains the names of the fields to be updated

values – an array that contains the values to be assigned to each of the fields

Note: the fields and values arguments are optional.  However, if you specify one, you must specify both and the two arrays must be the same size.  The behavior of LIMS is unspecified if these conditions are not met and is likely to result in a runtime error.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the user record for the specified user name.  If this record does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "The user record does not exist, or is removed".

•LIMS retrieves the analysis record for the specified analysis name.  If this record does not exist, LIMS will trigger the error "The analysis does not exist".

•LIMS retrieves the analysis approval record for the specified user and analysis.  If this record does not exist, LIMS will perform the following steps.

•a.   LIMS creates a new analysis approval record for the specified user and analysis.

•LIMS sets the last certification date of the new record equal to the specified approval date.

•LIMS sets the approval interval of the new record equal to the specified interval.

•LIMS sets the approver flag equal to the specified boolean value (false if left blank).

•If the fields and values arrays were specified, LIMS will perform the following steps for each field/value pair.

•If the specified field name does not exist in the analysis approval table, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified value does not have the correct data type or, in the case of a list or link field, the value does not appear in the corresponding list or table, LIMS will skip this field/value pair without triggering an error.

•LIMS updates the specified field in the analysis approval record with the specified value.

•LIMS saves the analysis approval record to the database, and writes a record to the audit trail with audit type "Certified".

•If the analysis record does exist, LIMS will perform the following steps instead.

•a. LIMS creates a new user analysis log record.

•b. LIMS sets the event date of the new log record equal to the specified approval date.

•c. LIMS sets the event name of the new log record equal to "Recertified".

•d. LIMS sets the comment of the new log record equal to the specified comment.

•e. LIMS sets the last certification date of the analysis approval record equal to the specified approval date.

•f. If the fields and values arrays were specified, LIMS will perform the following steps for each field/value pair.

•If the specified field name does not exist in the analysis approval table, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid field for table".

•If the specified value does not have the correct data type or, in the case of a list or link field, the value does not appear in the corresponding list or table, LIMS will skip this field/value pair without triggering an error.

•LIMS updates the specified field in the analysis approval record with the specified value.

•LIMS saves the analysis approval record to the database, and writes a record to the audit trail with audit type "Recertified".

•LIMS saves the new log record to the database, and writes a record to the audit trail with audit type "Recertified".

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following example approves user JSMITH for the PH analysis for 90 days.

u = "JSMITH"
a = "PH"
d = Date()
status = AnalysisApproval(u, a, "F", d, 90, "")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

AnalysisRemoveApproval    

Purpose:

The AnalysisRemoveApproval function modifies the targeted user account by removing approval for the specified analysis.

Syntax:

status = AnalysisRemoveApproval(userName, analysis, logComment)

userName – name of the user account to be updated.

Analysis – name of the analysis to be removed from the user account’s approvals.

logComment – string posted to the log for the event.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the specified user name is valid.  If the user name is not valid, the LIMS returns the last error: "The user record does not exist, or is removed."

•LIMS verifies that the analysis name is valid.  If the analysis is not valid, LIMS returns the error: "The analysis does not exist:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS verified that the specified user has approval for the specified analysis.  If the user does not have approval, LIMS returns the following error:
"Approval for analysis does not exist:" followed by the input value.

•If successful, the analysis approval will be removed for the specified user.

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following code updates the user account by removing approval for the specified analysis.

userName = "DEMO_B"
analysis = "DEMO_A"
logComment = "Documentation"
status = AnalysisRemoveApproval(userName, analysis, logComment)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

AssignSpecTests    

Purpose:

The AssignSpecTests function assigns tests to a sample according to the Product Specifications definition. 

Syntax:

status = AssignSpecTests(sampleNumber, product, grade, samplingPoint, stage, specType)

sampleNumber – integer defining the sample number of the parent sample of the target test.

product –  string defining the product.

grade –  string defining the product grade.

samplingPoint –  string defining the sampling point

stage –   string defining the stage.

specType –  string defining the specification type.

Comments:

All fields must be passed to the function.  Empty strings are permissible where there is enough information to do the test assignment. 

NOTE: This function does not do a database commit.


Example:

status = AssignSpecTests(1, "CHOC", "NONE" , "NONE", "NONE", "NONE")

Returns:

status =  true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

AssignTest    

Purpose:

The AssignTest function assigns the specified number or replicates of the defined analysis to the target sample and returns an array of the assigned test numbers  The function also supports controlled population of the test fields.

Syntax:

status = AssignTest(sampNum, analysisName, numReps, "testNumberArrayName",fieldsArray, valuesArray, maxReps, parentTestNumber, version)

SampNum – the number of the sample the test is to be added to.

AnalysisName – the name of the Analysis to be used as a template for the assigned test. The Analysis must not be inactive.

NumReps – the number of replicates of the test to be added.

TestNumberArrayName- the name of the one-dimensional array containing Test Numbers

FieldsArray- one-dimensional array containing the field names to be updated by the function.

ValuesArray- one-dimensional array returning the values to be populated into the specified fields.

MaxReps- The maximum number of replicates to be assigned to the sample.

ParentTestNumber- The number of the test to act as a Parent Test, this number is written to the PARENT_TEST field.  If null, the test is assigned to the sample.

Version – (optional) – the version of the analysis to add.  If not present, the current active version will be used.

Comments:

When this function is called within LIMS Basic, the system performs the following actions:

•The system evaluates SampNum.  If the sample number is not valid, the system returns the following LastError: “Sample does not exist:  SampNum”.

•The system evaluates the status of the sample.  If the sample has a status of Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled, The system returns the error: “Sample is reviewed or canceled: SampNum”.

•The system evaluates the ParentTestNumber.  If the test number is not a valid test number for the database, the system returns the error: “The specified parent test does not exist on the sample: ParentTestNumber”.

•The system evaluates the AnalysisName and Version.  If the combination of analysis and version not a valid, the system returns the error: “Invalid analysis/version specified: AnalysisName / Version.

•The system evaluates the MaxReps.  If this value is not null and the specified number of tests already exist on the sample, then the system returns the error: “No tests added for specified analysis: AnalysisName”.

•If the MaxReps is null or greater than the existing tests with the specified Analysis, The system added the appropriate number of tests.

•The system then populates the fields defined on the FieldsArray with the values within the ValuesArray.  The system also sets the Parent_Test field with the ParentTestNumber.

 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example adds 2 replicates of the analysis PLATE_SBA, version 2 as child tests to the test with the sample number: 2 and the test number: 6.

sn = 2
anName = "PLATE_SBA"
nReps = 2
tnParent = 6

fieldsArray[1] = "REPORTED_NAME"

valuesArray[1] = "Plate SBA"

version = 2

status = AssignTest(sn, anName, nReps, "tns" ,fieldsArray, valuesArray, , tnParent, version)

Save()

 

Returns:

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  It returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

AssignTests

Purpose:

Assigns the specified number or replicates of the defined analyses to the target sample and returns an array of the assigned test numbers.  This function also provides arrays to populate fields in the Test table for the assigned tests.

Syntax:

status = AssignTests(sample, analysisArray, numRepsArray, "testNumberArrayName", fieldsArray, valuesArray, maxRepsArray, parentTestNumberArray, versionArray)

sample – the number of the sample to which the tests are to be added.

analysisArray – a one-dimensional array for names of the Analyses to be used as a template for the assigned tests. The Analyses must not be inactive.

numRepsArray – a one-dimensional array for the number of replicates of the tests to be added.

testNumberArrayName- the name of the return array containing the assigned Test Numbers

fieldsArray- Two-dimensional array containing the field names to be updated by the function.

valuesArray- Two-dimensional array returning the values to be populated into the specified fields.

maxRepsArray - a one-dimensional array for the maximum number of replicates to be assigned to the sample for the analysis

parentTestNumberArray – (optional) a one-dimensional array for the number of the test to act as a Parent Test, this number is written to the PARENT_TEST field.  If null, the test is assigned to the sample.

versionArray – (optional) – a one –dimensional array of the analysis versions to add.  If not present the current active version will be used.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system evaluates analysisArray, numRepArray, and versionArray.  If they do not have the same number of elements, the system will trigger the error: “Error: all arrays must be the same size”.

•The system evaluates fieldsArray.  If it is not a 2 dimensional array, the system will trigger the error: “Error: field names array must be two dimensional”.

•The system evaluates valuesArray.  If it is not a 2 dimensional array, the system will trigger the error: “Error: field values array must be two dimensional”.

•The system evaluates the sample.  If the sample number is not valid, the system will trigger the error: “Sample does not exist:  sample.

•The system evaluates the status of the sample.  If the sample has a status of Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled, the system will trigger the error: “Sample is reviewed or canceled:  sample.

•The system evaluates the ParentTestNumber.  If the test number is not a valid test number for the database, the system will trigger the error: “The specified parent test does not exist on the sample:” followed by the input ParentTestNumber.

•The system evaluates the AnalysisName and Version.  If the combination of analysis and version not a valid, the system returns the error: “Invalid analysis/version specified: AnalysisName / Version.

•The system evaluates the MaxReps.  If this value is not null and the specified number of tests already exist on the sample, then the system will trigger the error: “No tests added for specified analysis:” followed by the input AnalysisName.

•If the MaxReps is null or greater than the existing tests with the specified Analysis, The system will add the appropriate number of tests.

•The system then populates the fields defined on the fieldsArray with the values within the Values Array.  The system also sets the Parent_Test field with the ParentTestNumber.

 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

 

 

sample = 84

analysisArray[1] = "LB_PH"

numRepsArray[1] = 2

versionArray[1] = 1

maxRepsArray[1] = 3

 

fieldsArray[1,1] = "REPORTED_NAME"

valuesArray[1,1] = "pH"

status = AssignTests(sample, analysisArray, numRepsArray, "testNumberArrayName",fieldsArray, valuesArray ,maxRepsArray , , versionArray)

Returns:

 

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  It returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

AssignTestList    

Purpose:

The AssignTestList function assigns a test list to a sample.

Syntax:

status = AssignTestList(sampleNumber, testList, parentTestNumber)

sampleNumber – integer defining the sample number of the target sample.

testList –  string defining the test list to be assigned.

parentTestNumber – integer defining the test number of the test that will defined as the parent test for the tests added by the test list. This must be a test on the target sample. This parameter is optional.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The system checks the testList parameter.  If no test list has been specified, the system will trigger the error "Test list not specified".  If an invalid testList is specified, the system will trigger the error " Test list does not exist: testList ".

•The system checks the sampleNumber parameter.  If an invalid sampleNumber is specified, the system will trigger the error "Sample does not exist: sampleNumber".

•The system checks the sampleNumber status.  If status is Authorized, Rejected or Canceled, the system will trigger the error "Sample is reviewed or canceled: sampleNumber".

•The system tries to assign the test to the sample.  If there is a problem assigning the tests, the system will trigger the error " Error assigning tests for list: testList ".

•If specified, the system checks the parentTestNumber parameter.  If an invalid test number is specified, the system will trigger the error "The specified parent test does not exist on the sample: parentTestNumber '.  If the corresponding test status is Authorized, Rejected or Canceled, the system will trigger the error "The specified parent test has been reviewed or cancelled".

The system puts the tests in context for the sample.

Example:

status = AssignTestList(1, "MICRO_TLIST")

Returns:

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

AssignTestToSamples    

Purpose:

The AssignTestToSamples function assigns the specified number of replicates of the defined analysis to the target samples and returns an array of the assigned test numbers.  The function also supports controlled population of the test fields.

Syntax:

status = AssignTestToSamples(sampleArray, analysis, numReps, testNumberArray, fieldsArray, valuesArray, maxReps)

sampleArray – a one dimensional array containing a series of sample numbers as integers.

analysis – the name of the analysis to be assigned to the sample.

numReps – the number of replicates to assign This value must be an integer.  If numReps is not specified the value defaults to 1.

"testNumberArray" – the name of the 1 dimensional array used by LIMS to return the test number of the assigned tests.  This value must be set in quotes.

fieldsArray – the name of the 1 dimensional array that contains the field names of the assigned tests to be populated when the test is assigned.

valuesArray – the name of the 1 dimensional array that contains the field values of the assigned tests to be inserted when the test is assigned.

Maxreps – the maximum number of replicates the sample can have when the function is triggered.  This value must be an integer.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Sample Number provided is a valid entry.  If there is no sample with the provided sample number, the LIMS return:
"Sample does not exist:" followed by the sample number.

•LIMS verifies that the sample has not been canceled or reviewed.  If the sample has been canceled or reviewed, the LIMS returns:
"Sample is reviewed or canceled:" followed by the sample number.

•LIMS determines the number of replicates the sample should have.

•a.   If the maxRep value is populated, LIMS will check the current number of replicates on the sample and will insert analysis replicates according to the numReps value, as long as the maxRep value is not exceeded.  If the current number of replicates on the sample equals or exceeds the maxRep value, no replicates will be added.

•b.   If the maxRep value is not populated, LIMS will insert the number of replicates specified by the numRep input.

•LIMS assigns the required number of analysis replicates with the audit type: AddTest.  While adding the assign tests, LIMS populates the fields defined in the fieldArray with the values defined in the valuesArary.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.  To post the tests to the samples, a save must be triggered within the LIM Basic.


Example:

The following code adds two replicates of the DEMO_A analysis to samples 9 and 11.

SampleArray[1] = 9
SampleArray[2] = 11
analysis = "DEMO_A"
numReps = "2"
fieldsArray[1] = "TEST_COMMENT"
fieldsArray[2] = "PRIORITY"
valuesArray[1] = "Documenting AssignTestToSamples"
valuesArray[2] = 1
maxReps = 5

status = AssignTestToSamples(sampleArray, analysis, numReps, "testNumberArray", fieldsArray, valuesArray, maxReps)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

AssignTestWithVariation    

Purpose:

The AssignTestWithVariation function assigns the specified analysis variation to the specified sample and optionally assigns values to the created test record(s).

Syntax:

status = AssignTestWithVariation(sample, analysis, numReps, variation, testNumberArray, fieldsArray, valuesArray, parentTestNumber, maxReps)

sample – the number of the sample the test is to be added to.

analysis – the name of the Analysis to be used as a template for the assigned test.

numReps – the number of replicates of the test to be added.

variation – identity of the variation of the analysis to be applied to the test.

testNumberArrayName- the array containing Test Numbers

fieldsArray- a one-dimensional array containing the field names to be updated by the function.

valuesArray- a one-dimensional array returning the values to be populated into the specified fields.

parentTestNumber – The number of the test to act as a Parent Test, this number is written to the PARENT_TEST field.  If null, the test is assigned to the sample.

maxReps – integer defining the maximum number of replicates for the test.

Comments:

When this function is called within LIMS Basic, the LIMS performs the following actions:

•The LIMS evaluates the sample number.  If the sample number is not valid, the system returns the following LastError: Sample does not exist:  followed by the input sample number.

•The LIMS evaluates the status of the sample.  If the sample has a status of Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled, the LIMS returns the LastError: Sample is reviewed or canceled:  followed by the input sample number.

•The LIMS evaluates the ParentTestNumber.  If the test number is not a valid test number for the database, the LIMS returns the LastError: The specified parent test does not exist on the sample:  followed by the input ParentTestNumber.

•The LIMS evaluates the NumReps.  If this value is 0 or null, then the LIMS returns the LastError: No tests added for specified analysis:  followed by the input AnalysisName.

•The LIMS evalutes the Varitation input.  If the variation is not valid, the LIMS returns the LastError: ‘No tests added for specified analysis:’ followed by the input analysis.  Then: ‘variation:’ followed by the input variation.

•The LIMS adds the input number of replicate tests using the specified analysis template and applying the specified variation.

•The LIMS then populates the fields defined on the FieldArray with the values within the Values Array.  The LIMS also sets the Parent_Test field with the ParentTestNumber.

NOTE: A database commit is not part of this function. 

Example:

The following example adds 2 replicates of the analysis PLATE_SBA as child tests to the test with the sample number: 2 and the test number: 6.  The assigned tests reflect variation A1.

sn = 2
anName = "PLATE_SBA"
nReps = 2
var = "A1"
tnParent = 6

status = AssignTest(sn, anName, nReps, var,"tns",, , , tnParent)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully, else false.

 

AuthorizeTest    

Purpose:

The AuthorizeTest function authorizes a test.

Syntax:

status = AuthorizeTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

sampleNumber – integer defining the sample number of the parent sample of the target test.

testNumber – integer defining the test number of the target test.

Comments:

Test status must be Complete or Rejected (In-Progress samples may be authorized if business rules allow it).  Sample status mustnot be Canceled, Authorized or Incomplete.

NOTE:  This  function does not do a database commit.


Example: 

sampleNumber = 35

testNumber  = 99

status = AuthorizeTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

CancelTest    

Purpose:

The CancelTest function cancels a test in a sample.

Syntax:

status = CancelTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

sampleNumber – integer defining the Sample Number of the parent sample of the target test.

testNumber – integer defining the Test Number of the target test.

Comments:

Test status must be Incomplete, In Progress, or Complete. Sample status must be Unreceived, Incomplete, In Progress or Complete.

This  function does not do a database commit.

NOTE:  The business rules, function privileges and CONTINUE_CANCEL status rule is checked before this function is performed.


Example:

testNumber = TestFld(748, "C_BOT", "TEST_NUMBER")

status = CancelTest(748, testNumber)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

MoveTest

Purpose:

Moves one or more tests from one sample to another sample.

Syntax:

status = moveTest(sourceSample,testNumbersArray, targetSample)

sourceSample – The sample number from which the tests are being moved

testNumberArray – one-dimensional array of test numbers to be moved

targetSample – The sample number to which the tests are being moved

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system checks the sourceSample.  If the sample number is not valid, the system will trigger the error: “Sample does not exist:  sourceSample.

•The system checks the targetSample.  If the sample number is not valid, the system will trigger the error: “Sample does not exist:  targetSample.

•The system evaluates the targetSample.  If the sample number is authorized, canceled or rejected, the system will trigger the error: “Sample is reviewed or canceled:  targetSample.

•The system check the size of testNumberArray. If not specified, the system will trigger the error: “No tests specified”.

•The system check the tests in the testNumberArray. If a test is not on the sourceSample, the system will trigger the error: “Test does not exist:” followed by the test number. If a test is canceled, the system will trigger the error: “Test already canceled”.  If a test has results entered, the system will trigger the error: “Test has results”. Otherwise the test will be moved with an Audit transaction of “EditTests” on the target sample and ‘RemoveTest’ on the source sample.

 

NOTE: This function does save changes to the database.


Example:

 

 

sourceSample = 368

testNumbersArray[1] = 991

testNumbersArray[2] = 995

targetSample = 541

status = moveTest(sourceSample,testNumbersArray, targetSample)

Returns:

 

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  It returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

OptionalTests    

Purpose:

The OptionalTests function assigns the optional tests for a sample.

Syntax:

status = OptionalTests(sampleNumber)

Comments:

NOTE:  This function does not do a database commit.

NOTE:  This function will not add the optional test if it is already present on the sample.


Example:

status = OptionalTests(748)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

ProductSpecTests    

Purpose:

The ProductSpecTests function retrieves the names of all analyses based on the product specifications for the sample with a Boolean indicating if they are assigned to the specified sample.

Syntax:

status = ProductSpecTests(sampleNumber, "analysisNameArray")

sampleNumber – the sample number of the sample to be checked

analysisNameArray – the name of the array (as a string in double quotes, i.e., not the array itself) in which LIMS will place the names of the analyses that match the sample Product,Grade, Sampling Point and Stage.  The array returns the analysis name, stage, number of reps that need to be assigned ( this is the Num Reps from the product minus the currently assigned reps) and a Boolean that indicates whether the test is assigned or not.

NOTE:  Some entries in the Stage table may have Num Reps set to 0 in the spec so the Boolean will indicate if the test is assigned to the sample or not.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the specified sample.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified sample does not exist".

•If the value of analysisNameArray is missing or blank, LIMS will trigger the error "No analysis array name was specified".

•LIMS retrieves the names of all analyses that appear on the product specifications for the sample but that have not yet been assigned to the sample.  This includes any analyses for which the product specifications require more replicates than have actually been assigned.  LIMS places the names of the analyses in an array with the specified array name.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example finds all analysis that appear on the product specifications for sample 4192 but have not yet been assigned to that sample.  The analysis names are stored in the array named "pendingAnalyses". 

status = ProductSpecTests(4192, "pendingAnalyses")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ProductSpecTestsList    

Purpose:

The ProductSpecTestsList function creates a list of analyses that are defined for one or more product grade stages.

Syntax:

status = ProductSpecTestsList(product, version, grade, samplingPoint, stage, specType, "analysisNameArray")

product – the name of the product (mandatory)

version – the version of the specified product (optional; the latest version will be used if no version is specified)

grade – the name of the grade for the specified product (optional; all grades will be used if no grade is specified)

samplingPoint – the name of the sampling point for the specified product (optional; all sampling points will be used if no sampling point is specified)

stage – the name of the stage of the specified product (optional; all stages will be used if no stage is specified)

specType – the name of the spec type for the specified product.  This Spec Type and the Spec Type of NONE will be checked (optional; if no spec type is specified only the spec type of NONE will be used.)

analysisNameArray – the name of the LIMS Basic array that should be created.  Note that this argument is a text value that contains the name of the array.  It is not the array itself.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If an integer version number is specified, LIMS will retrieve the product record for the specified product name and version.  Otherwise, LIMS will retrieve the latest version of the specified product name.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "No product is specified".

•If no value was specified for analysisNameArray, LIMS will trigger the error "No analysis array name was specified".

•LIMS retrieves all stages for the retrieved product record that match the specified grade, sampling  point, stage, and spec type, and that have at least one replicate specified.

•LIMS creates an analysis array with the specified name and adds elements for each retrieved stage as follows.

•LIMS adds the analysis name of the stage to the array.

•If the stage has no specs, LIMS will add two empty strings to the array.

•Otherwise, for each spec, LIMS will add the component name and the entry code of the spec to the array.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example creates an array named "MyList" with all analyses at sampling point "TOP" for product "ASPIRIN".

status = ProductSpecTestsList("ASPIRIN", "", "", "TOP", "", "", "MyList")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ReactivateTest    

Purpose:

The ReactivateTest function reactivates the specified test based on test number or associated analysis.

CAUTION: The LB command ReactiveTest() does not check for the case of cross-test calculations, where tests need to be handled as a group of inter-dependant tests. In such systems, this command should be used in conjunction with the LB command InputsToCalcsOnATest().


Syntax:

status = ReactivateTest(sample, testNumberOrAnalysisName)

sample – integer defining the sample number of the parent sample of the target test

testNumberOrAnalysisName – string defining the test number or the associated analysis of the target test.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the specified sample.  If unsuccessful, LIMS returns the last error: Sample does not exist.

•LIMS retrieves the specified test.  If unsuccessful, LIMS returns the last error: Test does not exist.

•The ReactivateTest command is submitted to Status Manager where standard rules for reactivating tests are applied.

NOTE: This function does not perform a database commit.

Example:

status = ReactivateTest(741, 800)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, otherwise false.

 

RefreshTest    

Purpose:

The RefreshTest function ensures that the test being referenced in LIMS Basic is in sync with  the database.

Syntax:

status = RefreshTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

Comments:

Since Tests can be modified outside of LIMS Basic (i.e. Test Editor) while LIMS Basic is executing, this function ensures that LIMS Basic is working with committed data.

Example:

status = RefreshTest(741, 800)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

RejectTest    

Purpose:

The RejectTest function rejects a test and the results associated with a test.

Syntax:

status = RejectTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

Comments:

Test status must be Complete or Authorized (In-Progress samples may be rejected if business rules allow it).  Sample status mustnot be Canceled, Authorized or Rejected.

This  function does not do a database commit.

NOTE:  The business rules, function privileges and CONTINUE_REJECT status rule is checked before this function is performed.


Example:

testNumber = TestFld(732, "C_BOT", "TEST_NUMBER")

status = RejectTest(732, testNumber)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

ReleaseTest    

Purpose:

The ReleaseTest function is used to release a test associated with a sample.

Syntax:

status = ReleaseTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

sampleNumber – number of the Sample whose test is to be released

testNumber – number of the Test for that Sample

Comments:

NOTE: A database commit is not part of this function.

Example:

The following example is used to release Test Number 1 of Sample Number 1

Sample Number = 1

Test Number = 1

status = Release Test (sample Number, test Number)

Returns:

status = true if the function is successful, false otherwise.

 

RemoveTest    

Purpose:

The RemoveTest function removes a test from a sample.

Syntax:

status = RemoveTest(sampleId, test_number)

Comments:

 

If test status is Incomplete, the test is removed from the sample.  If test status is In Progress or Complete, the test is Canceled.  If test status is Authorized, the function fails.

NOTE: This function does not perform a database commit.


Example:

status = RemoveTest(33, 70)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise. 

 

ReplicateTest    

Purpose:

The ReplicateTest function creates test replicates.

Syntax:

newTestNumber = replicateTest(sampleNumber, testNumber)

Comments:

newTestNumber is the test number of the newly added test.

This function does not do a database commit.

Example:

newTestNumber = replicateTest(48, 103)

Returns:

newTestNumber = 224

 

RestoreTest    

Purpose:

This function restores the specified test.

Syntax:

status = RestoreTest(sampleNumber,testNumber)

sampleNumber – integer defining the sample number of the parent sample of the target test.

testNumber – integer defining the test number of the target test.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•LIMS retrieves the specified sample.  If unsuccessful, the LIMS returns the last error: Sample does not exist.

•LIMS retrieves the specified test.  If unsuccessful, the LIMS returns the last error: Test does not exist.

•LIMS verifies tha the test can be restored.

•LIMS restores the test.

NOTE: This function does not perform a database commit.

Example:

Status = RestoreTest(275, 863)

Returns:

status = true if function is successful, otherwise false.

 

SetTestDisplayFields   

Purpose:

The SetTestDisplayFields function sets the fields that will be displayed in the right-hand pane when a test is selected in a window such as the Folder Manager window.

Syntax:

status = SetTestDisplayFields(fieldNames)

fieldNames – an array in which each element is the name of a field in the test table.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that each field name exists in the test table.  If at least one field does not exist in the test table, LIMS will trigger the error "Field does not exist in the test table".

•LIMS sets the display fields for the test table equal to the specified field names.

•It is acceptable to specify an empty array.  In that case, all fields will be displayed.

NOTE:  This specified field names will remain in effect until they are modified or until the user logs out, whichever happens first.


Example:

The following example causes the system to show only the test number and analysis name when a sample is selected in Folder Manager.

myFields[1] = "TEST_NUMBER"
myFields[2] = "ANALYSIS"
status = SetTestDisplayFields(myFields)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

TestFld    

Purpose:

The TestFld function returns the value of a test field in a sample.

 Syntax:

x = TestFld(sampleNumber, testNumberOrAnalysisName, field)

Comments:

field is any field in the Test table.

Example:

x = TestFld(10, "COLOR", "REVIEWER")

Returns:

x = "BROWN-J"

 

TestFlds    

Purpose:

The TestFlds function returns returns an array containing the values of a test field for all the test records in a sample.

Syntax:

status = TestFlds(sampleNumber, fieldName, "arrayName")

Comments:

field is any field in the Test table. Values are returned in test.order_number order.  If field is invalid, null values are returned.

Example:

status = TestFlds(97, "ANALYSIS", "theAnalyses")

Returns: 

 

theAnalyses[1] = "LOD" 

theAnalyses[2] = "APC"

theAnalyses[3] = "FAT"

theAnalyses[4] = "MOISTURE"

 

UpdateTestFld    

Purpose:

The UpdateTestFld function updates a field in a test.

Syntax:

status = UpdateTestFld(sampleNumber, testNumber, field, value, commitFlag)

sampleNumber – sample number of the parent sample of the target test.

testNumber – test number of the target test.

field – string defining the test field to be updated.

value – value to be posted to the specified field.

commitFlag – a Boolean, If set to T, the data is committed immediately, otherwise the data is committed during the next commit to the sample (the next commit may happen when a later LIMS Basic function performs a commit or when a Save operation is performed). The default value is false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system retrieves the specified sample. If unsuccessful, the system returns the last error: Sample does not exist.

•The system retrieves the specified test. If unsuccessful, the system returns the last error: Test does not exist.

•The system evaluates the specified field.  If the field is not on the Test table, the system returns the error:  Invalid field for table: Test.

•The system evaluates the specified value.  If it is an invalid value, the system returns the error:  Invalid value for test record: <value> for field: <field>.

•The system updates the field values in cache if the commitFlag is false, otherwise it is committed to the database.

NOTE: You cannot use this function update the test status since the test status is recalculated after this function is performed and will be reset to the calculated value.


Example:

sampleNumber = 48

testNumber = 103

field = "TEST_LOCATION"

value = "LAB_A"

commitFlag = "T"

 

status = UpdateTestFld(sampleNumber, testNumber, field, value, commitFlag)

Returns:

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  It returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

UpdateTestFlds    

Purpose:

This function updates multiple fields on a specified test.

Syntax:

Status = UpdateTestFlds(sampleNumber, testNumber, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

sampleNumber – sample number of the parent sample of the target test.

testNumber – test number of the target test.

fieldsArray – string defining the test field to be updated.

valuesArray – value to be posted to the specified field.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The system compares the fieldsArray with the valuesArray.  If they are not the same size, the system returns the last error: Error: field and value arrays must be the same size.

•The system retrieves the specified sample. If unsuccessful, the system returns the last error: Sample does not exist.

•The system retrieves the specified test. If unsuccessful, the system returns the last error: Test does not exist.

•The system evaluates the specified fields.  If there is a field not on the Test table, the system returns the error:  Invalid field for table: Test.

•The system evaluates the specified values.  If there is an invalid value, the system returns the error:  Invalid value for test record: <value> for field: <field>.

•The system updates the field values in cache.

 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.

Example:

sampleNumber = 1137

testNumber = 8847

fieldsArray[1] = "TEST_COMMENT"

fieldsArray[2] = "DATE_RECEIVED"

valuesArray[1] = "LIMS Basic demo"

valuesArray[2] = date()

Status = UpdateTestFlds(sampleNumber, testNumber, fieldsArray, valuesArray)

Returns:

The function returns the status of True if it completes successfully.  It returns the status of False if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.47  Visual Workflow Functions
Function

CloseVisualWorkflow

ExecuteVisualWorkflowScript

GenerateVisualWorkflow

OpenVisualWorkflow

RefreshVisualWorkflow

 

CloseVisualWorkflow        

Purpose:

The CloseVisualWorkflow function closes an open Visual Workflow window.

Syntax:

status = CloseVisualWorkflow(workflowName)

workflowName – the workflow which is to be closed.

Comments:

Useful when calling one workflow from another and want to remove the original workflow window. 

 

An error "No workflow was specified" is returned, if the value in workflowName is empty ("").

Example:

The following example closes an open Visual workflow called VW_1.

status = CloseVisualWorkflow("VW_1")

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

ExecuteVisualWorkflowScript  

Purpose:

This function applies Java Script to the workflow in context. 

Syntax:

returnValue = ExecuteVisualWorkflowScript(javascript)

javascript – string representing the Java Script to be executed on the workflow in context.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that the current workflow is not part of a WebLIMS process.  If it is part of a WebLIMS process, the status returns false and the last error is: Not implemented in WebLIMS.

•LIMS verifies that there is a visual workflow in context.  If there is not a visual workflow in context, the status returns false and the last error is: No workflow found.

•LIMS executes the Java Script and applies it to the current workflow in context.

Example:

javascript = "document.bgColor = 'CCFFCC';"

returnValue = ExecuteVisualWorkflowScript(javascript)

This example turns the background of the Visual Workflow green.

Returns:

If successful, the returnValue is determined by the JavaScript.  It returns the value "ERROR" if an error is triggered.

 

GenerateVisualWorkflow  

Purpose:

This function produces the temporary .htm file LIMS uses to display the visual workflow without displaying the workflow in the main pane.

Syntax:

htmlFile = GenerateVisualWorkflow(workflowName)

workflowName – name of the visual workflow record to be used to generate the temporary file.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that the workflow name submitted is valid.  If not, LIMS populates the LastError with:  "Invalid workflow specified: " followed by the submitted value.

•LIMS produces the temporary htm file and places it in the external directory for visual workflows.

Example:

workflowName = "DEMO"

htmlFile = GenerateVisualWorkflow(workflowName)

Returns:

If successful the function returns a reference to the generated HTML file; otherwise, ERROR. 

 

OpenVisualWorkflow    

Purpose:

The OpenVisualWorkflow function opens the specified Visual Workflow.  If  Pre and/or Post arrays are specified then the workflow is built using these parameters and the workflow details defined in the specified workflow record are ignored.  This allows the workflow to be built on the fly.

Syntax:

status = OpenVisualWorkflow(workflowName, preWorkflowArray, postWorkflowArray)

workflowName – the name of the visual workflow to be opened.

preWorkflowArray – an optional one dimensional array that holds the names of the visual workflows that is opened in the same window before the workflowName visual workflow is opened.

postWorkflowArray – an optional one dimensional array that holds the names of the visual workflows that is opened in the same window after the workflowName visual workflow is opened.

Comments:

This function does the following:

•Evaluates workflowName.  If no value is specified for the argument, the lastError variable is set to “No workflow was specified” and the function returns false.

•Evaluates postWorkflowArray. If an invalid visual workflow name is specified, no error message is displayed.

•Evaluates preWorkflowArray. If an invalid visual workflow name is specified, no error message is displayed. Otherwise workflow is opened.

NOTE:  All Visual Workflows opened using LIMS Basic will be displayed in a new tab unless re-opening an open workflow.  .

Example:

This example will open the visual workflow with the name “EXAMPLE_A” in a tab in a new window.  The “VW_PRE” workflow will be opened in the same tab before the “EXAMPLE_A” workflow is opened.  The “VW_POST” workflow will be opened in the same tab after the “EXAMPLE_A” is opened.

workflowName = "EXAMPLE_A"

preWorkflowArray[1] = "VW_PRE"

postWorkflowArray[1] = "VW_POST"

status = OpenVisualWorkflow(workflowName, preWorkflowArray, postWorkflowArray)

Returns:

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

 

RefreshVisualWorkflow  

Purpose:

This function refreshes the workflow by recreating all the elements.  That means that all configured subroutines, stored queries, query tags, data explorers, and sql are re-triggered.

Syntax:

status = RefreshVisualWorkflow(workflowName, updateEntireWorkflow)

workflowName – the name of the visual workflow to be refreshed.

updateEntireWorkflow – a Boolean flag used to control whether the entire visual workflow is refreshed, or only individual elements that have been configured to be refreshed.  The default value is “F”.

Comments:

The function performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that a workflow name was submitted.  If not, LIMS populates the workflow name with the workflow currently in context.

•LIMS verifies that a workflow name was submitted.  If not, LIMS returns the status of false and populates the LastError with: "No workflow was specified."

•LIMS verifies that the workflow name submitted is valid.  If not, LIMS returns the status of false and populates the LastError with:  "Invalid workflow specified: " followed by the submitted value.

•Evaluates updateEntireWorkflow. If the argument is not specified, the value for the Update Entire Workflow field on the visual workflow record is used. If the Boolean flag is set to false, individual elements in the visual workflow that are configured to refresh will be refreshed, else the entire visual workflow will be re-rendered.

CAUTION: When using this function, the best practice is to call this function at the end of the routine.  Any functions used after this functioni may interfere with the refresh.


Example:

This example will cause individual elements in a visual workflow that have been configured to refresh.

workflowName = "EXAMPLE_A"

updateEntireWorkflow = "F"

status = RefreshVisualWorkflow(workflowName, updateEntireWorkflow)

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the status = true, otherwise false.

 

28.3.48  Web Service Functions
 

Function

CloseWebService

CreateWebService

InvokeWebService

 

CloseWebService      

Purpose:

The CloseWebService function closes a web service.

Syntax:

status = CloseWebService(serviceHandle)

serviceHandle – the service handle for the web service (as defined by the CreateWebService function).

Comments:

This function must be used after the CreateWebService function since serviceHandle is defined by the CreateWebService function. The function performs the following steps:

•If no service handle exists, LIMS will generate the error "No web service handle specified".

•If the service defined by the service handle is not valid, LIMS returns the error "Invalid web service handle specified:" followed by the service handle value.

•LIMS closes the specified web service.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

serviceHandle = CreateWebService("WEATHER")

‘(code to invoke web service)

status = CloseWebService(serviceHandle)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

 

CreateWebService  

Purpose:

The CreateWebService function creates a web service.

Syntax:

serviceHandle = CreateWebService(serviceName, optionalAuthUsername, optionalAuthPassword)

serviceName – the name of a Web Service, as defined in the Web Service table.

optionalAuthUsername – User name for windows authentication (optional)

optionalAuthPassword – Password for windows authentication (optional)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•If no serviceName exists, LIMS returns the error:  "No web service specified"

•If the service name is not valid, LIMS returns the error:
"Invalid web service specified:" followed by the service name value.

•LIMS creates the specified web service.

•If a username or password is specified, then Windows Authentication is used, with the specified username and password.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

serviceHandle = CreateWebService("WEATHER")

Returns:

The function returns the service handle of the created web service if successful. The function returns ERROR if it is not successful.

 

InvokeWebService     

Purpose:

The InvokeWebService function invokes a web service and returns the response.

NOTE: This function replaces the CallWebService() function.


Syntax:

serviceResponse = InvokeWebService(serviceHandle, xmlMessage, optionalHdrXml, optionalServicePath, optionalServiceAction)

serviceHandle – the service handle for the web service (as defined by the CreateWebService function).

xmlMessage – the message to be sent to the invoked web service.

optionalHdrXML – SOAP header XML string (optional).

optionalServicePath -  Service Path, overrides value in Web Service table (optional)

optionalServiceAction - Service Action, overrides value in Web Service table  (optional)

Comments:

This function must be used after the CreateWebService function since serviceHandle is defined by the CreateWebService function.

The function performs the following steps.

•If no service handle exists, LIMS returns the error:  "No web service handle specified".

•If the service defined by the service handle is not valid, LIMS returns the error: "Invalid web service handle specified:" followed by the service handle value.

•LIMS invokes the specified web service.  If the call is not successful, LIMS returns one of the following errors:

 "Failed to open WinHttp object"

"Invalid response, or no response at all…."

"XML parsing of reply soap failed"
"XML parsing of reply xml failed"
"No objects returned"
"General fault ..."

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

serviceHandle = CreateWebService("WEATHER")

‘(code to create soapMessage)

serviceResponse = InvokeWebService(serviceHandle, soapMessage)

Returns:

The function returns the service response string if successful.  The function returns ERROR if it is not successful.
