The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the templateName, If not found, LIMS will trigger the error "Template does not exist: templateName".

•If an inactiveName is specified, LIMS will retrieve it.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Inactive standard reagent does not exist: inactiveName".  If the standard is found but it is active, LIMS will trigger the error "Standard reagent is active: inactiveName". 

•LIMS checks the templateName against the inactiveName. If the templateName does not match the standard, LIMS will trigger the error "Standard reagent template does not match: inactiveName".  If the template is not specified, LIMS will set the template name to the template associated with the inactiveName standard. If this template does not exist in the database, LIMS will trigger the error "Template does not exist".

•LIMS opens the Standard Reagent Manager window and displays the specified template. If inactiveName is specified the standard is opened.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example opens the standard  named "STD1-22".

status = OpenStandardReagentManager("STD1", "STD1-22")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

OpenStoredQueryManager   

Purpose:

The OpenStoredQueryManager function opens the specified query in the Stored Query Manager window.

Syntax:

status = OpenStoredQueryManager(queryName, title)

queryName – the name of a stored query to be opened when Stored Query Manager is opened

title – the tile to be displayed in Stored Query Manager once opened

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the queryName.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "The specified stored query is not valid: queryName". 

•If the query is found, LIMS will open the Stored Query Manager window and display the query with the title, if specified in the Tile bar..

 Example:

The following example opens the stored query named "LABA_SAMPLESI" with a title.

queryName = "LABA_SAMPLESI"

title = "Incomplete Samples from Lab A"

Status = OpenStoredQueryManager(queryName, title)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

OpenTable    

Purpose:

The OpenTable function opens the specified record in Table Manager.

Syntax:

status = OpenTable(table)

table – the name of the table to be opened

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified table name is not the name of one of the LIMS tables, LIMS will trigger the error "Table does not exist".

•LIMS opens the specified table in Table Manager. 

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example opens the analysis table.

status = OpenTable("ANALYSIS")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

OpenTestEditor    

Purpose:

The OpenTestEditor function opens the Test Editor dialog for the specified sample.

Syntax:

status = OpenTestEditor(sampleNumber)

sampleNumber – the sample number of the sample to be displayed on the Test Editor dialog.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the sample specified.  If the sample is not retrieved, the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: the specified sample does not exist:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS opens the Test Editor dialog with the specified sample displayed.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following code opens the Test Editor dialog with sample 8 displayed.

SampleNumber = 8
status = OpenTestEditor(sampleNumber)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

The system variable: DialogCanceled is set to true if the dialog is closed using the Cancel button; otherwise true.

 

OpenTextEditor    

Purpose:

The OpenTextEditor function prompts for a long text string.

Syntax:

x = OpenTextEditor(title,default,maxSize)

title – the prompt text to be shown (optional)

default – the default value to be displayed (optional)

maxSize – the maximum permitted length of the text string (this argument is required for this function)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS opens a Long Text Prompt Dialog, displays the specified title and default value, and waits for the user to press OK or Cancel.

•If the user presses Cancel, LIMS will return an empty string.

•If the user presses OK, LIMS will return the value entered by the user.

Example:

The following example prompts for additional comments of up to 100 characters.

x = OpenTextEditor("Additional Comments", "", 100)

Returns:

The function returns a string.

 

PlateWindowClose    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowClose function closes the Plate Window.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowClose (windowName)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is open.  If that Plate Window is not open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window is closed".

•LIMS closes the specified Plate Window.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example will close the Plate Window named "PLATE:

windowName = "PLATE"

status = PlateWindowClose(windowName)

Returns: 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

         

PlateWindowOpen    

Purpose:

The PlateWindowOpen function opens the Plate Window and names the window for reference by other Plate Window LIMS Basic functions.

Syntax:

status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, title, clickedSubName)

windowName – the name of the Plate Window (as defined by the PlateWindowOpen function)

title – the text that will be displayed in the dialog Title bar

clickedSubName – the subroutine that is run when an object in the Plate Window is clicked with the mouse.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS verifies that the Plate Window with the specified windowName is not open.  If that Plate Window is open, system triggers and error and the Last Error is set to: "Window already open".

•LIMS displays the Plate Window, sets the title to the specified input, and sets the subroutine triggered by selecting a well to the input value.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example results in the Plate Window shown below

windowName = "PLATE"
status = PlateWindowOpen(windowName, "Test Plate Window", "A_SUB")



Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

RemoveRowFromUserGrid  

Purpose:

The RemoveRowFromUserGrid function removes the specified row from a user grid.

NOTE: If using this function in a LIMS Basic loop care should be taken to remove rows in descending order i.e.. they need to be removed starting with the highest number row then working back in descending order.  If removed in ascending or mixed order, the row references will be confused as the number of  rows are changed.

Syntax:

status = RemoveRowFromUserGrid(row, deleteRecord)

row – row number of row that is to be removed from grid;

deleteRecord -– a Boolean indicating if the corresponding database record is to be deleted.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks for a user grid window.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "There is no active window".

•LIMS checks that the User Grid is currently selected. If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "The active window is not User Grid".

•LIMS removes the specified row.

This function does NOT save changes to the database.

Example:

The following example removes row 2 from the grid but does not delete the database record:

status = RemoveRowFromUserGrid(2, "F")

Returns:

 

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ResetMenuConfig   

Purpose:

The ResetMenuConfig function resets the menu configuration for the specified window and the specified user to the system default by deleting the entry in the MENUS table for the specified window and the specified user.

Syntax:

status = ResetMenuConfig(windowName, userName)

windowName – the name of the window

userName- the name of the user to be reset

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS checks the windowName specified.  If the windowName is null, the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: no window name specified."

•The LIMS checks the userName specified.  If the userName is null, the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: no user name specified.". If the userName is 'SYS-BMP', the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: cannot delete SYS-BMP configuration.".  If the userName is 'SYS-DEF', the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: cannot delete SYS-DEF configuration.".  Otherwise the LIMS deletes the entry in the MENUS table for the specified userName for the specified windowName.

NOTE: This function makes changes to the database.


 Example:

The following examples deletes the MENUS entry for Batch Manager for the user JONES:

windowName = "BatchManager"

userName = "JONES"

status = ResetMenuConfig(windowName, userName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ResetToolbarConfig   

Purpose:

The ResetToolbarConfig function resets the toolbar configuration for the specified window and the specified user to the system default by deleting the entry in the TOOLBAR table for the specified window and the specified user.

Syntax:

status = ResetToolbarConfig(windowName, userName)

windowName – the name of the window

username- the name of the user to be reset

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The LIMS checks the windowName specified.  If the windowName is null, the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: no window name specified."

•The LIMS checks the userName specified.  If the userName is null, the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: no user name specified.". If the userName is 'SYS-BMP', the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: cannot delete SYS-BMP configuration.".  If the userName is 'SYS-DEF', the LIMS returns the last error: "Error: cannot delete SYS-DEF configuration.".  Otherwise the LIMS deletes the entry in the TOOLBAR table for the specified userName for the specified windowName.

NOTE: This function makes changes to the database.


 Example:

The following examples deletes the TOOLBAR entry for Project Manager for the user SMITH:

windowName = "ProjectManager"

userName = "SMITH"

status = ResetToolbarConfig(windowName, userName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

SetMenuConfig

Purpose:

The SetMenuConfig function reconfigures the menus for the specified window using the configuration supplied in the config array. The function will update the corresponding entry in the MENUS table for the currently logged user if the UpdateFlag is True.

Syntax:

status = SetMenuConfig(aWindowName, configArray,UpdateFlag)

aWindowName – (Required) string defining the name of the window.

configArray – (Required) a 1D array where each row corresponds to a single menu item.  The entries should be the exact same format as it appears in Menus table entry, including indentation.

UpdateFlag – (Optional) Boolean that determines if the MENUS record of the user is updated by the function.  If False, the database is not updated. If not specified, the default is True.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The system checks that a user is logged into the system.  If invalid, the system will trigger the error "A user is not logged in ".  If the use name is not valid, the system will trigger the error "Invalid user specified: user".  

•The system checks the aWindowName parameter. If not specified, the system will trigger the error "The Window name was not specified. ".

•The system checks the configArray parameter. If not specified, the system will trigger the error "The menu configuration was not specified.".

•The system will attempt to set the menus. If this fails, the system will trigger the error “Failed to set the menu configuration. ".

NOTE: This function does save changes to the database.


Example:

aWindowName = "PdfWindow"

configArray[1] = "~MENU~ FileMenu"

configArray[2] = "   ~MENUITEM~ fileExit"

configArray[3] = "   ~MENUITEM~ fileOpenReport"

configArray[4] = "   ~SEPARATOR~"

configArray[5] = "   ~MENUITEM~ fileOpenFile"

UpdateFlag = "F"

status = SetMenuConfig(aWindowName, configArray, UpdateFlag)

Returns:

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

SetToolbarConfig

Purpose:

The SetToolbarConfig function reconfigures the toolbar for the specified window using the configuration supplied in the config array.  The function will update the corresponding entry in the TOOLBAR table for the currently logged user if the UpdateFlag is True.

Syntax:

status = SetToolbarConfig(aWindowName, configArray, UpdateFlag)

aWindowName – (Required) string defining the name of the window.

configArray – (Required) A 1D array where each row corresponds to a single toolbar item.  The entries should be the exact same format as it appears in Toolbar table entry.

UpdateFlag – (Optional) Boolean that determines if the TOOLBAR record of the user is updated by the function.  If False, the database is not updated. If not specified, the default is True.

NOTE: Even if the UpdateFlag is false, changes to the toolbar will be saved if the user opens the dialog.  LIMS saves the toolbar configuration on exit.


Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The system checks that a user is logged into the system.  If invalid, the system will trigger the error "A user is not logged in ".  If the use name is not valid, the system will trigger the error "Invalid user specified: user".  

•The system checks the aWindowName parameter. If not specified, the system will trigger the error "The Window name was not specified. ".

•The system checks the configArray parameter. If not specified, the system will trigger the error "The toolbar configuration was not specified.".

•The system will attempt to set the menus. If this fails, the system will trigger the error “Failed to set the toolbar configuration. ".

NOTE: This function can save changes to the database if UpdateFlag is not set to False.


Example:

aWindowName = "PdfWindow"

configArray2[1] = "fileExit"

configArray2[2] = "fileOpenReport"

UpdateFlag = "F"

status = SetToolbarConfig(aWindowName, configArray2, UpdateFlag)

Returns:

The function returns true if successful. If it is not successful, it will answer false and lastError will be set with an appropriate error message.

 

UserDialog  

Purpose:

The UserDialog function opens a User Dialog as a modal dialog.

NOTE: In the web client, the Enter is not always consistent in the way it navigates between fields so it is recommended to use the Tab key in user dialogs.

Syntax:

status = UserDialog(template, "valuesArrayName", fields, defaultValues, lastFieldToOK, oneColumnFlag)

template – the name of the template for the window.

valuesArrayName – array of returned values of template fields.

fields – array of fields to be populated with default values.

defaultValues – array of values for corresponding fields in the fields array.

lastFieldToOK – flag specifying if focus is to be set on the OK button after entering the last field value.

oneColumnFlag –  Boolean, if "T", true; the fields are displayed in a single column, if "F", false, the fields are displayed in multiple columns.

Comments:

The UserDialog LIMS Basic function returns an array: fieldnames that contains a list of field names.

Example:

With a User Template "PARAMS" defined with no Template Table, with fields as shown here:

fig3

status = UserDialog("PARAMS", "valuesArrayName",,,"No")

Returns:

The following dialog is opened:

fig3

On closing the dialog, the values of the fields are returned in the array valuesArrayName.

status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

The system variable: DialogCanceled is set to true if the dialog is closed using the Cancel button; otherwise true.

 

UserGrid  

Purpose:

The UserGrid function opens a User Dialog in a grid view.

Syntax:

status = UserGrid(template, fields, defaultValues, recordIds, modal, recordsArray, maximizeOnOpen,  colWidth, rowHeight, readOnly, keyFieldNames, freezeColumns, freezeRows)

template – the name of the User Dialog template;

fields – array of fields to be populated with default values;

defaultValues – array of values for corresponding fields in the fields array (these default values are used when creating new records);

recordids – array of values of key to open the grid on specific records;

modal = a Boolean indicating if the dialog is to be modal;

recordsArray – a two dimensional array containing values to be populated into the grid dialog.  The column headers are defined by the fields configured on the specified template.  This input can support updating existing record or the creating new records within the user grid;

maximizeOnOpen – a Boolean indicating if the grid is to be maximized when opened.  If T then the grid window is maximized when the dialog is opened and the width and height values on the template are not used.  If the Modal flag is true then the maximizeOnOpen flag is ignored;

colWidth – integer defining the default width of the columns in the dialog(Optional);

rowheight – integer defining the default height of the rows in the dialog (Optional);

readOnly – a Boolean indicating if the window is to be opened in Read Only mode;

keyFieldNames– a 1-D array with the names of the key field names for the Table associated with the User Dialog template, this array is only used if the recordids array is 2 D;

freezeColumns– integer defining the number of columns to the left of the grid scroll freeze  (Optional);

freezeRows – integer defining the number of rows from the top to freeze (Optional);

 

CAUTION: To align with code modification introduced by an earlier module, the maximizeOnOpen parameter was moved from the 6th parameter to the 7th parameter.  If using  this function prior to this release, it may be necessary to change the code by inserting a comma after the recordsArray parameter to ensure that the maximizeOnOpen parameter and the following parameters are properly recognized.

 

NOTE:   The UserGrid function is useful only for templates which have a Template Table defined.  User Grids should not be used for base LIMS child tables because there is no link back to the parent record.

Example:

The following example opens a user grid (not maximized but in Read Only mode) with the User Template "CUST" with two customers as shown here:

Template = "CUST"

recordids[1] = "CM_SELECT"

recordids[2] = "WECOYOTE"

status = UserGrid(template, , , recordIds, "F", recordsArray, "F", 20, 15, "T", , 2, 1)

Returns: 

The following grid view is opened:



status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise. 

File Menu Options:

Open Record: Browses against the Template Table and displays the configured field information for the selected record.

Create New Record:  Creates a new entry on the Template Table.

Scan Records to Open: This function enables the opening of records by scanning barcodes.

Scan Records to Create:  This function enables the insertion of records by scanning barcodes.

Save Record:  Posts field information to the corresponding record in the Template Table.

Delete Record:  Removes the corresponding record from the Template Table.

Exit:  Closes the Grid dialog.

Edit Menu Options:

Cut:  Removes text and stores the information in the desktop buffer.

Copy:  Stores the selected text in the desktop buffer.

Paste:  Inserts buffered text into the selected entry cell(s).

Propagate Down:  Copies information from the selected row to all the blank entries displayed on the dialog.

Insert Row Before- Insert a new row before currently selected row.

Insert Row After- Insert a new row after currently selected row.

Delete Row- Deletes currently selected row.

Set Sorting – Select column for sorting records- records will be sorted using the selected column.

Set Filter- select field and field value for filtering the records.  Only records that match the criteria will be displayed.

 

 

UserWindow   

Purpose:

The UserWindow function opens a User Dialog as a Window.

Syntax:

status = UserWindow(template, fields, defaultValues, recordId, oneColumnFlag, maximizeOnOpen, readOnly, version)

template – the name of the template for the window;

fields – 1 D array of fields to be populated with default values (this array is only used when creating a new record, not if an existing record is opened);

defaultValues – 1 D array of values for corresponding fields in the fields array (these default values are used when creating new records, not when opening an existing record);

recordid – value of key to open the window on a specific record;

oneColumnFlag – Boolean, if "T", true; the fields are displayed in a single column, if "F", false, the fields are displayed in multiple columns;

maximizeOnOpen – a Boolean indicating if the window is to be maximized when opened.  If "T" then the window is maximized when the dialog is opened;

readOnly – a Boolean indicating if the window is to be opened in Read Only mode.

version – the version of the record to be opened, if the table includes versioning (optional).

modal – a Boolean indicating if the window is to be opened as a modal window.  If the modal flag is True, the LIMS Basic code execution will halt until the User Window is closed.

 

CAUTION: To align with code modification introduced by an earlier module, the maximizeOnOpen parameter was moved from the 6th parameter to the 7th parameter.  If updating code using this function that is prior to this release, it may be necessary to change the code by inserting a comma after the oneColumnFlag parameter to ensure that the maximizeOnOpen parameter and readOnly parameter are properly recognized.

Comments:

•The recordid parameter applies only if a Template Table is defined for the User Dialog template.

NOTE: The End key is hard coded to close UserDialogWindows so using the End key will close the User Window.

Example:

The following example opens a user window (not maximized) with the User Template Template "CUST", defaulting the value DE in the ADDRESS4 field:

fields[1] = "ADDRESS4"

defaultValues[1] = "DE"

status = UserWindow("CUST", fields, defaultValues,, "F","F","F",)

Returns:

 

The following window is opened:



status = true if the function was successful, false otherwise.

File Menu Options:

 

Open Record:  Browses against the records for the table defined in the Template Table field of the User Dialog template and displays the existing field information for the selected record.

Create New Record:  Creates a new entry on the Template Table.

Save Record:  Posts updated field information to the corresponding record in the Template Table.

Delete Record:  Removes the corresponding record from the Template Table.

Exit:  Closes the Grid dialog.

 

 

ViewSample    

Purpose:

The ViewSample function opens the Sample Window with the specified sample displayed.

Syntax:

status = ViewSample(sampleNumber)

sampleNumber – the sample number of the sample to be displayed on the Sample Window dialog.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves the sample specified.  If the sample is not retrieved, the LIMS returns the last error: "Sample does not exist:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS opens the Sample Window dialog with the specified sample displayed.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following code opens the Sample Window dialog with sample 8 displayed.

SampleNumber = 8
status = ViewSample(sampleNumber)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

WindowMenuItemNames  

Purpose:

The WindowMenuItemNames function returns arrays listing names of the menu items for the specified menu and window.

Syntax:

status = WindowMenuItemNames(windowClass, menuName, "nlsKeyArrayName", "menuItemArrayName", "menuSelectorArrayName")

windowClass – the name of the window which will be checked

menuName– the name of the menu for the specified window which will be checked

"nlsKeyArrayName" – a string specifiying the name of the array which will contain the NLS keys for the menu item names

 "menuItemArrayName" – a string specifiying the name of the array which will contain the NLSed menu item names

"menuSelectorArrayName" – a string specifiying the name of the array which will match menu item selectors as found in the MENUS table

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the windowClass size.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: a window name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the menuName size.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: a menu name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the windowClass.  If this not valid, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: The specified window is not valid: windowClass". 

•LIMS checks the nlsKeyArrayName.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: an nls array name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the menuItemArrayName size.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: a menu array name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the menuName.  If this not valid, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: Invalid menu name specified: menuName".

•LIMS will output the results for the specified window and menu..

 Example:

The following example outputs the menu items for the File menu in the Restricted Table Manager:

windowClass = "RestrictedTableManager"

menuName = "FileMenu"

nlsarrayName = "nlsKeysArrayName"

ItemsArrayName = "menuItemArrayName"

MenuSelArrayName = "menuSelectorArrayName"

status = WindowMenuItemNames(windowClass, menuName, nlsarrayName , ItemsArrayName , MenuSelArrayName )

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

WindowMenuNames  

Purpose:

The WindowMenuNames function returns an array listing names of the menus for the specified window.

Syntax:

status = WindowMenuNames(windowClass, "nlsKeyArrayName", "menuArrayName")

windowClass – the name of the window which will be checked

 "nlsKeyArrayName" – a string specifying the name of the array which will contain the NLS keys for menu item names

 "menuArrayName" – a string specifying the name of the array which will contain the NLSed menu names

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the windowClass size.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: a window name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the windowClass.  If this not valid, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: The specified window is not valid: windowClass". 

•LIMS checks the nlsKeyArrayName.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: an nls array name must be specified". 

•LIMS checks the menuArrayName size.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: a menu array name must be specified". 

•LIMS will output the results for the specified window and menu..

 Example:

The following example outputs the menus for Batch Manager:

windowClass = "BatchManager"

nlsarrayName = "nlsKeyArrayName"

MenusArrayName = "menuArrayName"

status = WindowMenuNames(windowClass, nlsarrayName , MenusArrayName)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

WindowsSupportingAutomationScripts  

Purpose:

The WindowsSupportingAutomationScripts function outputs a list of windows that support automation scripts.

Syntax:

status = WindowsSupportingAutomationScripts("arrayName")

arrayName – name of a 1-D array that will contain the output from the function

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS checks the arrayName.  If this is null, LIMS will trigger the error "Error: an array name must be specified". 

•If the arrayName is provided, LIMS will output a list of all LIMS windows that support automation scripts..

 Example:

ArrayOut = "windowsArray"

status = WindowsSupportingAutomationScripts(ArrayOut)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

28.3.50  Window Config Functions
 

Function

DatabaseType

DateFormat

DateSeparator

IsTerminalServer

PrinterNames

RegistryValue

 

DataBaseType  

Purpose:

The DataBaseType function returns the database type as reported by ODBC.

Syntax:

dbType = DataBaseType()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If no user is currently logged in, LIMS will trigger the error "LIMS not logger in".

•LIMS calls the SQLGetInfo function of the ODBC interface and passes it the value 17 as argument in order to get the value of the SQL_DBMS_NAME parameter.  LIMS then returns this value.

Example:

The following example returns the database type.

dbType = DataBaseType()

Returns:

The function returns the database type as a string.

 

DateFormat    

Purpose:

The DateFormat function returns the short date format setting that is currently in effect.

Syntax:

formatString = DateFormat()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS returns the short date format setting from the Microsoft Windows operating system environment in which LIMS is currently executing.

Example:

The following example returns the current short date format setting.

formatString = DateFormat()

An example of a date format is "M/d/yyyy".

Returns:

The function returns a string.

 

DateSeparator    

Purpose:

The DateSeparator function returns the date separator that is currently in effect.

Syntax:

separatorString = DateSeparator()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS returns the date separator setting from the Microsoft Windows operating system environment in which LIMS is currently executing.

Example:

The following example returns the current date separator.

separatorString = DateSeparator()

An example of a date separator is "/" (the forward slash character).

Returns:

The function returns a string.

 

IsTerminalServer  

Purpose:

The IsTerminalServer function indicates if the LIMS is operating within a Terminal Server environment or not.

Syntax:

status = IsTerminalServer()

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS determines if the application is currently working in a Terminal Server environment.

•If the system is operating within a Terminal Server environment, the function returns True.

•If the system is not operating with a Terminal Server environment, the function returns False.

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Returns:

The function returns true if a Terminal Server environment and false if it is not.

 

PrinterNames  

Purpose:

The PrinterNames function returns the names of all printers that are defined on the computer on which the current LIMS executable is running.  This list consists of all entries defined in the [devices] section of the WIN.INI file.

Syntax:

status = PrinterNames("arrayOfNames")

arrayOfNames – the name of the array in which LIMS should store the printer names.  Note that this argument is the name of the array and not the array itself.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS asks the operating system for the list of devices and stores this list in a LIMS Basic array with the specified name.

Example:

The following example stores the names of all available printers in an array named "myPrinters".

status = PrinterNames("myPrinters")

Returns:

The function returns the value true.

 

RegistryValue  

Purpose:

The RegistryValue function returns the value of the specified key and setting from the HKEY_CURRENT_USER root in the Microsoft Windows registry.

Syntax:

regValue = RegistryValue(keyName, setting)

keyName – the key name to be searched

setting – the setting whose value is to be returned

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS searches the registry for the specified key.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Invalid registration database key".

•LIMS searches the entries under the specified key for the specified setting.  If this fails, LIMS will trigger the error "No registry entry for the specified setting".

•LIMS returns the retrieved value.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database


Example:

The following example returns the full path of the desktop.

kn = "Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders"
setting = "Desktop"
regValue = RegistryValue(kn, setting)
Msgbox(regValue)

The following example returns the short date format configured on the operating system.

keyName = "Control Panel\International"
setting = "sShortDate"
regValue = RegistryValue(keyName, setting)
msgbox(regValue)

Returns:

The function returns a registry value if it completes successfully.  It returns the value "EMPTY" if an error is triggered.

 

 

28.3.51  XML Functions
 

Function

AsXmlString

ConvertJSONToXML

ExportObjectXML

ExportProjectXML

ExportSampleXML

FromXmlString

GenerateObjectXML

ImportXMLObject

SqlToXML

ToXML

TransformXML

XMLAddTagToMessage

XMLAddTagValueToMessage

XMLCloseTagInMessage

XMLCreateMessage

XMLEscape

XMLMessageContents

XMLParser

XMLQuery

XMLUnescape

 

AsXMLString

Purpose:

Converts a DateTime, Date, or Time to XML.

Syntax:

xmlString = AsXmlString(anObject)   

anObject – a Date, Time, or DateTime value to be converted to XML. 

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The System checks the object type.  If number,  Boolean, or Blank returns the anObject value

•Checks the input value for date/time elements and converts to XML format

•Returns the XML formatted Date/Time

Example:

 

 

aDate = "04/12/2016 06:25:33 PM"

anObject = cvDateTime(aDate)

xmlString = AsXmlString(anObject)   

returns:

xmlString = "2016-04-12T18:25:33.000-04:00"

(where GMT offset is 4 hours)

 

Returns:

 

XML String of the value.  If not recognized as a Date, Time, or Date/Time returns the original value

 

ConvertJSONToXML

Purpose:

Converts the JSON in jsonData to XML, which is stored in the variable specified in xmlData.

Syntax:

status = ConvertJSONToXML(jsonData, "xmlData")

jsonData – (string) - The JSON data to be converted.

xmlData – (string) - The name of the variable that will receive the XML data.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

1.  LabWare checks if jsonData is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument jsonData must be a string”

2.  LabWare checks if xmlData is a string. If not, the following error is returned: “Argument xmlData must be a string”

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

apiTokenValue = GetConstant("API_TOKEN_EX")

'** Initiate session

curlSessionName = "getInfo1"

status = CurlEasyInit(curlSessionName)

 

'** Set Options for URL and Header, and the where to write the data to

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_URL", "http://example.com/api/a1")

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "headers", "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8;")

status = CurlSListAppend(curlSessionName, "headers", ("Authorization: Basic " & Base64Encode("apiToken:" & apiTokenValue)))

status = CurlEasySetOption(curlSessionName, "CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER",,, "headers")

status = CurlEasySetWriteData(curlSessionName, "curlWriteData")

 

'**  Perform and cleanup

status = CurlEasyPerform(curlSessionName)

status = CurlEasyCleanup(curlSessionName)

 

'** Convert the JSON data retrieved by the CurlEasySetWriteData function to XML data

status = ConvertJSONToXML(curlWriteData, "xmlData")

Returns:

Answers true if successful.  Answers false if unsuccessful and sets an error message, “Error processing JSON data” or “Error converting JSON to XML” in lastError.

 

ExportObjectXML    

Purpose:

The ExportObjectXML function exports an object to a disk file in XML format.

Syntax:

status = ExportObjectXML(tableName, keyFieldValue, version, filePathName, keepActive)

tableName – the name of the table that contains the object to be exported.

keyFieldValue – the primary key value of the object to be exported.

version – the version number of the object to be exported, if applicable.

filePathName – the name (and extention), including path, of the file to which the object is to be exported.

NOTE:The filePathName argument must have a .zip file extension.

keepActive – A Boolean indicating if the Active flag should be preserved for the object to be exported, if applicable. If this argument is set to True then if the current object has Active = true the exported object will have the Active flag = true.  Otherwise the Active flag is set to false.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the specified table is not a TableManager table, LIMS will generate the error "Specified table does not exist".

•LIMS retrieves the record for the specified primary key value and version number.  If no version is specified, but the table is versioned, LIMS will use the latest version.  If the retrieval fails, LIMS will trigger the error "Specified record does not exist".

•LIMS exports the object to the specified file.  If no file is specified, LIMS will generate a file name based on the primary key of the object with extension ".xml".  If this fails, LIMS will generate the error "Error exporting xml".

NOTE: This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example exports the latest version of the analysis named "LOD" to the file named " ANALYSIS_LOD.zip" in the current directory.

fileName = "ANALYSIS_LOD.zip"

filePathName = objdirectory & fileName

status = ExportObjectXML("ANALYSIS", "LOD", , filePathName,"T")

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

ExportProjectXML    

Purpose:

The ExportProjectXML function exports a specified project in XML from the LIMS database.

Syntax:

status = ExportProjectXML(projectName, fileName, exportSamplesFlag,action, purpose)

projectName  - the name of the project to export

fileName - the name of the export file

exportSamplesFlag  - flag to indicate if samples are to be exported along with the project. True (T) if you want to export the sample detail and False (F) if not.

Action - (INSERT, UPDATE, COPY)  If importing to another LabWare system

Insert will create new project id, sample number, test numbers and result numbers then import data

Copy will insert all objects with their existing key values

Update will update existing object with values from the xml being imported

purpose - Purpose is simply a comment . It is the reason for the export/import (becomes the audit reason on import)

Comments:

This function is useful when you want to create an interface with an external application. This function exports Project items along with the project.

Example:

projectName = " TEST-0000000001"

fileName = "D:\Documentation\ XML Files\" & projectName &" .XML"

exportSamplesFlag = "T"

action = "INSERT"

purpose = "Demo for the LIMS Basic function ExportProjectXML()"

status = ExportProjectXML(projectName, fileName, exportSamplesFlag, action, purpose)

Returns:

The function returns true when successful, false when unsuccessful.

 

ExportSampleXML    

Purpose:

The ExportSampleXML function exports a specified sample in XML from the LIMS database.

Syntax:

status = ExportSampleXML(sampleNum, fileName, action, purpose, reportableOnlyFlag)

sampleNum  - It is the sample number to export

fileName - FileName is the name and full path of the file to create

action  - (INSERT, UPDATE, COPY)  If importing to another LabWare system

Insert will create new sample number, test numbers and result numbers then import data

Copy will insert all objects with their existing key values

Update will update existing object with values from the xml being imported

purpose  - Purpose is simply a comment. It is the reason for the export/import (becomes the audit reason on import)

reportableOnlyFlag  If true only reportable results are included in the output XML..

Comments:

This function is useful when you want to create an interface with an external application.

Example:

sampleNum = 4

fileName = "D:\Documentation\ XML Files\ " & sampleNum &" .XML"

action = "INSERT"

purpose = "Demo for the LIMS Basic function ExportSampleXML()"

reportableOnlyFlag = "F"

status = ExportSampleXML(sampleNum, fileName, action, purpose, reportableOnlyFlag)

Returns:

The function returns true when successful, false when unsuccessful.

 

FromXMLString

Purpose:

Converts a Date, Time, or Date Time XML format to an Object format

Syntax:

anObject = FromXmlString(anXmlString) 

anXmlString – An XML formatted Date, Time, or DateTime string

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•The System checks the xml sting value.  If the value is a Date/Time, boolean, or number, returns a blank value.

•The system checks the xml string value.  If the value is a string, but not in a Date/Time format, returns the entered value.

•Checks the input value for date/time elements and converts from XML to system date/time format

Example:

 

 

anXmlString = "2002-05-30T09:30:10+04:00"

anObject = FromXmlString(anXmlString) 

returns:

anObject = “05/29/2002 08:30:10 PM”

Returns:

 

Object value of Date/Time.  If string not recognized as a Date, Time, or Date/Time returns the original value. If not a string, returns a blank value.

 

GenerateObjectXML  

Purpose:

This function produces an XML object for the specified record.

Syntax:

string = GenerateObjectXML(tableNames, keyValues, versions, xslRef)

tableNames – Array of table names that correspond to the target records.

keyValues – Array of unique identifier(s) for the target records.

versions – Array of version numbers for the target records.

xslRef – This parameter is the file path and name of the xsl file to be references within the generated xml file.  When the xml file is subsequently opened with a browser application, the browser displays the HTML that results from applying the xsl file to the xml file if the xsl file is available.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS evaluates the version input.  If no version input was provided, the input is conditioned to a blank array with the same size as the tableNames array.

•LIMS generates the xml file with the set of collected records based on the TableNames array, keyValues array, and versions array.  The XML is structured as defined by the references xsl file.

Example:

tableNames[1] = "VISUAL_WORKFLOW"

tableNames[2] = "VISUAL_WORKFLOW"

tableNames[3] = "VISUAL_WORKFLOW"

keyValues[1] = "DEMO_1"

keyValues[2] = "DEMO_2"

keyValues[3] = "DEMO_3"

versions = ""

xslRef = ""

 

string = GenerateObjectXML(tableNames, keyValues, versions, xslRef, myXML)

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the XML output; otherwise, ERROR. 

 

ImportXMLObject    

Purpose:

The ImportXMLObject function pulls a record currently stored in XML format into the LIMS database.

Syntax:

status = ImportXMLObject(tableName, filePathName, overwriteFlag)

tableName – the name of the LIMS table the xml object is to be imported into.

filePathName – a string defining the file name with path.

overwriteFlag – Boolean field defining LIMS behavior if the record already exists in LIMS.  The Boolean defaults to False. 

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•LIMS retrieves specified file.  If the specified file is not found, LIMS returns the error: "Specified file does not exist:" followed by the input value.

•LIMS constructs a record based on the xml file.  If a record could not be constructed, LIMS returns the error: "Object load failed."

•If the record already exists in the database the LIMS performs on of the following:

•If the overwriteFlag is set to True, the system imports the record.  (if the table is version controlled, the system increments the version).

•If the overwriteFlag is set to False, the system does not import the record.  Instead, the LIMS returns the last error: "Record already exists".

NOTE: This function saves changes to the database.


Example:

The following code imports the file: target.xml into the Common Name table overwriting the existing record.

tableName = "COMMON_NAME"
filePathName = "c:\ DEMO\OBJECT_FILES\target.xml"
overwriteFlag = "Y"
status = ImportXMLObject(tableName, filePathName, overwriteFlag)

Returns:

The function returns the status True if the completes successfully.  The function returns a status of False if an error is triggered. 

 

SqlToXML    

Purpose:

The SqlToXML function outputs the results of an SQL statement to a file in xml format

Syntax:

status = SqlToXML(sqlStatement, hierarchyArray, fileName, xsdFlag, gmtOffset)

sqlStatement – the query string defined the field information that will be used to build the XML

hierarchyArray –  the name of the table(s) that the hierarchy will be based on.  (Limited to six entries.)

fileName – the name of the xml file

xsdFlag - a Boolean indicating whether the created XML file will support LabWare XSD . True (T) if you want the output in standard XSD format and False (F) otherwise.

gmtOffset – the GMT Offset to be applied all date fields returned by the sqlStatement.  Specified in minutes.

Comments:

This function is useful when you want to create an interface with an external application.

Example:

sqlStatement = "SELECT sample.sample_number, result.Test_number, result.result_number, result.entry FROM sample, result WHERE sample.sample_number = result.sample_number AND result.sample_number = 1"

hierarchyArray[1,1] = "sample"

hierarchyArray[1,2] = "test"

hierarchyArray[1,3] = "result"

fileName = "D:\Documentation\ XML Files\SQL2XML.xml"

xsdFlag = "T"

gmtOffset = 120

status = SqlToXML(sqlStatement, hierarchyArray, fileName, xsdFlag, gmtOffset)

Returns:

The function returns true when successful, false when unsuccessful.

 

ToXML  

Purpose:

This function collects the objects that are currently in contexts for the specified type and generates an XML file with the data.

Syntax:

xml = ToXML(varName, xslRef)

varName – This parameter defines the type of object that is targeted by this function.  All objects of this type currently in context will be included in the generated XML.

xslRef – This parameter is the file path and name of the xsl file to be references within the generated XML file.  When the XML file is subsequently opened with a browser application, the browser displays the HTML that results from applying the xsl file to the XML file if the xsl file is available.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that the varName parameter is populated.  If not, LIMS return ERROR and populates the LastError with:  "No interpreter variable name provided. "

•LIMS verifies that the specified object types are valid.  If not, LIMS returns ERROR and populates the LastError with: "No interpreter variable found named:" followed by the input

•LIMS generates the XML for all the objects of the specified type currently in the LIMS Basic context.

Example:

This example generates an XML that contains all the samples in the system with the description: CASE_2.

samNum = SELECT SAMPLE.SAMPLE_NUMBER

        WHERE DESCRIPTION = "CASE_2"

        ORDER BY SAMPLE_NUMBER

xml = ToXML("SAMPLE", xslRef)

Returns:

If successful this function returns the XML, otherwise ERROR.

 

 

TransformXML  

Purpose:

This function applies the formatting defined in the xsl file to the target XML file generating the resulting HTML.

Syntax:

html = TransformXML(xmlFilePath, xslFileRef)

xmlFilePath – This is the file path and name of the XML file that is to supply the data to be presented in the generated html.

xslfileRef – This is the file path and name of the xsl file to be applied to the XML file to generate the HTML.

Comments:

When this function is triggered, LIMS performs the following:

•LIMS verifies that the xmlFilePath has been submitted.  If not, LIMS sets the status to false and populates the LastError with:  "No xml file path and name provided ".

•LIMS verifies that the xslRef has been submitted.  If not, LIMS set the status to false and populates the LastError with:  "No xsl file path and name provided ".

•LIMS attempts to generate the HTML.  If the xsl file path and name is invalid, LIMS sets the status to false and populates the last error with: "TransformXML Failed: Call to #transformNode failed".

•If the XML file path and name is invalid, then the generated HTML is not populated with data from the XML file.

Example:

tablename = "VISUAL_WORKFLOW"

keyFieldValue = "MAIN_TEST_VIEW"

versions = empty

xmlFilePath =  "C:\Clients\v7client\xsd\daj.zip"

status = ExportObjectXML(tableName, keyFieldValue, version, xmlFilePath, "F")

xslFileRef = "C:\Clients\v7client\xsd\workflow.xsl"

html = TransformXML(xmlFilePath, xslFileRef)

This example returns the HTML that when presented in a workflow appears as follows:



 

Returns:

If successful, this function returns the HTML, otherwise ERROR. 

 

XMLAddTagToMessage     

Purpose:

The XMLAddTagToMessage function adds a (start) tag to an XML message.

Syntax:

status = XMLAddTagToMessage(handle, tagName, propertiesString)

handle – the handle for the XML message (as defined by the XMLCreateMessage function).

tagName – the name of the tag to be added to the XML message.

propertiesString – properties string ("attribute") to be included with tag (optional).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps:

•If the handle is not valid, LIMS returns the error:
"Invalid handle for XML message specified:" followed by the handle value.

•If no tagName is specified, LIMS returns the error: "Error, no tag name specified"

•LIMS adds the tag (i.e., the start-tag) to the XML message, with properties as specified in the propertiesString.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

handle = XMLCreateMessage()

propertiesString = "xmlns:ns1=" + CHR(34) + "http://ws.cdyne.com/WeatherWS/" + CHR(34)

status = XMLAddTagToMessage(handle, "GetCityWeatherbyZIP", propertiesString)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if successful.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

XMLAddTagValueToMessage     

Purpose:

The XMLAddTagValueToMessage function adds a tag value to an XML message.

Syntax:

status = XMLAddTagValueToMessage(handle, tagName, tagValue, propertiesString) 

handle – the handle for the XML message (as defined by the XMLCreateMessage function).

tagName – the name of the tag to be added to the XML message.

tagValue – the value (element) to be added for the tag.

propertiesString – the properties string to be included with the tag (optional).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the handle is not valid, LIMS returns the error:
"Invalid handle for XML message specified:" followed by the handle value.

•If no tag Name exists, LIMS returns the error: "Error, no tag name specified"

•LIMS adds the tag value (i.e., the start-tag, contents, and end-tag) to the XML message, with properties as specified in the propertiesString.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

handle = XMLCreateMessage()

tagName = "templateName"

tagValue = "Default"

propertiesString = "xsi:type=" + CHR(34) + "xsd:string" + CHR(34)

status = XMLAddTagValueToMessage(handle, tagName, tagValue, propertiesString)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if successful.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

XMLCloseTagInMessage  

Purpose:

The XMLCloseTagInMessage function closes a tag (adds an end tag) in an XML message.

Syntax:

status = XMLCloseTagInMessage(handle) 

handle – the handle for the XML message (as defined by the XMLCreateMessage function).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If the handle is not valid, LIMS returns the error:
"Invalid handle for XML message specified:" followed by the handle value.

•If there is no tag open in the message, LIMS returns the error: "Error, no tag to close"

•LIMS closes the current tag (i.e., adds the end-tag to the XML message for the last tag added).

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

handle = XMLCreateMessage()

status = XMLAddTagToMessage(handle, tagName, propertiesString)

‘(tag values, etc)

status = XMLCloseTagInMessage(handle)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if successful.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

XMLCreateMessage     

Purpose:

The XMLCreateMessage creates (initiates) an XML message

Syntax:

handle = XMLCreateMessage() 

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The function creates a handle for an XML message.

•If unsuccessful, LIMS returns the error: "Error creating XML message".

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

handle = XMLCreateMessage()

Returns:

The function returns the handle of the created XML message if successful.  The function returns ERROR if it is not successful.

 

XMLEscape     

Purpose:

The XMLEscape function escapes an XML string

Syntax:

escapedData = XMLEscape(stringData)

stringData – the XML string to be escaped.

Comments:

The function converts a XML string to escaped format.  The entities < > & ' ", control characters and characters with Ascii value > 126 are escaped.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

stringData = "<tag>this&that</tag>"

escapedData = XMLEscape(stringData)

results in

escapedData = "&lt;tag&gt;this&amp;that&lt;/tag&gt;"

Returns:

The function returns the escaped string

 

XMLMessageContents  

Purpose:

The XMLMessageContents function retrieves the contents of an XML message.

Syntax:

xmlString = XMLMessageContents(handle) 

handle – the handle for the XML message (as defined by the XMLCreateMessage function).

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•The function closes any unclosed tags and extracts the contents of the XML message with the specified handle.

•If unsuccessful, LIMS returns the error: "Invalid handle for XML message specified:", followed by the handle value.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example retrieves the contents of the XML message with handle in variable "handle":

xmlString = XMLMessageContents(handle)

Returns:

The function returns the contents of the XML message if it completes successfully.  The function returns ERROR if it is not successful.

 

 

XMLParse    

Purpose:

The XMLParse function parses an XML message.

Syntax:

status = XMLParse(parserName, xmlMessage)

parserName – the name of the parser that should parse the message

xmlMessage – the XML content to be parsed

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If a parser with the specified name does not yet exist, LIMS will create one.

•LIMS parses the message using the specified parser.  If the parsing is unsuccessful, LIMS will trigger an error with the parser’s message as the error message.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

The following example reads the contents of a file named "c:\mymsg.xml" and parses it.

myxml = FileContents("c:\mymsg.xml")
status = XMLParse("PARSER1", myxml)

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

XMLQuery    

Purpose:

The XMLQuery function queries a parsed XML message.

Syntax:

status = XMLQuery(parserName, queryString, "returnArrayName")

parserName – the name of the parser whose parsed XML should be queried

queryString – the query to be performed

returnArrayName – the name of the array in which the results of the query should be stored.  This argument is a string variable that contains the name of an array.  It does not represent the array itself.

Comments:

The function performs the following steps.

•If a parser with the specified name does not yet exist, or if that parser has not yet successfully parsed an XML message, LIMS will trigger the error "No valid XML text has been specified".

•LIMS queries the parsed content using the specified parser.  If the query is unsuccessful, LIMS will trigger an error with the parser’s message as the error message.

•LIMS stores the results of the query in a two-dimensional array whose name is equal to the value of returnArrayName.  The first dimension identifies the record, and the second dimension identifies the field.

Example:

The following example reads the contents of a file named "c:\mymsg.xml" and queries its content for the ID and name of the customer.  The first name found is displayed in a message box.

myxml = FileContents("c:\mymsg.xml")
status = XMLParse("PARSER1", myxml)
if (status) then
    myquery = "SELECT customer_id, customer_name FROM customer"
    status = XMLQuery("PARSER1", myquery, "myresult")
    if (status) then
        MsgBox(myresult[1,2])
    end if
endif

Returns:

The function returns the value true if it completes successfully.  It returns the value false if an error is triggered.

 

XMLUnescape     

Purpose:

The XMLUnescape function unescapes an XML string

Syntax:

string = XMLUnescape(escapedData)

escapedData – the escaped XML string to be unescaped

Comments:

The function converts an escaped XML string to an unescaped string.

NOTE:  This function does not save changes to the database.


Example:

escapedData = "&lt;tag&gt;this&amp;that&lt;/tag&gt;"

string = XMLUnescape(escapedData)

results in

string = "<tag>this&that</tag>"

Returns:

The function returns the unescaped string
